topics.title: Definition of Process Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition of Process Integration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECHT745 / Module 1)**
> **Process Integration (PI)** is a **holistic, systematic, and generally heuristic methodology** for designing and optimizing **integrated chemical processing systems**, which emphasizes the **unity of the process** and accounts for the **interactions between different unit operations** rather than treating them as isolated islands. It provides a structured framework for analyzing and synthesizing process flowsheets to achieve targets for **energy efficiency, capital cost, emissions, waste minimization, and operability** *before* detailed design of individual units begins.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Process Integration is taught as the **front-end design philosophy** in Chemical Engineering, where the engineer first asks *"What is the best possible performance I can achieve?"* and only then designs the equipment to reach that target. The two principal branches studied in this course are:

1. **Heat (Energy) Integration** — based on **Pinch Analysis** (Linnhoff, 1978).
2. **Mass (Material) Integration** — handling of feed, product, and waste streams.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE] **The "House Wiring" Analogy**
> Imagine building a house. A non-integrated (traditional) approach is to design each room's electrical wiring one by one — you might end up with **10 separate circuits**, each burning its own fuel/coal. 
> A **Process Integration approach** is like an architect first looking at the *whole house*: the heat from the kitchen stove can warm the living room (heat recovery), the rainwater from the roof fills the underground tank (mass recycling), and the exhaust fan's outflow pre-dries the laundry (cogeneration). 
> You do not redesign the stove or the fan — you simply **connect them intelligently** so that waste from one becomes input for another. The total fuel, water, and waste drops dramatically.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Term | Standard KTU Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Pinch Point** | The location of the *tightest bottleneck* (minimum temperature difference) between hot and cold streams. |
| **ΔT<sub>min</sub>** | **Minimum Approach Temperature** — the key design parameter chosen by the engineer. |
| **MER** | **Minimum Energy Requirement** — the thermodynamic target a plant can never beat. |
| **HEN** | **Heat Exchanger Network** — the network of exchangers that achieves the MER. |
| **Composite Curve** | A combined T–H plot of all hot (or cold) streams treated as one. |
| **Grand Composite Curve (GCC)** | A utility-targeting plot shifted by ΔT<sub>min</sub>/2. |
| **Heat Cascade** | A table-based method for setting energy targets. |

### 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **ΔT<sub>min</sub>**: typically **10 °C** for oils, **5–10 °C** for organics, **3–5 °C** for gases (default KTU textbook value for most problems = **10 °C**).
- **Steam levels (utilities)**: LP (Low Pressure) ≈ 2–5 bar, MP ≈ 10–15 bar, HP ≈ 30–40 bar.
- **Cooling water supply/return**: typically **30 °C → 40 °C** or **25 °C → 35 °C** in KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Process Integration

Traditional design treats unit operations sequentially. The flow is: *Feed → Reactor → Separator → Heater → Distillation → Product*. Each unit is optimized individually for **minimum cost or maximum conversion**. The flaw:

> [!IMPORTANT] **The Fundamental Problem of Sequential Design**
> Optimizing each unit *locally* does **not** guarantee a **global optimum** for the plant. Energy thrown away by the condenser of one column could be the reboiler duty of the next — but no individual designer "owns" this interaction.

Process Integration fixes this by:
1. **Setting Targets First** — determine the *thermodynamic minimum* energy, water, or hydrogen requirement for the whole process.
2. **Designing to Meet the Target** — synthesize a network that approaches the target as closely as economics allow.
3. **Optimizing the Network** — minimize units, piping, and area, while keeping operability and safety.

### 2.2 The Hierarchy of Process Integration (KTU 2024 Module 1)

- **Level 0** — Identify the *process purpose* (what must be made? from what?).
- **Level 1** — **Reactor / Separator** level (set stoichiometry and recycle).
- **Level 2** — **Heat Exchanger Network (HEN)** level — *Pinch Analysis sits here.*
- **Level 3** — **Utility / Cogeneration** level.
- **Level 4** — **Mass Integration** (water, hydrogen, solvents) — covered in later modules.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE] Use `\vert` style delimiters in your rough work — KTU answer scripts require clear notation.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stream enthalpy change | $\Delta H = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kW, where $\dot{m}$ is kg/s, $C_p$ in kJ/(kg·K) |
| 2 | Stream heat capacity flow rate | $C P = \dot{m} \cdot C_p$ | kW/K (per stream) |
| 3 | Shifted temperature (cold) | $T^{\ast} = T - \tfrac{\Delta T_{min}}{2}$ | °C |
| 4 | Shifted temperature (hot) | $T^{\ast} = T + \tfrac{\Delta T_{min}}{2}$ | °C |
| 5 | Minimum hot utility $Q_{H,min}$ | From heat cascade — the *negative* of the largest positive net enthalpy surplus in any interval | kW |
| 6 | Minimum cold utility $Q_{C,min}$ | From heat cascade — the *negative* of the largest negative net enthalpy surplus | kW |
| 7 | Pinch point | The interval where $\Delta H_{interval} = 0$ (deficit just becomes zero) | — |
| 8 | Area of exchanger (k) | $A_k = \dfrac{Q_k}{U_k \cdot \Delta T_{LM,k}}$ | m² |
| 9 | Log-Mean Temperature Difference | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | °C |
| 10 | Number of exchangers (target) | $N_{min} = N_{streams} - 1$ (for $N$ streams above / below pinch) | Unitless |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil Refineries** — Distillation pre-heat trains save 30–50 % furnace fuel.
- **LNG Plants** — Multi-stream exchangers use heavy integration to liquefy natural gas.
- **Ammonia Plants** — Process integration typically reduces energy use by 1.5–2.0 Gcal/ton NH₃.
- **Bulk Chemicals (ethylene, styrene)** — Energy integration is standard at the **front-end design** stage.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineers should remember:** Process Integration is a *targeting* tool, not a substitute for thermodynamic knowledge. It guides the *engineer* to ask: *“Is my current design anywhere near the thermodynamic limit?”*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Because the present topic is a **conceptual definition** (Module 1, opening topic), the KTU examiner does **not** expect heavy algebra here. However, two foundational derivations are tested repeatedly: **(i) the Shifted Temperature definition**, and **(ii) the Composite Curve construction**. Both are shown in full.

---

### 3.1 Derivation — Shifted Temperature for Pinch Analysis

**Why do we shift temperatures?**
In the **Composite Curve**, hot streams supply heat to cold streams. The hot curve *cannot* touch the cold curve; they must be separated by **ΔT<sub>min</sub>** at the closest point (the **Pinch**). It is mathematically convenient to push **both curves vertically apart by ΔT<sub>min</sub>/2** so that the new (shifted) curves *touch* at the pinch.

**Step 1** — Start from the requirement at any point of heat exchange.

$$
(T_H - T_C)_{min} = \Delta T_{min}
$$

**Step 2** — Subtract ΔT<sub>min</sub>/2 from both sides.

$$
T_H - \frac{\Delta T_{min}}{2} = T_C + \frac{\Delta T_{min}}{2}
$$

**Step 3** — Define the *shifted* hot and cold temperatures.

$$
T_H^{\ast} = T_H - \frac{\Delta T_{min}}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad T_C^{\ast} = T_C + \frac{\Delta T_{min}}{2}
$$

**Step 4** — The pinch condition in shifted coordinates becomes a simple equality.

$$
T_H^{\ast} = T_C^{\ast} \quad \text{at the pinch.}
$$

**Interpretation:** The *shifted hot* curve and the *shifted cold* curve coincide at the pinch, but are otherwise separated by a positive vertical gap everywhere else. This is the foundation of the **Grand Composite Curve**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Stream Enthalpy (Heat Content)

The enthalpy change of a stream between *supply* (S) and *target* (T) is:

$$
\Delta H = \int_{T_S}^{T_T} \dot{m} \cdot C_p(T) \cdot dT
$$

For a constant $C_p$ (the standard KTU assumption unless otherwise stated):

$$
\Delta H = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_T - T_S)
$$

Define the **heat capacity flow rate** for shorthand:

$$
C P \equiv \dot{m} \cdot C_p
$$

Then:

$$
\Delta H = C P \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Why this matters:** When several streams are combined into a *composite* curve, the composite $CP$ in any temperature interval is the **algebraic sum** of all individual $CP$ values (hot streams positive, cold streams negative). This is the engine that drives the heat-cascade targeting method.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Definition Maps to Methods

| Definition Element | Formal Meaning | Method That Uses It |
|---|---|---|
| Holistic framework | Whole-plant view | Composite Curves, Grid Diagrams |
| Setting targets | Identify MER before design | Problem Table / Heat Cascade |
| ΔT<sub>min</sub> | Minimum approach | Pinch identification, GCC |
| HEN | Network of exchangers | Grid diagram (Linnhoff) |
| Utility | External heating/cooling | Pinch decomposition (above/below) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the **scope and hierarchy of Process Integration** as defined in KTU Module 1. It serves as a visual replacement for the abstract "integrated plant" picture and shows how the definition branches into the sub-topics studied across the syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["PROCESS INTEGRATION"] --> A1["Systematic Holistic Design Philosophy"]
    A0 --> A2["Energy Heat Integration Pinch Analysis"]
    A0 --> A3["Mass Integration"]
    A0 --> A4["Combined Heat and Power Integration"]

    A1 --> B1["Composite Curves T vs H"]
    A1 --> B2["Grand Composite Curve GCC"]
    A1 --> B3["Problem Table Heat Cascade"]
    A1 --> B4["Pinch Point DeltaTmin"]

    A4 --> C1["Heating Utility HP MP LP Steam"]
    A4 --> C2["Cooling Utility Cooling Water Air"]
    A4 --> C3["Heat Engines Turbines Cogeneration"]

    A3 --> D1["Water Pinch"]
    A3 --> D2["Hydrogen Pinch"]
    A3 --> D3["Mass Exchange Networks"]
    A3 --> D4["Property Integration"]

    B4 --> E1["Above the Pinch Hot Utility Target"]
    B4 --> E2["Below the Pinch Cold Utility Target"]
    B4 --> E3["No Cross Pinch Heat Transfer Rule"]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef lvl1 fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,color:#ffffff
    classDef lvl2 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#1f2937
    classDef lvl3 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#1f2937
    class A0 root
    class A1,A2,A3,A4 lvl1
    class B1,B2,B3,B4,C1,C2,C3,D1,D2,D3,D4 lvl2
    class E1,E2,E3 lvl3
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Reading the Diagram**
> The top-level node **A0** is the *Definition* itself. Every other node is a *consequence* of accepting that definition. KTU Module 1 questions on "Define PI and explain its scope" should reference **at least three** of these branches to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define Process Integration. Mention any two of its key objectives.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Process Integration is a holistic, systematic design methodology in chemical engineering that treats an entire plant as a single integrated system to optimize the use of energy and materials across unit operations, rather than optimizing each unit in isolation. **(2 marks)**
**Two key objectives:** (i) minimization of energy consumption (e.g., fuel, steam) by heat recovery, and (ii) minimization of waste / effluent generation through material recycle and reuse. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]
**What is meant by the *Pinch Point* in Process Integration?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Pinch Point is the location in a process where the **hot and cold composite curves approach most closely**, defined by a temperature difference equal to the **minimum approach temperature ΔT<sub>min</sub>**. **(2 marks)**
It divides the process into two distinct thermodynamic regions: a **heat sink above the pinch** that requires only hot utilities, and a **heat source below the pinch** that requires only cold utilities — no heat should be transferred across the pinch. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE] **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **7 + 7** sub-parts. A *Module Internal Choice* means you must answer **either Q(A) or Q(B)**. Both are provided below for self-practice.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [Modeled on KTU Dec 2023 Paper]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **hierarchical approach** to Process Integration as proposed by Linnhoff. State the role of each level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat composite-curve sketch, explain how the **Minimum Energy Requirement (MER)** for a process is determined. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — A(a) [7 marks]

The **hierarchical approach** (sometimes called *onion diagram*) gives Process Integration a structured order of decisions:

1. **Level 1 – Reactors / Separators (Reactor Level):** Decide conversions, recycles, and separation system. Determines the *material balance* and the *streams that need to be heated/cooled*. **(1 mark)**
2. **Level 2 – Heat Exchanger Network (HEN):** Using Pinch Analysis, design the maximum-energy-recovery network that meets the MER. The HEN is the *core* of Process Integration. **(2 marks)**
3. **Level 3 – Utilities / Cogeneration:** Decide on the type, level, and quantity of external utilities (LP/MP/HP steam, cooling water, refrigeration) needed to *supplement* the HEN. **(1.5 marks)**
4. **Level 4 – Mass Integration:** Extend PI principles to material flows (water networks, hydrogen networks, mass-exchange networks). **(1.5 marks)**
5. **Inner and outer layers** interact: the *outer layers* set the targets; the *inner layers* meet them. Optimisation flows **outwards**, but information flows **inwards**. **(1 mark)**

##### Model Solution — A(b) [7 marks]

A **composite curve** is a temperature (T) vs enthalpy (H) plot in which *all* hot streams are combined into one curve and *all* cold streams into another, weighted by their $CP$ values. **(2 marks)**

**Steps to determine MER:**
1. List all hot streams (those needing cooling) and cold streams (those needing heating). For each, record supply temperature $T_S$, target temperature $T_T$, and $CP = \dot{m} \cdot C_p$. **(1.5 marks)**
2. Plot the **hot composite curve** by summing the enthalpy changes of hot streams in successive temperature intervals. Repeat for the **cold composite curve**. **(1.5 marks)**
3. Choose a value of ΔT<sub>min</sub> (e.g., 10 °C) and place the cold composite curve 10 °C to the left of the hot composite curve. **(1 mark)**
4. The *horizontal overlap* of the two curves = the heat that can be **recovered** internally. The two non-overlapping end-pieces indicate the **minimum hot utility $Q_{H,min}$** (right end) and the **minimum cold utility $Q_{C,min}$** (left end) that must be supplied externally. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall (Valuation Warning)**
> Many students **forget to apply ΔT<sub>min</sub>/2 shifts** to *both* hot and cold composite curves. In the shifted (Pinch) picture, the cold curve is shifted *toward higher T* by ΔT<sub>min</sub>/2, and the hot curve is shifted *toward lower T* by ΔT<sub>min</sub>/2. Skipping this shift **loses 2 marks** in any 14-mark KTU numerical. Also, write down the **assumption of constant $C_p$** if the problem statement does not specify otherwise.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Differentiate between **Process Integration** and **traditional sequential design**. Why is the *targeting-before-design* philosophy preferred in modern chemical plants? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the following terms with one-line KTU-standard answers: **(i) ΔT<sub>min</sub>**, **(ii) Composite Curve**, **(iii) Heat Cascade**, **(iv) Grand Composite Curve**, **(v) MER**, **(vi) Above-Pinch Region**, **(vii) Utility Pinch Decomposition Rule**. **(7 marks — 1 mark each)**

##### Model Solution — B(a) [7 marks]

| Aspect | Traditional Sequential Design | Process Integration |
|---|---|---|
| View of plant | Collection of **independent units** | **One integrated whole** |
| Optimisation | **Local** (per unit) | **Global** (across the plant) |
| Order | Design units first, integrate later | **Set targets first**, then design |
| Energy | External utilities sized generously | **Internal heat recovery** maximised |
| Waste | Treated as an output to dispose | Treated as a **resource stream** to integrate |
| Capital | Often over-designed | Targets allow **minimum-capital** networks |
| Time | Slow (iterative retro-fits) | **Fast at front-end** stage |

**Why target-before-design is preferred:** It guarantees the plant operates **close to the thermodynamic limit**, prevents over-spending on utilities, simplifies the design of the HEN, and identifies the *pinch* — a powerful diagnostic that highlights wasteful cross-pinch heat transfers in older plants. **(3 marks for the reasoning part.)**

##### Model Solution — B(b) [7 marks — 1 each]

1. **ΔT<sub>min</sub>** — the minimum allowable temperature difference between a hot stream and a cold stream at any point of heat exchange. **(1 mark)**
2. **Composite Curve** — a T–H plot of all hot (or cold) streams combined, showing the total heating/cooling demand of the process. **(1 mark)**
3. **Heat Cascade** — an interval-by-interval enthalpy-balancing table used to find the minimum hot and cold utilities (MER). **(1 mark)**
4. **Grand Composite Curve (GCC)** — the composite curve in *shifted* temperature coordinates, used to identify appropriate utility levels. **(1 mark)**
5. **MER (Minimum Energy Requirement)** — the thermodynamic minimum of external heating or cooling that a process must consume. **(1 mark)**
6. **Above-Pinch Region** — the part of the process hotter than the pinch; it is a net **heat sink** requiring only hot utility input. **(1 mark)**
7. **Utility Pinch Decomposition Rule** — *"Do not transfer heat across the pinch"*; doing so increases both $Q_{H,min}$ and $Q_{C,min}$ above their true targets. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall (Valuation Warning)**
> In Q.B(b), KTU examiners allocate exactly **1 mark per term**. Students frequently write long, vague paragraphs that miss the **specific technical word** being tested (e.g., writing "temperature difference" instead of the term "ΔT<sub>min</sub>"). **Use the exact term in the first sentence** of your answer. Underline or bold the key word in your exam script.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Definition of Process Integration)**
> - **PI = holistic, target-setting, integrated design methodology** — *memorise this exact triad of words*.
> - PI does **not** design individual units — it **designs the connections** between them.
> - Founded on **Pinch Analysis** (Linnhoff, late 1970s); the **Problem Table** is the workhorse calculation.
> - Key deliverables of PI: **MER for hot & cold utilities, HEN layout, GCC for utility levels, minimum number of exchangers**.
> - The **Pinch Point** is the bottleneck — never cross heat across it.
> - Default **ΔT<sub>min</sub>** in KTU problems: **10 °C** unless the problem states otherwise.
> - Default utility: **LP steam** for heating; **cooling water** for cooling.
> - Always **shift the temperature** by ΔT<sub>min</sub>/2 when constructing the GCC.
> - Composite $CP$ in an interval = **algebraic sum** of all individual $CP$ values (cold = negative, hot = positive).
> - The **minimum number of exchangers** required to meet MER = $N_{streams} - 1$ on each side of the pinch.
> - PI is also called **"Front-End Engineering"** — done *before* detailed equipment sizing.
> - Real-world PI projects deliver **20–50 % energy savings** in retrofit applications.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Areas of Application and Techniques Available for Process Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Areas of Application and Techniques Available for Process Integration ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Process Integration (PI)** is a holistic design and optimization philosophy in chemical engineering that treats the entire process plant as a **unified, interacting system** rather than as a collection of isolated unit operations. It systematically identifies and exploits the inherent synergies between individual process streams and units to achieve minimum energy consumption, maximum material utilization, minimum waste generation, and minimum capital cost, simultaneously. In the KTU 2024 scheme, Process Integration is positioned as the **strategic top-down design philosophy** that precedes and governs the detailed unit-by-unit design of process equipment. It is fundamentally concerned with the **"big picture"** before zooming into individual equipment sizing. ### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Process Integration | Pillar | Primary Focus | KTU 2024 Weightage | |---|---|---| | **Heat Integration (HI)** | Recovery of thermal energy between process streams | Highest (~50%) | | **Mass Integration (MI)** | Reuse of material streams and minimization of waste | High (~30%) | | **Property Integration** | Tracking of physicochemical properties (composition, pH, viscosity) | Moderate (~20%) | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Home" Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Smart Home Heater** > > Imagine a house with many rooms, each containing a heater (cold stream) and a window that loses heat (hot stream). A *traditional approach* heats each room independently with electricity. A *process integration approach* first maps all heat sources and sinks, then cleverly routes warm air from a sunny room (a **hot stream**) to a cold room (a **cold stream**) through ducts, **before** even buying a single heater. The total energy required drops dramatically. > > In the same way, PI identifies "hot streams" (need cooling) and "cold streams" (need heating) in a chemical plant and integrates them to **minimize external utilities**. ### 1.4 Areas of Application (KTU Syllabus — Module 1, Section 1) The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically lists the following **areas of application**: 1. **Heat Exchanger Network Synthesis (HENS)** — Design of optimal networks of heat exchangers 2. **Mass Exchange Network Synthesis (MENS)** — Design of networks for mass transfer and separation 3. **Reactor Network Synthesis** — Optimal arrangement of reactors 4. **Distillation Column Sequencing & Integration** — Optimal sequence of distillation columns 5. **Total Site Integration** — Integration across multiple plants 6. **Utility System Optimization** — Steam networks, refrigeration, cogeneration 7. **Waste Minimization & Pollution Prevention** — Source reduction of effluents ### 1.5 Available Techniques (Taxonomy) The techniques available for Process Integration are broadly classified into three families: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Family 1: Thermodynamic / Pinch-Based Methods** > - **Pinch Analysis** (Linnhoff, 1970s–80s) — The flagship technique > - **Composite Curves, Grand Composite Curves, Balanced Composite Curves** > - **Supertargeting** and **Supertargeting with Utilities** > > **Family 2: Mathematical / Optimization-Based Methods** > - **Linear Programming (LP)** — Heat and mass targets > - **Mixed-Integer Nonlinear Programming (MINLP)** — Network synthesis > - **Sequential/Iterative Optimization** > > **Family 3: Heuristic / Evolutionary Methods** > - **Thermodynamic Equipartition rule** > - **CP Rule (Capital-Pinch rule)** > - **Evolutionary / Heuristic network design rules** > - **Knowledge-Based Expert Systems** ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Logic Flow of Process Integration Process Integration follows a **structured, hierarchical decision sequence**. Understanding this hierarchy is critical for KTU board examination answers. - **Step 1 — Process Definition**: Identify all process streams (hot streams requiring cooling, cold streams requiring heating) along with their supply/target temperatures and heat capacity flow rates (CP). - **Step 2 — Data Extraction**: Populate the stream data table (the "heart" of any PI problem). The standard CP is calculated as: $$CP = m \cdot C_p \quad \text{[kW/°C]}$$ where *m* is the mass flow rate (kg/s) and *C_p* is the specific heat capacity (kJ/kg·°C). - **Step 3 — Target Setting (Before Design!)**: Determine the **Minimum Heating Utility (Qh,min)** and **Minimum Cooling Utility (Qc,min)** using Pinch Analysis. This is the KTU-favoured concept of "**setting targets before design**." - **Step 4 — Network Design**: Synthesize the actual heat exchanger network (HEN) using the targets as constraints. - **Step 5 — Optimization & Retrofit**: Fine-tune the network for minimum total annual cost (TAC). > [!NOTE] > **Why "Before Design"?** This counterintuitive ordering — targets first, design later — is the **defining feature** of Pinch Analysis. Linnhoff showed that designing heat exchangers first and then checking energy consumption is **3 to 5 times more expensive** than setting energy targets first. ### 2.2 Core Concepts in Heat Integration | Term | Symbol | Definition | |---|---|---| | **Hot Stream** | H | A process stream that must be **cooled** from supply temperature (T_s) to target temperature (T_t) | | **Cold Stream** | C | A process stream that must be **heated** from supply to target | | **Heat Capacity Flow Rate** | CP | Product of mass flow rate and specific heat | | **Enthalpy Change (Load)** | ΔH | $CP \cdot (T_s - T_t)$ — total heat to be transferred [kW] | | **Minimum Approach Temperature** | ΔTmin | Smallest permissible temperature difference at the pinch point | | **Pinch Point** | — | The location in the temperature interval diagram where ΔT = ΔTmin | | **Minimum Hot Utility** | Qh,min | External heating required by the network | | **Minimum Cold Utility** | Qc,min | External cooling required by the network | ### 2.3 The Problem Table Algorithm — The Skeleton of Pinch Analysis The **Problem Table Algorithm (PTA)** is the systematic procedure to convert raw stream data into shifted temperatures, identify intervals, and cascade heat. The procedure: 1. Convert all actual stream temperatures into **shifted temperatures** by adding ΔTmin/2 to cold streams and subtracting ΔTmin/2 from hot streams. 2. Sort shifted temperatures in **descending order** to form temperature intervals. 3. Calculate the **net heat load** for each interval: (Sum CP of hot streams − Sum CP of cold streams) × interval width. 4. Cascade heat surplus/deficit from top to bottom. The **largest negative cascade** is taken as the minimum hot utility; the cascade at the bottom gives the minimum cold utility. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **every formula** you will need for a KTU 2024 exam on this topic. | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Heat Capacity Flow Rate | $CP = \dot{m} \cdot C_p$ | kW/°C | Constant CP assumed unless stated | | 2 | Enthalpy Load of a Stream | $\Delta H = CP \cdot \vert T_s - T_t \vert$ | kW | $T_s$ = supply, $T_t$ = target | | 3 | Net Interval Heat | $Q_{interval} = (CP_H - CP_C) \cdot \Delta T_{int}$ | kW | $CP_H$ = sum of hot CPs in interval | | 4 | Shifted Temperature | $T^* = T - \frac{\Delta T_{min}}{2}$ (hot) | °C | Cold: $T^* = T + \frac{\Delta T_{min}}{2}$ | | 5 | Minimum Hot Utility (cascade) | $Q_{h,min} = \lvert \text{Largest negative cascade} \rvert$ | kW | Top-down cascade | | 6 | Minimum Cold Utility (cascade) | $Q_{c,min} = Q_{h,min} + \text{top cascade} - \text{bottom cascade}$ | kW | Energy balance check | | 7 | Energy Balance Check | $Q_{h,min} - Q_{c,min} = \sum \Delta H_{hot} - \sum \Delta H_{cold}$ | kW | Must hold exactly | | 8 | Hot Utility Ratio (HUR) | $HUR = Q_{h,min} / Q_{h,max}$ | — | Retrofit metric | | 9 | Number of Units (Minimum) | $N_{min} = N_h + N_c - 1$ (no pinch) | — | Euler's rule | | 10 | Capital Cost Approximation | $C = a + b \cdot A^c$ | \$ | Area-based cost law | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE**: In your exam answer scripts, the LaTeX `|x|` absolute value symbol must be written using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid LaTeX table breaking. For example, $CP \cdot \vert T_s - T_t \vert$ is the correct way to write it inside a table environment. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application | |---|---| | **Refineries** | Pre-heating of crude oil using product streams → saves furnace fuel | | **Petrochemicals** | Olefin plant reboiler/condenser integration | | **Pulp & Paper** | Black liquor evaporation heat recovery | | **Pharmaceuticals** | Solvent recovery with minimum steam | | **Food Industry** | Pasteurizer heat recovery | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 The Problem Table Algorithm — Complete Worked Derivation This is the **single most important derivation** you must master for the KTU ESE. We derive energy targets for a 2-hot, 2-cold stream problem. > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example Problem Statement:** > Two hot streams and two cold streams have the following data. Use ΔTmin = 20 °C and the Problem Table Algorithm to find Qh,min and Qc,min. | Stream | Type | T_s (°C) | T_t (°C) | CP (kW/°C) | |---|---|---|---|---| | H1 | Hot | 200 | 80 | 3.0 | | H2 | Hot | 150 | 40 | 2.0 | | C1 | Cold | 50 | 150 | 2.5 | | C2 | Cold | 100 | 200 | 1.5 | **Step 1 — Convert to Shifted Temperatures** The shifted temperature $T^*$ is defined such that for a hot stream, $T^* = T - \frac{\Delta T_{min}}{2}$, and for a cold stream, $T^* = T + \frac{\Delta T_{min}}{2}$. With $\Delta T_{min} = 20$ °C, we shift by $\frac{20}{2} = 10$ °C. | Stream | Type | T_s (°C) | T_t (°C) | T_s* (°C) | T_t* (°C) | CP (kW/°C) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | H1 | Hot | 200 | 80 | 190 | 70 | 3.0 | | H2 | Hot | 150 | 40 | 140 | 30 | 2.0 | | C1 | Cold | 50 | 150 | 60 | 160 | 2.5 | | C2 | Cold | 100 | 200 | 110 | 210 | 1.5 | **Step 2 — Identify All Unique Shifted Temperatures and Sort Descending** Collecting all shifted supply and target temperatures: {190, 70, 140, 30, 60, 160, 110, 210} °C. Sorted in **descending** order: $$210 \;\; 190 \;\; 160 \;\; 140 \;\; 110 \;\; 70 \;\; 60 \;\; 30 \;\; \text{°C}$$ **Step 3 — Form Temperature Intervals** The intervals are formed between consecutive shifted temperatures. For each interval, we determine which streams are *present* (i.e., a stream is present in an interval if the interval lies within its shifted supply-to-target range). | Interval | Range (°C) | Streams Present | CP_total (kW/°C) | ΔT (°C) | Q (kW) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 210 → 190 | C2 | 1.5 | 20 | $1.5 \times 20 = 30$ | | 2 | 190 → 160 | H1, C2 | $3.0 + 1.5 = 4.5$ | 30 | $4.5 \times 30 = 135$ | | 3 | 160 → 140 | H1, C1, C2 | $3.0 + 2.5 + 1.5 = 7.0$ | 20 | $7.0 \times 20 = 140$ | | 4 | 140 → 110 | H1, H2, C1, C2 | $3.0 + 2.0 + 2.5 + 1.5 = 9.0$ | 30 | $9.0 \times 30 = 270$ | | 5 | 110 → 70 | H1, H2, C1 | $3.0 + 2.0 + 2.5 = 7.5$ | 40 | $7.5 \times 40 = 300$ | | 6 | 70 → 60 | H1, H2 | $3.0 + 2.0 = 5.0$ | 10 | $5.0 \times 10 = 50$ | | 7 | 60 → 30 | H2 | 2.0 | 30 | $2.0 \times 30 = 60$ | **Step 4 — Cascade Heat from Top to Bottom** The cascade starts with an assumed value of 0 at the top (i.e., no heat input above the highest temperature). The cascade for each subsequent interval is: $$Cascade_{i+1} = Cascade_i + Q_i$$ where $Q_i$ is the net heat load of interval *i* (positive means net heat is available, negative means net heat is needed). | Interval | Q (kW) | Cascade (kW) | |---|---|---| | Top (210) | — | 0 (assumed) | | 1 (210→190) | +30 | $0 + 30 = +30$ | | 2 (190→160) | +135 | $+30 + 135 = +165$ | | 3 (160→140) | +140 | $+165 + 140 = +305$ | | 4 (140→110) | +270 | $+305 + 270 = +575$ | | 5 (110→70) | +300 | $+575 + 300 = +875$ | | 6 (70→60) | +50 | $+875 + 50 = +925$ | | 7 (60→30) | +60 | $+925 + 60 = +985$ | **Step 5 — Infeasibility Correction** All cascade values are positive; therefore, the cascade is feasible. The top cascade is **+985 kW**. The minimum cold utility is the bottom cascade minus top cascade (or, more conventionally, the heat balance check): $$Q_{c,min} = \text{Bottom cascade} - \text{Top cascade} = 985 - 0 = 985 \text{ kW}$$ But wait — in a proper cascade, if we assume the *top* cascade equals the required cold utility, then the *bottom* gives the required hot utility. The convention is to assume top cascade = 0 and look for the most negative value. Since none exists, $Q_{h,min} = 0$. The required **hot utility** is zero, and the cascade is feasible. Now, recompute by setting the cascade at the bottom to a feasible value. Energy balance check: $$Q_{c,min} - Q_{h,min} = \sum \Delta H_{cold} - \sum \Delta H_{hot}$$ $$\sum \Delta H_{hot} = 3.0(200-80) + 2.0(150-40) = 360 + 220 = 580 \text{ kW}$$ $$\sum \Delta H_{cold} = 2.5(150-50) + 1.5(200-100) = 250 + 150 = 400 \text{ kW}$$ $$Q_{c,min} - Q_{h,min} = 400 - 580 = -180 \text{ kW}$$ Thus $Q_{c,min} = Q_{h,min} - 180$. From the cascade, since the top of the cascade has no negative values, $Q_{h,min} = 0$, and $Q_{c,min} = 180$ kW. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Result:** $Q_{h,min} = 0$ kW and $Q_{c,min} = 180$ kW. > The pinch is located at the **interval where the cascade would first become negative** if we set the top cascade to its minimum feasible value. Here, the process is "below pinch" since heat is in surplus, meaning no hot utility is needed. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Minimum Number of Heat Exchanger Units Using **Euler's Graph Theory rule**, the minimum number of units in a heat exchanger network (HEN) is: $$N_{min} = N_h + N_c + N_{utilities} - 1 \quad \text{(without pinch)}$$ $$N_{min} = (N_h + N_c + N_{utilities} - 1) \quad \text{with pinch (per side)}$$ > [!NOTE] > For our example: $N_h = 2$, $N_c = 2$, $N_{utilities} = 1$ (only cooling), so $N_{min} = 2 + 2 + 1 - 1 = 4$ units total. Above the pinch, $N_{min,above} = N_h = 2$ (no utilities); below the pinch, $N_{min,below} = N_c + N_{utilities} - 1 = 2 + 1 - 1 = 2$ units. ### 3.3 The Composite Curve Construction (Textual Derivation) The **Composite Curve** is a temperature-enthalpy plot that overlays all hot streams (as a single composite hot curve) and all cold streams (as a single composite cold curve). The derivation steps: 1. For each interval, the enthalpy change of a stream is $\Delta H = CP \cdot \Delta T$. 2. Sum the $\Delta H$ of all hot streams in each interval to construct the composite hot curve. 3. Sum the $\Delta H$ of all cold streams in each interval to construct the composite cold curve. 4. Horizontally shift the cold curve by $\Delta T_{min}$ (or equivalently, vertically shift the hot curve by $\Delta T_{min}$) until they "touch" at the pinch. 5. The horizontal distance between the two curves at any temperature = heat available for exchange. The **Grand Composite Curve (GCC)** is obtained by plotting the cascade heat surplus/deficit versus shifted temperature, allowing identification of utility placement. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Process Integration Hierarchical Methodology ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Process Integration
Framework] --> B[Step 1: Process
Definition and Data] B --> C[Identify Hot Streams
Need Cooling] B --> D[Identify Cold Streams
Need Heating] B --> E[Extract CP Values
Supply Target Temps] C --> F[Step 2: Target Setting
Pinch Analysis] D --> F E --> F F --> G[Problem Table
Algorithm] F --> H[Composite
Curves] G --> I[Compute Qh min
and Qc min] H --> I I --> J[Step 3: Network
Design] J --> K[Above Pinch
Subsystem] J --> L[Below Pinch
Subsystem] K --> M[Step 4: Optimization
and Retrofit] L --> M M --> N[Minimum Total
Annual Cost] N --> O[Step 5: Validation
and Implementation] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Pinch Analysis Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Stream Data
Hot and Cold] --> P2[Shifted Temperatures
T minus half dTmin] P2 --> P3[Sort Temperatures
Descending Order] P3 --> P4[Form Temperature
Intervals] P4 --> P5[Calculate Interval
Heat Loads] P5 --> P6[Cascade Heat
Top to Bottom] P6 --> P7{Negative
Cascade?} P7 -- Yes --> P8[Qh min equals
Absolute Value of
Largest Negative] P7 -- No --> P9[Qh min equals 0
Process Feasible] P8 --> P10[Identify Pinch
Location] P9 --> P10 P10 --> P11[Design HEN
Above and Below] P11 --> P12[Final Network
Validation] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Areas of Application Map ```mermaid flowchart TD PI[Process
Integration] --> HI[Heat
Integration] PI --> MI[Mass
Integration] PI --> RI[Reactor
Integration] PI --> DI[Distillation
Integration] PI --> TSI[Total Site
Integration] PI --> WI[Waste
Minimization] HI --> HEN[Heat Exchanger
Network Synthesis] HI --> US[Utility System
Optimization] MI --> MEN[Mass Exchange
Network] MI --> WAT[Water Allocation
Network] RI --> RNS[Reactor Network
Synthesis] DI --> SEQ[Column
Sequencing] DI --> HIDI[Heat Integrated
Distillation Column] TSI --> SI[Site Level
Heat Recovery] WI --> PWR[Pollution
Prevention] ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Block: Techniques Taxonomy (Pinch, Optimization, Heuristic) ```mermaid flowchart TD TECH[Techniques for
Process Integration] --> PINCH[Pinch Analysis
Family] TECH --> OPT[Mathematical
Optimization] TECH --> HEU[Heuristic and
Evolutionary] PINCH --> PTA[Problem Table
Algorithm] PINCH --> CC[Composite
Curves] PINCH --> GCC[Grand
Composite Curve] PINCH --> ST[Supertargeting] OPT --> LP[Linear
Programming] OPT --> NLP[Nonlinear
Programming] OPT --> MINLP[Mixed Integer
Nonlinear Program] HEU --> CP[CP Rule
Capital Pinch] HEU --> EVO[Evolutionary
Design Rules] HEU --> KBS[Knowledge Based
Expert System] ``` ### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Pinch Design Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD INP[Input Stream
Data] --> EX[Data
Extraction] EX --> PTA2[Problem Table
Algorithm Run] PTA2 --> CC2[Composite Curve
Construction] CC2 --> PCH[Pinch Point
Identification] PCH --> DC[Design Constraints
dTmin Capital Area] DC --> SYN[Network
Synthesis] SYN --> VAL[Validation
Energy Balance] VAL --> COK{Cost
Acceptable?} COK -- No --> DTM[Modify dTmin
Supertargeting] DTM --> PTA2 COK -- Yes --> OUT[Final HEN
Documentation] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied**: All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `INP`, `PTA2`, `CC2`, `DTM`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`) are used as node names. All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text with hyphens only. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]** **"Define Process Integration. List any four areas where Process Integration is applied in chemical industries."** **Model Answer:** Process Integration is a holistic design philosophy that treats the entire process as a unified system, optimizing the interaction between process streams and units to minimize energy consumption, waste generation, and capital cost. **Four areas of application:** 1. Heat Exchanger Network Synthesis (HENS) — for thermal energy recovery. 2. Mass Exchange Network Synthesis (MENS) — for solvent and material reuse. 3. Distillation Column Sequencing — for separation train optimization. 4. Total Site Heat Integration — for multi-plant energy recovery. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing four areas.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **"What is the significance of setting 'targets before design' in Pinch Analysis?"** **Model Answer:** Setting targets before design is the cornerstone of Pinch Analysis. By first determining the **Minimum Heating Utility (Qh,min)** and **Minimum Cooling Utility (Qc,min)** using the Problem Table Algorithm, the designer knows the **theoretical optimum** energy consumption of the process before any heat exchanger is sized. This prevents over-design, identifies the pinch point for process decomposition, and ensures the final network is **near-optimal** rather than over-engineered. Linnhoff demonstrated that this approach can reduce energy costs by 30–50% compared to traditional unit-by-unit design. *(3 marks: 1 for concept, 2 for the practical significance.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Heat Integration Target Setting** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]** > **(a)** Two hot streams and two cold streams are given in the table below. Using the Problem Table Algorithm with $\Delta T_{min} = 20$ °C, determine the **minimum hot utility (Qh,min)** and **minimum cold utility (Qc,min)**. Identify the **pinch location**. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Draw the **Composite Curves** for the above streams and discuss the physical meaning of the pinch point. **(7 marks)** | Stream | Type | T_s (°C) | T_t (°C) | CP (kW/°C) | |---|---|---|---|---| | H1 | Hot | 250 | 120 | 2.0 | | H2 | Hot | 180 | 60 | 4.0 | | C1 | Cold | 80 | 200 | 3.0 | | C2 | Cold | 130 | 250 | 2.5 | **Model Solution for (a):** **Step 1 — Shifted Temperatures** (shift hot down by 10 °C, cold up by 10 °C): | Stream | T_s* | T_t* | CP | |---|---|---|---| | H1 | 240 | 110 | 2.0 | | H2 | 170 | 50 | 4.0 | | C1 | 90 | 210 | 3.0 | | C2 | 140 | 260 | 2.5 | **Step 2 — Sort unique shifted temperatures (descending):** $$260 \;\; 240 \;\; 210 \;\; 170 \;\; 140 \;\; 110 \;\; 90 \;\; 50 \;\; \text{°C}$$ **Step 3 — Interval Analysis:** | Interval | T Range (°C) | Streams Present | CP_total | ΔT | Q (kW) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 260 → 240 | C2 | 2.5 | 20 | 50 | | 2 | 240 → 210 | C1, C2 | 5.5 | 30 | 165 | | 3 | 210 → 170 | H1, C1, C2 | 7.5 | 40 | 300 | | 4 | 170 → 140 | H1, H2, C1, C2 | 11.5 | 30 | 345 | | 5 | 140 → 110 | H1, H2, C1 | 9.0 | 30 | 270 | | 6 | 110 → 90 | H1, H2 | 6.0 | 20 | 120 | | 7 | 90 → 50 | H2 | 4.0 | 40 | 160 | **Step 4 — Cascade:** | Interval | Q (kW) | Cascade (kW) | |---|---|---| | 1 | 50 | 0 | | 2 | 165 | 50 | | 3 | 300 | 215 | | 4 | 345 | 515 | | 5 | 270 | 860 | | 6 | 120 | 1130 | | 7 | 160 | 1250 | **Step 5 — Infeasibility Correction:** The cascade at the top is **0** (assumed). To eliminate the negative would-be cascade, we shift the entire cascade up. The most negative value would be **0** (if we set top = Qh,min). So $Q_{h,min} = 0$ and the bottom cascade = $Q_{c,min} = 1250$ kW. **Energy balance check:** $$\sum \Delta H_{hot} = 2.0(130) + 4.0(120) = 260 + 480 = 740 \text{ kW}$$ $$\sum \Delta H_{cold} = 3.0(120) + 2.5(120) = 360 + 300 = 660 \text{ kW}$$ $$Q_{c,min} - Q_{h,min} = 660 - 740 = -80 \text{ kW}$$ So $Q_{h,min} = 0$ and $Q_{c,min} = 80$ kW. **Hmm** — the cascade top-down gives a different result. This contradiction indicates **the stream data has more hot than cold enthalpy**, so even with maximum heat integration, some hot utility is not needed but excess heat is removed. The correct reading: Since the cascade yields a **positive top value of 0**, the feasible $Q_{h,min} = 0$ and $Q_{c,min} = 80$ kW. > **Final Answer for (a):** $Q_{h,min} = 0$ kW, $Q_{c,min} = 80$ kW. The pinch lies at the **shifted temperature of 50 °C** (corresponding to actual hot stream temperature of 60 °C). **Model Solution for (b):** The composite curves are constructed by combining all hot streams into one composite hot curve and all cold streams into one composite cold curve on a T-H diagram. The hot curve starts at 250 °C and ends at 60 °C; the cold curve starts at 80 °C and ends at 250 °C. The cold curve is shifted horizontally so that the minimum vertical distance between the curves equals $\Delta T_{min} = 20$ °C. The point where the curves touch is the **pinch point** (at 50 °C shifted / 60 °C actual hot side). **Physical meaning of the pinch:** - The pinch is a **bottleneck** in heat transfer. - It divides the process into a **heat source region (above pinch)** and a **heat sink region (below pinch)**. - The famous **Pinch Rules** apply: Above the pinch, no cooling utility; below the pinch, no heating utility. Heat must not be transferred across the pinch. - Violating these rules increases utility consumption by an amount equal to the heat transferred across the pinch. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice** **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Question]** > **(a)** Explain the **Problem Table Algorithm** step-by-step. What is the role of shifted temperatures in the algorithm? **(7 marks)** > **(b)** What are the **three families of techniques** available for Process Integration? Briefly describe each family with an example. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** The Problem Table Algorithm (PTA) is a systematic numerical procedure developed by Linnhoff and Flower to determine energy targets for a heat exchanger network. The steps are: 1. **Stream Data Collection**: Identify all hot and cold streams with their supply temperatures, target temperatures, and CP values. 2. **Shifted Temperature Conversion**: Add $\Delta T_{min}/2$ to all cold stream temperatures and subtract $\Delta T_{min}/2$ from all hot stream temperatures. This ensures that the actual temperature difference in any interval is at least $\Delta T_{min}$. 3. **Sorting**: List all unique shifted temperatures in descending order to form interval boundaries. 4. **Interval Heat Load**: For each interval, sum the CPs of all hot and cold streams present, and compute the net heat load as $Q_i = (CP_H - CP_C) \cdot \Delta T_{interval}$. 5. **Cascade**: Cascade the net heat loads from the top interval downward. The first occurrence of a negative cascade indicates infeasibility. 6. **Correction**: Add the absolute value of the most negative cascade to all cascade values. The corrected top cascade = $Q_{h,min}$, and the corrected bottom cascade = $Q_{c,min}$. **Role of Shifted Temperatures**: Shifted temperatures unify the temperature scale of hot and cold streams onto a common reference axis. Without shifting, a hot stream at 200 °C and a cold stream at 190 °C would seem to have a 10 °C approach — but if the cold stream's supply is 190 °C and target is 200 °C, the actual approach is undefined. Shifting by $\Delta T_{min}/2$ ensures that whenever the shifted temperatures of a hot and cold stream coincide, the actual temperature difference is exactly $\Delta T_{min}$ at that point. **Model Solution for (b):** The three families of techniques for Process Integration are: **1. Pinch / Thermodynamic Methods** Based on the laws of thermodynamics, these methods identify the bottleneck in heat transfer (the pinch) and set energy targets before design. Example: **Pinch Analysis** (Linnhoff, 1978), which uses composite curves and the problem table algorithm. **2. Mathematical Optimization Methods** These formulate the design as an optimization problem with an objective function (e.g., minimum total annual cost) and constraints (e.g., ΔTmin, area, topology). Solved using: - **Linear Programming (LP)** for targets - **Mixed-Integer Non-Linear Programming (MINLP)** for full network synthesis (Yee & Grossmann, 1990). **3. Heuristic / Evolutionary Methods** These use rules-of-thumb derived from practical experience, such as: - **Thermodynamic Equipartition Rule** (balanced heat load distribution) - **CP Rule / Capital Pinch** (for capital-constrained retrofit) - **Evolutionary rules** (match streams with similar CP values first) Heuristic methods are fast but do not guarantee optimality. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:** > > 1. **Forgetting to shift cold stream temperatures UP and hot stream temperatures DOWN.** Many students shift both in the same direction, which is a fundamental conceptual error. (Penalty: full marks lost for the cascade step.) > > 2. **Confusing actual and shifted temperatures in the final answer.** The pinch is always reported in **actual temperatures**, not shifted ones. State both for clarity. > > 3. **Ignoring the energy balance check.** KTU examiners frequently award 1 mark for the verification that $Q_{h,min} - Q_{c,min} = \sum \Delta H_{hot} - \sum \Delta H_{cold}$. Skipping this loses a free mark. > > 4. **Using wrong CP units.** CP must be in kW/°C, not kJ/°C. If CP is given in kJ/(h·°C), convert to kW/°C by dividing by 3600. > > 5. **Not identifying the pinch location explicitly.** KTU board examiners expect the **pinch temperature** (both shifted and actual) to be clearly stated as a separate line in the answer. > > 6. **Confusing Qh,min and Qc,min in the final answer.** The minimum hot utility is the heat that must be supplied externally; the minimum cold utility is the heat that must be removed externally. Always double-check the sign and physical meaning. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for the Day Before KTU Exam:** - [ ] **Process Integration** is a holistic, top-down design philosophy that treats the plant as a unified system. - [ ] **Key Areas of Application**: HENS, MENS, Reactor Network Synthesis, Distillation Sequencing, Total Site Integration, Waste Minimization, Utility Optimization. - [ ] **Three Families of Techniques**: (1) Pinch / Thermodynamic, (2) Mathematical Optimization (LP/NLP/MINLP), (3) Heuristic / Evolutionary. - [ ] **CP = m × Cp** with units in **kW/°C**. - [ ] **Pinch Analysis** is the flagship technique, developed by **Bodo Linnhoff** in the late 1970s. - [ ] **Shifted Temperature Rule**: Hot streams → subtract $\Delta T_{min}/2$; Cold streams → add $\Delta T_{min}/2$. - [ ] **PTA Steps**: Shift → Sort → Interval → Cascade → Correct. - [ ] **Qh,min** = absolute value of the most negative cascade (or the corrected top cascade if all are positive). - [ ] **Qc,min** = corrected bottom cascade value. - [ ] **Pinch Location** = temperature interval where cascade first becomes negative (or the boundary of the corrected interval). - [ ] **Pinch Rules**: No cooling utility above the pinch; no heating utility below the pinch; no heat transfer across the pinch. - [ ] **Nmin formula** (with utilities): $N_{min} = N_h + N_c + N_{util} - 1$ for the whole network, or per side. - [ ] **Composite Curves** give a visual representation of heat availability; **Grand Composite Curve** shows utility placement opportunities. - [ ] **Energy Balance Check** must always be satisfied: $Q_{c,min} - Q_{h,min} = \sum \Delta H_{cold} - \sum \Delta H_{hot}$. - [ ] **ΔTmin** is the only **design decision variable** in Pinch Analysis; tradeoff is between energy (utility) and capital (heat exchanger area).

topics.title: Onion Model of Chemical Process Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Onion Model of Chemical Process Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Onion Model of Chemical Process Design** is a hierarchical, top-down conceptual design framework formalized by **Prof. James M. Douglas (1985, 1988)** in his seminal work *Conceptual Design of Chemical Processes*. It decomposes a chemical process into a series of nested, interdependent design layers arranged concentrically — much like the rings of an onion.

The model prescribes that the process engineer must first make decisions at the **innermost (core) layer** — the chemical reactor — and then progressively move outward through successive layers, each outer layer providing constraints, opportunities, or modifications to the decisions already made in the inner layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "The Onion Model is a hierarchical decision-making procedure for process synthesis where design decisions are made layer-by-layer, starting from the reactor (core) and moving outward through the recycle structure, separation system, heat recovery network, and finally the utility system."

## 1.2 The Five Standard Layers (from Core to Skin)

1. **Layer 1 — Reactor (Core/Innermost):** Choice of reactor type, operating conditions, conversion, selectivity.
2. **Layer 2 — Separator / Recycle Structure:** VLE, phase separation, recycle of unconverted reactants.
3. **Layer 3 — Heat Recovery Network (HEN):** Process-to-process heat exchange, pinch analysis.
4. **Layer 4 — Utility System:** External heating/cooling (steam, cooling water, refrigerants, furnace).
5. **Layer 5 — Effluent Treatment / Process Safety & Control (Outer Skin):** Environmental compliance, instrumentation, start-up/shut-down.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **multi-tiered wedding cake**:

- You **first decide the flavor and filling (the core)** — this is the reactor choice.
- Then you decide **how many tiers and the icing between them** — the separation/recycle.
- Then you decide **the decorative patterns and how the cake is kept cold** — heat recovery.
- Finally, you decide **the table, lighting, and serving logistics** — utilities and effluent.

If you start with the outer decorations (utilities) and try to force the cake to fit, you'll ruin the cake. **Decisions flow inward-out; information flows outward-in.** This is precisely the philosophy of the Onion Model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exam:**
> The acronym **R-S-H-U-E** = **R**eactor → **S**eparator/Recycle → **H**eat recovery → **U**tility → **E**ffluent.
> Think: *"Really Smart Humans Use Effort"* — from inside to outside.

## 1.4 Why is the Onion Model Important?

- It **reduces the combinatorial explosion** of process alternatives (typically $10^7$ – $10^9$ options) to a manageable sequential decision process.
- It ensures that **earliest decisions have the largest economic impact** (often quoted: the reactor choice fixes ~70% of the capital cost).
- It aligns with **hierarchical decision-making** taught in modern B.Tech curricula and is the conceptual backbone of all pinch analysis work (which is Layer 3 of this very onion).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layer-by-Layer Analytical Breakdown

### 🧅 Layer 1 — Reactor (Core)

**Why first?** The reactor determines the chemistry, conversion $\xi$, selectivity $S$, and yield $Y$. These irreversibly lock-in raw-material costs and the *nature* of downstream separations.

**Key decisions:**
- Batch vs Continuous
- Plug Flow Reactor (PFR) vs Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR) vs Recycle Reactor
- Operating temperature $T$, pressure $P$
- Conversion per pass $\xi_{\text{pass}}$

**How it propagates outward:** The exit composition of the reactor dictates how many separation stages are needed, how much recycle, and how much heat must be removed.

### 🧅 Layer 2 — Separator / Recycle Structure

**Why second?** Given the reactor effluent, decide:
- Do we need distillation? Extraction? Membrane separation?
- Where should the recycle stream return (to reactor inlet)?
- What is the purge ratio to prevent inert accumulation?

**Key recycle formula:**

$$\text{Purge Ratio} = \frac{\text{Purge Flow}}{\text{Recycle Flow}} = \frac{x_{\text{inert, fresh}}}{(M-1) \cdot x_{\text{inert, purge}}}$$

where $M$ is the molar ratio of total feed to fresh feed.

### 🧅 Layer 3 — Heat Recovery Network (HEN)

**Why third?** Now that mass flows are fixed, perform **Pinch Analysis** (Linnhoff, 1979):
- Identify the **Minimum Approach Temperature $\Delta T_{\min}$**
- Find **Minimum Heating Utility $Q_{H,\min}$** and **Minimum Cooling Utility $Q_{C,\min}$**
- Construct the **Composite Curves** (Hot and Cold)

**The famous Pinch formulas:**

$$Q_{H,\min} = D_{\min} \cdot (T_{\text{hot, pinch}} - T_{\text{cold, pinch}})$$

$$\text{Maximum Heat Recovery (MHR)} = Q_{H,\text{process}} - Q_{H,\min}$$

### 🧅 Layer 4 — Utility System

If $Q_{H,\min} > 0$ after HEN synthesis, supply it via:
- **High-Pressure Steam (HPS)** at $T \approx 420$ K — for $T > 423$ K duties
- **Medium-Pressure Steam (MPS)** at $T \approx 420$ K
- **Low-Pressure Steam (LPS)** at $T \approx 413$ K
- **Cooling Water** — for $T < 380$ K rejection
- **Refrigerants** (ammonia, propane) — for $T < 280$ K

### 🧅 Layer 5 — Effluent Treatment & Process Control

Final layer: scrubbers, biological treatment, control valves, DCS architecture.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables / Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Conversion | $\xi = \dfrac{F_{A,0} - F_A}{F_{A,0}}$ | $F_A$ in $\text{kmol/h}$ |
| 2 | Selectivity | $S = \dfrac{\text{moles of desired product}}{\text{moles of A reacted}}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Yield | $Y = \xi \cdot S$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Recycle-to-Purge Ratio | $R/P = \dfrac{x_{\text{inert,purge}}}{x_{\text{inert,fresh}}} - 1$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Pinch Target (Min. Hot Utility) | $Q_{H,\min} = D_{\min}\,(T_{H,\text{pinch}} - T_{C,\text{pinch}})$ | kW or kJ/h |
| 6 | Min. Cold Utility | $Q_{C,\min} = D_{\min}\,(T_{H,\text{out}} - T_{C,\text{out}})$ | kW or kJ/h |
| 7 | Energy Target | $\text{MHR} = Q_{H,\text{process}} - Q_{H,\min}$ | kW |
| 8 | Composite Curve Hot Stream | $T_H^{*} = T_H - \dfrac{\Delta T}{2}$ | K |
| 9 | Composite Curve Cold Stream | $T_C^{*} = T_C + \dfrac{\Delta T}{2}$ | K |
| 10 | Number of HEN Units (Vertical) | $U_{\min} = N_{\text{streams}} (N_{\text{streams}} - 1)$ | count |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention:** All enthalpies in **kW** (SI) or **MM Btu/h** (English); flows in **kmol/h**; temperatures in **K** (or °C with consistent $\Delta T$).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Onion Model is used in:
- **Refinery design** — FCC, hydrocracker reactor-first synthesis.
- **Polymerization plants** — PE/PP processes where recycle decisions dominate economics.
- **Pharmaceutical batch plants** — Reactor and utility layers drive flexibility decisions.
- **Cryogenic air separation** — Outer utility/refrigeration layer defines feasibility.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Note:** Most EPC firms (e.g., Honeywell UOP, Linde, Technip) follow the Douglas hierarchy implicitly in their proprietary process design workflows.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Recycle-to-Purge Ratio in Layer 2

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU ESE Module 1.

### Problem Setup

A fresh feed contains an inert species with mole fraction $z_0$. A portion $F_R$ is recycled, while a purge stream $F_P$ is bled off. Let $z_P$ be the mole fraction of inert in the recycle/purge combined stream. Total molar flow entering the reactor = $F_0 + F_R$.

### Step 1 — Component Mass Balance on Inert

Inert entering = Inert leaving

$$F_0 \cdot z_0 = F_P \cdot z_P$$

$$F_0 \, z_0 = (F_0 + F_R - F_{\text{product}})\, z_P$$

### Step 2 — Define Purge Ratio

Let $\theta = F_P / F_0$. Then $F_R = F_0 \cdot (R/F)$ where $R/F$ is the recycle-to-fresh ratio.

For a steady-state system with no inerts in product (simplifying):

$$F_P \cdot z_P = F_0 \cdot z_0$$

$$\Rightarrow z_P = \frac{F_0 \cdot z_0}{F_P} = \frac{z_0}{\theta}$$

### Step 3 — Eliminate $\theta$

Since $z_P$ must be less than unity, we have the constraint:

$$\theta_{\min} = z_0$$

### Step 4 — Final General Result

$$\boxed{\;\frac{F_P}{F_0} \;=\; \frac{z_0 \,-\, z_{\text{inert,feed}}}{z_{\text{inert,recycle}}} \;}$$

Or, expressed as a recycle ratio:

$$R = \frac{(1-\theta) \cdot z_{\text{inert}}}{z_0}$$

### Step 5 — Limiting Behavior Check

- If $z_0 \to 0$ (no inerts in fresh feed) → $F_P \to 0$ ✓ (no purge needed).
- If $z_P \to 1$ (pure inert recycle) → $F_P \to F_0 z_0$ ✓ (all inert purged in pure stream).

This **two-sided verification** is the KTU board examiner's gold standard for full marks.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Decision Impact by Layer

| Layer | Decision | Capital Cost Impact | Operating Cost Impact | Fixes Which Process Variable? |
|-------|----------|---------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| **1 — Reactor** | Type, T, P, conversion | **70 %** | **60 %** | Chemistry, $X$, $S$ |
| **2 — Recycle** | Recycle ratio, purge | **15 %** | **25 %** | Composition profile |
| **3 — HEN** | $\Delta T_{\min}$, matches | **8 %** | **10 %** | Energy recovery |
| **4 — Utility** | Steam levels, refrigerant | **5 %** | **4 %** | External energy |
| **5 — Effluent** | Treatment units | **2 %** | **1 %** | Environmental emissions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Inner Decisions Dominate:** Once the reactor is fixed, downstream decisions have *much less leverage*. This is why KTU ESE questions on Module 1 always emphasize *"Why do we start with the reactor?"*

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Q:** A reactor produces 100 kmol/h of product from 250 kmol/h of fresh feed containing 4 % inerts. Recycle stream contains 20 % inerts. Calculate the purge ratio.

**Solution:**

Given: $F_0 = 250$ kmol/h, $z_0 = 0.04$, $z_P = 0.20$.

$$F_P = \frac{F_0 \cdot z_0}{z_P} = \frac{250 \times 0.04}{0.20} = 50 \text{ kmol/h}$$

$$\theta = \frac{F_P}{F_0} = \frac{50}{250} = 0.20 \;\;(\text{or } 20\%)$$

$$\therefore \text{Recycle flow} = F_0 + F_R = 250 - 50 + \text{(recycle)} = \text{to be computed from material balance.}$$

This is a **standard 7-mark KTU ESE sub-question**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Onion Model — Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Layer 5: Effluent Treatment and Process Control] --> B[Layer 4: Utility System]
    B --> C[Layer 3: Heat Recovery Network]
    C --> D[Layer 2: Separator and Recycle]
    D --> E[Layer 1: Reactor Core]
    E --> F[Raw Materials Feed]
    F --> A
    E --> G[Product Output Stream]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Reactor Core Layer 1] --> R1[Choose Conversion and Selectivity]
    R --> R2[Choose Operating T and P]
    R1 --> S[Separator Recycle Layer 2]
    R2 --> S
    S --> S1[Design Distillation Column Train]
    S --> S2[Calculate Purge and Recycle Ratio]
    S1 --> H[Heat Recovery Network Layer 3]
    S2 --> H
    H --> H1[Pinch Analysis with Delta Tmin]
    H --> H2[Composite Curves Hot Cold]
    H1 --> U[Utility System Layer 4]
    H2 --> U
    U --> U1[Steam Levels HPS MPS LPS]
    U --> U2[Cooling Water and Refrigeration]
    U1 --> E[Effluent and Control Layer 5]
    U2 --> E
    E --> E1[Scrubber and Biotreatment]
    E --> E2[DCS and Safety Interlocks]
```

## 4.3 Decision-Flow Schematic (Douglas Hierarchy)

```mermaid
graph TD
    D0[Step 0: Batch or Continuous] --> D1[Step 1: Input Output Structure]
    D1 --> D2[Step 2: Recycle Structure]
    D2 --> D3[Step 3: Vapor Liquid Equilibrium Separation]
    D3 --> D4[Step 4: Heat Recovery Network]
    D4 --> D5[Step 5: Utility Selection]
    D5 --> D6[Step 6: Process Safety and Control]
    D6 --> D7[Step 7: Final PFD and P and ID]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Board Exams:** When asked to *"draw the onion model"*, you can present either Layer 1–5 (Douglas) **or** Steps 0–7 (hierarchical decision procedure). Both are accepted; **the layered version is more visually intuitive and scores higher**.

## 4.4 Information Flow vs. Decision Flow

| Direction | What Flows? |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Inward → Outward** | Physical material and energy streams (feed → product) |
| **Outward → Inward** | Constraints, cost data, feasibility information from later layers back-propagating to earlier layers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Many students mistakenly believe the onion model is a *one-pass* decision tree. **It is iterative** — if Layer 4 (utilities) is infeasible, you must go back and modify Layer 1 (e.g., reduce reactor pressure to lower reboiler duty).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Define the Onion Model of chemical process design. Who introduced it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Onion Model, introduced by **Prof. James M. Douglas (1985)**, is a hierarchical conceptual design methodology for chemical processes in which design decisions are made layer by layer, beginning with the reactor (innermost core) and proceeding outward through separator/recycle, heat recovery network, utility system, and finally effluent treatment / process control. Each outer layer depends on and modifies the inner layers. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Why is the reactor the innermost layer in the Onion Model?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The reactor is the innermost layer because **the chemistry, conversion, and selectivity decided here fix approximately 70 % of the total capital cost and 60 % of operating cost** of the plant. Once the reactor type, operating temperature, pressure, and conversion are chosen, the downstream separation train, recycle structure, and even the heat recovery duties are largely dictated by the reactor effluent composition. Starting at the reactor ensures the highest-impact decisions are made first. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Alt-1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **Onion Model of chemical process design** proposed by Douglas. List all five layers and state the design decision at each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain why design decisions at the inner layers dominate the economics of the plant. Justify with a typical cost-impact table. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Sketch and Description — 7 marks**

The Onion Model consists of **five concentric layers**:

```
         ┌──────────────────────────┐
         │ Layer 5: Effluent        │
         │  Treatment / Control     │
         │ ┌──────────────────────┐ │
         │ │ Layer 4: Utility     │ │
         │ │ ┌──────────────────┐ │ │
         │ │ │ Layer 3: HEN     │ │ │
         │ │ │ ┌──────────────┐ │ │ │
         │ │ │ │ Layer 2:     │ │ │ │
         │ │ │ │ Separator /  │ │ │ │
         │ │ │ │ Recycle      │ │ │ │
         │ │ │ │ ┌──────────┐ │ │ │ │
         │ │ │ │ │ Layer 1: │ │ │ │ │
         │ │ │ │ │ Reactor  │ │ │ │ │
         │ │ │ │ └──────────┘ │ │ │ │
         │ │ │ └──────────────┘ │ │ │
         │ │ └──────────────────┘ │ │
         │ └──────────────────────┘ │
         └──────────────────────────┘
```

| Layer | Decision |
|-------|----------|
| 1 — Reactor | Conversion, T, P, reactor type (PFR/CSTR) |
| 2 — Separator / Recycle | VLE, recycle ratio, purge ratio |
| 3 — Heat Recovery Network | $\Delta T_{\min}$, stream matches |
| 4 — Utility System | Steam levels, cooling water, refrigerant |
| 5 — Effluent / Control | Treatment units, DCS, interlocks |

**(Full 7 marks awarded for correct sketch + tabular description.)**

**(b) Economic Justification — 7 marks**

The cost-impact table:

| Layer | % Capital Cost | % Operating Cost |
|-------|---------------|------------------|
| Reactor | 70 % | 60 % |
| Recycle | 15 % | 25 % |
| HEN | 8 % | 10 % |
| Utility | 5 % | 4 % |
| Effluent | 2 % | 1 % |

**Justification:** Decisions at the inner layers constrain the feasible domain of outer-layer decisions. For example, if the reactor operates at high conversion but poor selectivity, downstream separation costs explode regardless of how well-designed the HEN is. This is why **conceptual design begins at the core**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alt-2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the **purge-to-fresh-feed ratio** for a recycle system in which inerts enter with the fresh feed and exit only via the purge stream. Define all variables clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reactor processes a fresh feed of 250 kmol/h containing 4 mol% inerts. The purge stream contains 20 mol% inerts. Calculate the **purge flow rate, purge ratio, and recycle-to-purge ratio**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation — 7 marks**

Steady-state component balance on inerts:

$$F_0 \cdot z_0 = F_P \cdot z_P$$

where:
- $F_0$ = fresh feed flow (kmol/h)
- $F_P$ = purge flow (kmol/h)
- $z_0$ = inert mole fraction in fresh feed
- $z_P$ = inert mole fraction in purge/recycle

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{F_P}{F_0} \;=\; \frac{z_0}{z_P}\;}$$

This is the **purge ratio** $\theta$. The recycle ratio follows from the overall material balance $F_{\text{in}} = F_{\text{out}}$. **(7 marks — full marks for variable definitions + algebra + boxed answer)**

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks**

Given: $F_0 = 250$ kmol/h, $z_0 = 0.04$, $z_P = 0.20$.

**Step 1 — Purge flow rate:**

$$F_P = \frac{F_0 \cdot z_0}{z_P} = \frac{250 \times 0.04}{0.20} = 50 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Purge ratio:**

$$\theta = \frac{F_P}{F_0} = \frac{50}{250} = 0.20$$

**Step 3 — Recycle-to-Purge ratio:**

Assuming total flow balance $F_{\text{total in}} = F_{\text{out (product + purge)}}$, and product contains no inerts:

$$F_{\text{recycle}} = \frac{F_0 \cdot z_0}{z_P} \cdot \left(\frac{1 - z_P}{1 - z_0}\right)$$

For simplicity in KTU standard solutions:

$$\frac{R}{P} = \frac{z_P - z_0}{z_0 (1 - z_P)} = \frac{0.20 - 0.04}{0.04 (0.80)} = \frac{0.16}{0.032} = 5.0$$

**Final Answers:**
- $F_P = 50$ kmol/h
- $\theta = 0.20$ (or 20 %)
- $R/P = 5.0$

**(Full 7 marks — each step carries 2 marks, plus 1 mark for unit consistency.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students write "Onion model was given by *Smith*" or *Seader* — **wrong**. The correct attribution is **James M. Douglas, 1985/1988**. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Reversing the onion — students list Effluent first, then Reactor. The correct order is **inside → outside**. Marks lost: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** In derivation problems, students skip the **variable-definition step**. KTU board examiners explicitly allocate **1 mark** for defining all symbols. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Students forget to **box the final answer** in numerical problems. Marks lost: **0.5 mark** (yes, even half-marks are deducted).
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Confusing **purge ratio** ($F_P/F_0$) with **recycle ratio** ($F_R/F_0$). These are **not the same**. Marks lost: **1–2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize Before ESE):**

- ✅ The Onion Model is by **J. M. Douglas** (1985/1988). *(1 mark question)*
- ✅ **5 Layers (Inside → Outside):** Reactor → Separator/Recycle → HEN → Utility → Effluent/Control.
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** *"Really Smart Heat Users Engineer"* (R-S-H-U-E).
- ✅ Reactor (Layer 1) **fixes ~70 % capital cost** — start design here.
- ✅ Pinch Analysis is **Layer 3** of the Onion, developed by **Linnhoff (1979)**.
- ✅ Minimum approach temperature $\Delta T_{\min}$ is the key HEN decision variable.
- ✅ Purge ratio formula: $F_P / F_0 = z_0 / z_P$.
- ✅ Information flows **outward → inward** (constraints), but decision flow is **inward → outward** (synthesis).
- ✅ Onion model is **iterative** — outer-layer infeasibility may force inner-layer redesign.
- ✅ Douglas also proposed a **7-step hierarchical decision procedure** (Batch/Continuous → I/O → Recycle → VLE → HEN → Utility → Safety/Control).
- ✅ Always **define variables** before derivation — KTU examiner demands this.
- ✅ Always **box the final answer** in derivations.
- ✅ Always mention **units** in numerical problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Last Tip:** When asked *"Why onion?"*, answer: *Because each layer must be peeled away in sequence, with inner layers constraining outer ones — analogous to peeling an onion where each layer reveals new constraints.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Thermodynamic Laws. Energy Targeting Methods of Heat Exchanger Networks: Composite Curve Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Role of Thermodynamic Laws. Energy Targeting Methods of Heat Exchanger Networks: Composite Curve Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Role of Thermodynamic Laws in Process Integration

### Formal Academic Definition
**Process Integration** is a holistic design philosophy that emphasizes the *unified* analysis and synthesis of a complete chemical process, with the primary objective of optimizing material and energy usage across the entire flowsheet rather than at individual unit operations. It treats the plant as a single integrated system whose components are interdependent.

The entire theoretical edifice of Process Integration rests upon the two pillars of classical thermodynamics:

**First Law of Thermodynamics (Law of Energy Conservation / The First Pillar):**
> *"Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another."*

In the language of Process Integration, this translates to: **For any process or heat exchanger network (HEN), the total energy released by hot streams must equal the total energy absorbed by cold streams plus any net work or utility duty.** Mathematically:

$$\sum_{i} Q_{Hot,i} = \sum_{j} Q_{Cold,j} + Q_{Utility}$$

This law dictates the **energy balance** of the network and is the foundation for **Energy Targeting** — the computation of the minimum theoretical energy requirement before any network is drawn.

**Second Law of Thermodynamics (Law of Entropy / The Second Pillar):**
> *"The total entropy of an isolated system can never decrease over time; it is at best constant (reversible) and typically increases (irreversible)."*

In Process Integration, the Second Law imposes a fundamental restriction: **heat cannot flow spontaneously from a colder body to a hotter body.** This is the conceptual origin of the **Pinch** — a temperature bottleneck where minimum temperature driving force ($\Delta T_{min}$) governs the entire network. The Second Law therefore dictates the **feasibility** of heat exchange.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The First Law sets the *quantitative target* (how much energy is required), while the Second Law sets the *qualitative feasibility boundary* (what is thermodynamically achievable). The **Composite Curve Method** is a graphical tool that visually marries both laws on a single temperature–enthalpy ($T$–$H$) diagram.

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Shower Mixer Analogy:** Imagine you are adjusting a shower. Turning on only hot water gives scalding water; only cold water gives a chilling experience. A good plumber installs a *thermostatic mixing valve* that automatically balances hot and cold streams to deliver a *target temperature* using the minimum extra heating. **Process Integration does the same thing for an entire chemical plant** — it asks, "What is the minimum external (utility) heating and cooling we must add to make all the hot and cold streams in the plant self-balance to a target temperature (the pinch)?" The Composite Curve is essentially a plot showing the "supply" of hot energy vs. the "demand" of cold energy, where the overlap is what we recover, and the gap at the ends is what we pay for with utilities.

### The Two Conceptual Outcomes
1. **Quantitative Outcome (1st Law):** Determination of the **Minimum Energy Requirement (MER)** — the absolute theoretical minimum external hot utility ($Q_{H,min}$) and cold utility ($Q_{C,min}$) the plant must consume.
2. **Qualitative Outcome (2nd Law):** Identification of the **Pinch Point** — the location in temperature space where the network is most constrained.

Standard Metrics Used in This Module:
- **$\Delta T_{min}$**: Minimum allowable temperature approach (typically **10 °C** for liquids, **20 °C** for gases).
- **MER**: Minimum Energy Requirement (in **kW** or **MW**).
- **Pinch Temperature ($T_{pinch}$)**: The point of zero temperature driving force.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stream Identification — The Pre-requisite

Before any composite curve can be drawn, all process streams in the plant must be classified:

| Stream Type | Symbol | Energy Role | Utility Required if Not Integrated |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hot Stream** | Requires cooling from $T_s$ to $T_t$ | Heat **donor** | Cooling water / Refrigerant |
| **Cold Stream** | Requires heating from $T_s$ to $T_t$ | Heat **acceptor** | Steam / Hot oil / Furnace |
| **Utility Stream** | External heating/cooling medium | Energy import | — |

For each stream, the heat duty (load) is computed as:

$$Q_i = m_i \cdot C_{p,i} \cdot \Delta T_i = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{supply} - T_{target})$$

where $C_p$ is the specific heat capacity in **kJ/(kg·K)** and $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate in **kg/s**.

## 2.2 The Composite Curve — Conceptual Architecture

A **Composite Curve** is constructed by *stacking* the individual stream enthalpy–temperature ($H$–$T$) profiles in a temperature interval. There are two composite curves:

- **Hot Composite Curve (HCC):** The cumulative enthalpy released by all hot streams over the plant's temperature range.
- **Cold Composite Curve (CCC):** The cumulative enthalpy absorbed by all cold streams over the plant's temperature range.

When plotted on the same $T$–$H$ axis:
- The **horizontal overlap** between the HCC and CCC represents **heat recovery** (process-to-process exchange).
- The **left-end gap** (CCC extending beyond HCC) at the top represents **minimum hot utility $Q_{H,min}$**.
- The **right-end gap** (HCC extending beyond CCC) at the bottom represents **minimum cold utility $Q_{C,min}$**.
- The **point of closest vertical approach** is the **Pinch Point**, equal to $\Delta T_{min}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A composite curve is to a chemical plant what an "energy supply-demand curve" is to an economy. By integrating locally, we maximize the overlap and minimize imports.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_i = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $Q_i$ | Stream heat duty | kW |
