| 2 | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | $\Delta T_{LM}$ | Log-mean temperature difference | °C or K |
| 3 | $T^*_{hot} = T_{hot}$ | $T^*$ | Shifted (interval) temperature, hot side | °C |
| 4 | $T^*_{cold} = T_{cold} - \Delta T_{min}$ | $T^*$ | Shifted temperature, cold side (offset by $\Delta T_{min}/2$) | °C |
| 5 | $\sum Q_{released} = \sum Q_{absorbed}$ | — | Energy balance (1st Law) | kW |
| 6 | $Q_{H,min} = Q_{cold,total} - Q_{rec,max}$ | $Q_{H,min}$ | Minimum hot utility | kW |
| 7 | $Q_{C,min} = Q_{hot,total} - Q_{rec,max}$ | $Q_{C,min}$ | Minimum cold utility | kW |
| 8 | $Pinch = $ closest approach $= \Delta T_{min}$ | — | Zero driving force location | °C |
| 9 | $\eta_{recovery} = \dfrac{Q_{rec}}{Q_{hot,total}} \times 100\%$ | $\eta$ | Energy recovery efficiency | % |
| 10 | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LM}}$ | $A$ | Heat exchanger area | m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder for Tables:** The notation $\vert \Delta T \vert$ should be read as the absolute value of $\Delta T$. **Never** use the vertical bar `|` character inside the markdown table; it breaks the table parser. Use the word "abs" or the LaTeX `\vert` command in such expressions.

## 2.4 The Pinch Design Rules (Gold Rules of HEN Design)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** These three rules are worth **3–5 marks** if asked as a short question.

1. **Do not transfer heat across the Pinch.** Heat exchange must occur *only* on the same side of the pinch.
2. **Do not use external utilities across the Pinch.** A hot utility must not be placed below the pinch on the cold side, and vice versa.
3. **Minimize the number of units:** $N_{min} = N_{streams} - 1$, where $N_{streams}$ = total number of streams (excluding utilities) on one side of the pinch.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil Refineries:** Used to design crude preheat trains (e.g., reducing furnace fuel by 30–50%).
- **Ethylene Plants:** Optimize quench and cracking furnace integration.
- **LNG Plants:** Minimize the enormous cold energy demand in liquefaction trains.
- **Pulp & Paper:** Recovery boiler integration with drying and evaporation sections.
- **Ammonia Plants:** Syngas cooling and ammonia synthesis heat recovery.

The composite curve method is the **first and most fundamental** tool taught in any Pinch Analysis course, forming the gateway to the **Grand Composite Curve (GCC)** and **Total Site Integration**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stream Heat Duty

The fundamental heat balance on a single stream undergoing sensible temperature change (no phase change) is derived from the First Law of Thermodynamics for an open, steady-state system with no work and negligible kinetic/potential energy changes:

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot dT$$

Integrating between the supply temperature $T_s$ and target temperature $T_t$:

$$\int_{0}^{Q_i} dQ = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \int_{T_s}^{T_t} dT$$

Evaluating the integral (assuming $C_p$ constant over the interval, valid for moderate temperature spans):

$$Q_i = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_t - T_s)$$

For a hot stream ($T_s > T_t$), the released heat is taken as positive and conventionally defined as a heat load that must be removed. For a cold stream ($T_t > T_s$), the absorbed heat is the load to be supplied.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Composite Curve Construction

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A process has the following streams. Construct the composite curves and determine $Q_{H,min}$, $Q_{C,min}$, and the pinch point. Take $\Delta T_{min} = 20$ °C.

| Stream | Type | $T_s$ (°C) | $T_t$ (°C) | $C_p$ (kW/K) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | Hot | 250 | 60 | 10 |
| H2 | Hot | 200 | 100 | 15 |
| C1 | Cold | 40 | 230 | 12 |
| C2 | Cold | 120 | 250 | 8 |

### Step 1: Compute individual stream heat duties
$$Q_{H1} = 10 \times (250 - 60) = 1900 \text{ kW}$$
$$Q_{H2} = 15 \times (200 - 100) = 1500 \text{ kW}$$
$$Q_{C1} = 12 \times (230 - 40) = 2280 \text{ kW}$$
$$Q_{C2} = 8 \times (250 - 120) = 1040 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 2: Determine interval boundaries (true temperatures)
Sorted temperatures: 40, 60, 100, 120, 200, 230, 250 °C.
This creates **6 temperature intervals**.

### Step 3: Apply the shift transformation for interval temperatures
For the **hot composite**, use $T$ as-is. For the **cold composite**, shift the cold temperature down by $\Delta T_{min}/2$ to create an equivalent "interval temperature" basis:

| Interval (True) | Interval (Shifted Cold) | $T_{int}$ (°C) | $\Delta T$ (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| C1: 40 → 60 | 30 → 50 | 40 | 20 |
| H1: 60 → 100 | 50 → 90 | 70 | 40 |
| C2: 100 → 120 | 90 → 110 | 100 | 20 |
| H2: 120 → 200 | 110 → 190 | 150 | 80 |
| H1+H2: 200 → 230 | 190 → 220 | 205 | 30 |
| C1+C2: 230 → 250 | 220 → 240 | 230 | 20 |

### Step 4: Cumulative enthalpy cascade (Hot Composite)
Start at $T = 40$ °C with $H = 0$ and integrate downward:

$$\begin{aligned}
H(60) &= 0 + 10 \times (250-60)\big|_{60\to 40} = 0 \\
H(100) &= H(60) + 10 \times (100-60) = 400 \text{ kW} \\
H(120) &= H(100) + (10+15) \times (120-100) = 900 \text{ kW} \\
H(200) &= H(120) + (10+15) \times (200-120) = 2100 \text{ kW} \\
H(230) &= H(200) + 15 \times (230-200) = 2550 \text{ kW} \\
H(250) &= H(230) + 10 \times (250-230) = 2750 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Cumulative enthalpy cascade (Cold Composite)
Start at the top interval and integrate the cold loads upward:

$$\begin{aligned}
H(250) &= 0 \\
H(230) &= 0 + 12 \times (250-230) = 240 \text{ kW} \\
H(200) &= H(230) + (12+8) \times (230-200) = 840 \text{ kW} \\
H(120) &= H(200) + 8 \times (200-120) = 1480 \text{ kW} \\
H(100) &= H(120) + 12 \times (120-100) = 1720 \text{ kW} \\
H(60) &= H(100) + 12 \times (100-60) = 2200 \text{ kW} \\
H(40) &= H(60) + 12 \times (60-40) = 2440 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Identify the minimum energy targets
The horizontal separation of the two composite curves in the **shifted temperature domain** gives the pinch. The pinch point is identified where the two curves are closest (separation = $\Delta T_{min} = 20$ °C).

From the cascade above, evaluating net surplus/deficit at each interval:

| Interval | Hot release | Cold demand | Net (Hot – Cold) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 40–60 | 200 | 240 | $-40$ |
| 60–100 | 400 | 480 | $-80$ |
| 100–120 | 200 | 80 | $+120$ |
| 120–200 | 800 | 640 | $+160$ |
| 200–230 | 450 | 60 | $+390$ |
| 230–250 | 200 | 240 | $-40$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **pinch region** is at the interface where the net changes from negative to positive, located in the **100–120 °C** true-temperature interval. The exact pinch temperature in the shifted domain is **$T^*_{pinch} = 100$ °C**, corresponding to **$T_{hot,pinch} = 110$ °C** and **$T_{cold,pinch} = 90$ °C** (separated by exactly $\Delta T_{min} = 20$ °C).

**Minimum Energy Requirements:**
$$Q_{H,min} = 440 \text{ kW} \quad \text{(cumulative deficit at the top)}$$
$$Q_{C,min} = 440 \text{ kW} \quad \text{(cumulative surplus at the bottom, by First-Law balance)}$$

## 3.3 Verification via 1st Law Energy Balance

Total hot stream heat available:
$$Q_{H,total} = Q_{H1} + Q_{H2} = 1900 + 1500 = 3400 \text{ kW}$$

Total cold stream heat required:
$$Q_{C,total} = Q_{C1} + Q_{C2} = 2280 + 1040 = 3320 \text{ kW}$$

Maximum possible heat recovery (limited by the smaller value):
$$Q_{rec} = \min(3400, 3320) = 3320 \text{ kW}$$

Therefore:
$$Q_{H,min} = Q_{C,total} - Q_{rec} = 3320 - 3320 = 0 \text{ kW (in best case)}$$

But the **2nd-Law pinch constraint** enforces additional utility duty beyond the trivial 1st-Law minimum. The 440 kW value above represents the *2nd-Law-corrected* minimum — a perfect illustration of why both laws are needed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Process Integration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plant Stream Data Collection] --> B[Classify Hot Cold Streams]
    B --> C[Compute Stream Heat Duties Q = m.Cp.dT]
    C --> D[Define Temperature Intervals]
    D --> E[Construct Hot Composite Curve HCC]
    D --> F[Construct Cold Composite Curve CCC]
    E --> G[Shift Cold Curve by DeltaTmin]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Locate Pinch Point}
    H --> I[Read Q H min from End Gap]
    H --> J[Read Q C min from Other End Gap]
    I --> K[Energy Targeting Complete]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Apply Three Pinch Design Rules]
    L --> M[Design HEN Above Pinch]
    L --> N[Design HEN Below Pinch]
    M --> O[Merge Networks]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Validate 1st and 2nd Laws]
    P --> Q[Final Heat Exchanger Network]
```

## 4.2 Composite Curve Architecture (T–H Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T_H_Plot[T vs H Coordinate System]
        direction LR
        X1[H kW] --- P1
    end
    P1[Pinch Point - Tpinch 110 C] 
    P2[QH min Top End] 
    P3[QC min Bottom End]
    P4[Heat Recovery Overlap Zone]
    P1 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P1 --> P4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** $P_1$ is the pinch point — the *neck* of the composite curves. $P_2$ is the top-end gap filled by hot utility (e.g., steam). $P_3$ is the bottom-end gap filled by cold utility (e.g., cooling water). $P_4$ is the body of the curve where process-to-process heat exchange takes place.

## 4.3 Pinch Decomposition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Main[Complete Process System] --> Above[Above Pinch Subsystem]
    Main --> Below[Below Pinch Subsystem]
    Above --> A1[Heat Sink Net]
    Above --> A2[Requires Q H min]
    Below --> B1[Heat Source Net]
    Below --> B2[Requires Q C min]
    A1 -. NO CROSS-PINCH HEAT TRANSFER .-> B1
    A2 -. NO CROSS-PINCH UTILITY USE .-> B2
```

## 4.4 Stream Categorization Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Process Stream] --> Q1{Is stream temperature higher than pinch?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| H[Hot Stream - Heat Donor]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is stream temperature lower than pinch?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| C[Cold Stream - Heat Acceptor]
    Q2 -->|No| U[Utility Stream]
    H --> Cool[Needs Cooling Utility]
    C --> Heat[Needs Heating Utility]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**State the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics and explain their significance in Process Integration.**

**Model Answer:**
**First Law:** Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it is conserved. In Process Integration, this gives the **energy balance** for the entire network, enabling the calculation of total utility requirements and heat recovery potentials. $\sum Q_{hot} = \sum Q_{cold} + Q_{utility}$.

**Second Law:** Heat cannot spontaneously flow from a colder to a hotter body; entropy of the universe increases. In Process Integration, this **imposes a minimum temperature driving force ($\Delta T_{min}$)** at the *Pinch Point*, defining the theoretical minimum external utilities the plant needs. It prevents the design of thermodynamically infeasible networks.

**Significance:** Together, the First Law tells us *how much* energy must be transferred, and the Second Law tells us *whether such transfer is feasible*. The Composite Curve Method is the tool that visually integrates both laws on a $T$–$H$ diagram.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Define the Pinch Point and explain how it is identified on a Composite Curve.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Pinch Point** is the location in the process where the **temperature driving force between the Hot and Cold Composite Curves is at its minimum**, equal to the specified $\Delta T_{min}$ (typically 10–20 °C). At the pinch, heat transfer is most constrained, and any further reduction in $\Delta T_{min}$ would demand disproportionately higher exchanger area or be thermodynamically infeasible.

**Identification Procedure:**
1. Draw the HCC and CCC on the same $T$–$H$ axes.
2. Shift the cold curve to the right by $\Delta T_{min}/2$ to create the *shifted temperature* domain.
3. The pinch appears as the **point of closest vertical approach** between the two curves in this shifted domain.
4. Read the **hot pinch temperature** $T_{hot,pinch}$ and **cold pinch temperature** $T_{cold,pinch}$ such that $T_{hot,pinch} - T_{cold,pinch} = \Delta T_{min}$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

(a) **Explain in detail the construction of Hot and Cold Composite Curves with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of $\Delta T_{min}$ on the energy targets. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction Procedure:**

1. **Step 1 — Data Collection:** List all hot and cold streams with $T_s$, $T_t$, and $\dot{m} C_p$.
2. **Step 2 — Heat Duty Calculation:** Compute $Q_i = \dot{m} C_p (T_t - T_s)$ for each stream.
3. **Step 3 — Interval Definition:** Sort all $T_s$ and $T_t$ values in ascending order; each unique value creates a temperature boundary.
4. **Step 4 — Hot Composite Construction:** For each interval, sum the $C_p$ values of all hot streams passing through that interval. Plot $H$ (cumulative enthalpy change) vs $T$ (interval temperature) as a staircase.
5. **Step 5 — Cold Composite Construction:** Repeat Step 4 for cold streams, but using **shifted temperatures** $T^* = T - \Delta T_{min}/2$.
6. **Step 6 — Plot Together:** Overlay both curves on the same $T$–$H$ axes.

**Sketch (schematic):**
- $Y$-axis: Temperature $T$ (°C). $X$-axis: Enthalpy $H$ (kW).
- HCC: descending staircase from top-left to bottom-right.
- CCC: ascending staircase from bottom-left to top-right.
- Vertical gap at the top = $Q_{H,min}$. Vertical gap at the bottom = $Q_{C,min}$. Closest vertical touch point = **Pinch**.

**Role of $\Delta T_{min}$:**
- **Larger $\Delta T_{min}$** $\Rightarrow$ Smaller heat recovery, **larger $Q_{H,min}$ and $Q_{C,min}$**, **smaller capital cost** (smaller area).
- **Smaller $\Delta T_{min}$** $\Rightarrow$ Larger heat recovery, **smaller utility demand**, **larger capital cost** (larger area).
- The optimum $\Delta T_{min}$ is determined by **total cost = utility cost + capital cost** minimization (cost targeting).

(b) **A refinery has the following streams. Construct the Composite Curves, identify the Pinch Point, and compute $Q_{H,min}$ and $Q_{C,min}$. Take $\Delta T_{min} = 10$ °C. (7 marks)**

| Stream | Type | $T_s$ (°C) | $T_t$ (°C) | $C_p$ (kW/K) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | Hot | 180 | 40 | 20 |
| H2 | Hot | 150 | 50 | 30 |
| C1 | Cold | 30 | 170 | 25 |
| C2 | Cold | 100 | 200 | 15 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Heat duties:**
$$Q_{H1} = 20 \times (180-40) = 2800 \text{ kW}$$
$$Q_{H2} = 30 \times (150-50) = 3000 \text{ kW}$$
$$Q_{C1} = 25 \times (170-30) = 3500 \text{ kW}$$
$$Q_{C2} = 15 \times (200-100) = 1500 \text{ kW}$$

Total hot $= 5800$ kW; total cold $= 5000$ kW. By 1st Law, $Q_{H,min} \geq 5800 - 5000 = 800$ kW (theoretical lower bound).

**Step 2: Intervals (sorted unique temperatures):**
30, 40, 50, 100, 150, 170, 180, 200 °C → **7 intervals**.

**Step 3: Shifted cold temperatures (subtract $\Delta T_{min}/2 = 5$ °C):**
25, 35, 45, 95, 145, 165, 175, 195 °C.

**Step 4: Heat cascade:**

| Interval (°C) | $T^*$ (°C) | Hot $C_p$ | Cold $C_p$ | Hot Release | Cold Demand | Net |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 30–40 | 30 | 0 | 25 | 0 | 250 | $-250$ |
| 40–50 | 45 | 20 | 25 | 200 | 250 | $-50$ |
| 50–100 | 75 | 20 | 25 | 1000 | 1250 | $-250$ |
| 100–150 | 125 | 50 | 25 | 2500 | 1250 | $+1250$ |
| 150–170 | 160 | 30 | 25 | 600 | 500 | $+100$ |
| 170–180 | 175 | 0 | 25 | 0 | 250 | $-250$ |
| 180–200 | 190 | 0 | 15 | 0 | 300 | $-300$ |

**Step 5: Pinch Location (where net changes sign):**
- Running deficit/surplus cascade from top (180 °C): starting deficit accumulates, then a large surplus appears in the 100–150 °C range. The pinch sits at the **boundary between intervals where the cumulative balance reaches zero from above**.
- Computing cumulative from the top (deficit-positive convention):
  - 180–170: deficit 250 kW
  - 170–150: deficit 250 + 500 = 750 kW
  - 150–100: surplus 750 – 1250 = $-500$ (means 500 kW recovered at this point)
  - The pinch is at **$T^* = 100$ °C** (interface of 50–100 and 100–150).

**Step 6: Final Energy Targets:**
$$Q_{H,min} = 800 \text{ kW} \quad (\text{higher than the 1st-Law lower bound of 800 kW — i.e., this is the optimum})$$
$$Q_{C,min} = 800 \text{ kW}$$

Pinch temperatures: $T_{hot,pinch} = 105$ °C, $T_{cold,pinch} = 95$ °C (separated by 10 °C).

---

### Question B — Choice Option 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

(a) **Discuss the three "Golden Rules" of Pinch-based HEN design. Why must heat not be transferred across the Pinch? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The three Golden Rules are:
1. **No heat transfer across the Pinch.**
2. **No use of external utilities across the Pinch.**
3. **Maximum feasible heat exchange (minimum number of units).**

**Why No Cross-Pinch Heat Transfer:**
The process is divided by the Pinch into two thermodynamically distinct regions:
- **Above the Pinch (Hot Region):** Net heat *sink*. Requires external hot utility.
- **Below the Pinch (Cold Region):** Net heat *source*. Requires external cold utility.

If heat is transferred *across* the pinch (e.g., a hot stream above the pinch heats a cold stream below the pinch), it creates an additional **driving force imbalance**: more hot utility must be added above the pinch to compensate for the heat removed by the cross-pinch exchanger. This *increases* $Q_{H,min}$ and $Q_{C,min}$ beyond the true minimum — a direct violation of energy targeting. The Pinch therefore acts as a **thermodynamic firewall**.

**Why No Cross-Pinch Utility Use:**
If a cold utility is used above the pinch, it absorbs heat that should have been recovered by the cold streams above the pinch. Similarly, a hot utility below the pinch would add heat that should have been removed by the hot streams below. Both inflate utility consumption.

(b) **For a process with the following data, perform energy targeting and design the minimum utility HEN using the Composite Curve Method. Take $\Delta T_{min} = 15$ °C. (7 marks)**

| Stream | Type | $T_s$ (°C) | $T_t$ (°C) | $C_p$ (kW/K) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | Hot | 250 | 80 | 12 |
| H2 | Hot | 180 | 60 | 8 |
| C1 | Cold | 50 | 200 | 10 |
| C2 | Cold | 140 | 230 | 6 |

**Model Solution Outline (expected steps for full marks):**

1. Compute all four $Q_i$ values.
2. Sort temperatures and identify 6 intervals.
3. Apply the temperature shift ($\Delta T_{min}/2 = 7.5$ °C) to the cold curve.
4. Build the enthalpy cascade for HCC and CCC.
5. Identify the pinch at the sign-change boundary of the cascade.
6. Read $Q_{H,min}$ and $Q_{C,min}$ from the end gaps.
7. Sketch the composite curves and label the pinch.
8. Apply the Golden Rules to obtain the HEN design — a recommended examiner expectation is a **3-utility / 4-stream** minimum network with **stream splitting** at the pinch.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the temperature shift** by $\Delta T_{min}/2$ on the cold curve. Without this shift, the pinch cannot be located correctly.
> 2. **Summing enthalpies instead of cascades.** Composite curves are *cumulative* — students often confuse interval enthalpies with cumulative ones.
> 3. **Ignoring stream $C_p$ differences.** Each stream's slope in $T$–$H$ is $1/C_p$, not 1. Draw proportional slopes.
> 4. **Reporting only the 1st-Law minimum** and ignoring the 2nd-Law pinch penalty.
> 5. **Not labelling units** (kW vs MW) on the enthalpy axis.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **First Law** = Energy conservation $\Rightarrow$ **Quantitative targets** (heat balance).
- **Second Law** = Entropy non-decrease $\Rightarrow$ **Qualitative feasibility** + **Pinch constraint**.
- **Composite Curve** = a $T$–$H$ plot of *cumulative* hot and cold enthalpy loads, used for energy targeting.
- **Pinch Point** = closest vertical approach of the two curves, equal to $\Delta T_{min}$.
- **$Q_{H,min}$** = top-end horizontal gap (deficit that must be supplied by hot utility like steam).
- **$Q_{C,min}$** = bottom-end horizontal gap (surplus that must be removed by cold utility like cooling water).
- **Three Golden Rules** for HEN design: (i) no cross-pinch heat transfer, (ii) no cross-pinch utility use, (iii) minimum units.
- **$N_{min}$ formula** (Stream-based): Above pinch: $U_{H,min} + N_{hot,above} + N_{cold,above} - 1$; analogously for below.
- **$\Delta T_{min}$ trade-off:** Larger value $\Rightarrow$ less recovery, lower capital. Smaller value $\Rightarrow$ more recovery, higher capital. Optimize by **total cost targeting**.
- **Shifted temperature domain** is the *operating* domain where the hot and cold curves must not intersect.
- **Grand Composite Curve (GCC)** is the next-level tool (Module 2/3 territory) that incorporates utility placement.
- Always **state units** (kW, °C) explicitly — KTU examiners routinely deduct marks for missing units.
- **Sign convention:** Hot utility $Q_H > 0$ (imported); Cold utility $Q_C > 0$ (exported to environment).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Problem Table Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Problem Table Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Problem Table Algorithm (PTA)** is a numerical, step-by-step computational procedure — originally formalized by **Linnhoff and Flower (1978)** — that systematically determines the **Minimum Energy Requirement (MER)** of a chemical process, namely the **minimum hot utility ($Q_{H,min}$)** and **minimum cold utility ($Q_{C,min}$)** demands, and locates the **process Pinch temperature(s)** prior to any heat exchanger network (HEN) synthesis.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, PTA is classified under **Targeting** — the pre-design phase of Pinch Analysis — and forms the computational backbone that feeds the **Grand Composite Curve (GCC)** and the **Composite Curves (CC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT745 – Module 1):**
> The Problem Table Algorithm is the *gateway numerical tool* for energy targeting. Every subsequent concept — Pinch Point, ΔTmin selection, GCC, HEN design — depends on a correctly executed PTA cascade.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Waterfall" of Heat

Imagine a tall building with many floors. Each floor is a **temperature interval**. Hot water (heat from hot streams) enters at the top floor and cascades downward. As it falls, each floor "drinks" a certain amount of heat (the heat duty of that interval). The leftover heat spills onto the next floor, and so on.

Now, if at some floor the available heat is **less** than the heat that floor wants to drink, we must **bring in hot utility from outside** (like an external heater). Conversely, if excess heat reaches the bottom (cold end), we must **dump it into a cold utility** (like a cooling tower or refrigerant).

The floor where the cascade just barely balances (heat available = heat demanded) — or first goes negative — is the **PINCH FLOOR**. Below the pinch, we need heat; above the pinch, we have excess heat to reject.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** PTA is essentially a *heat balance bookkeeping* exercise across a stack of temperature intervals. It answers: "How much external heating and cooling do we absolutely need?"

## 1.3 Physical & Mathematical Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $T_s$ | Stream supply temperature | °C |
| $T_t$ | Stream target temperature | °C |
| $CP$ | Heat capacity flow rate ($= m \cdot C_p$) | kW/°C |
| $\Delta H$ | Enthalpy change of a stream ($= CP \cdot \Delta T$) | kW |
| $Q_H$ | Hot utility duty | kW |
| $Q_C$ | Cold utility duty | kW |
| $\Delta T_{min}$ | Minimum allowed temperature approach | °C |
| $T^*$ | Shifted (interval) temperature | °C |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — The 5 Operational Phases

The PTA executes in **five structured phases**:

### Phase 1 — Data Collection & Temperature Shift
- For each stream, record $T_s$, $T_t$, and $CP$.
- Apply the **temperature shift rule** to convert actual stream temperatures into interval (shifted) temperatures:
  - **Hot streams** : $T^* = T - \dfrac{\Delta T_{min}}{2}$
  - **Cold streams** : $T^* = T + \dfrac{\Delta T_{min}}{2}$
- This shift ensures a constant minimum approach of $\Delta T_{min}$ in the composite curves.

### Phase 2 — Interval Construction
- List all unique shifted temperatures in **descending order**.
- Form $N$ intervals between consecutive shifted temperatures.
- For each interval $k$, compute the **net heat duty** $D_k$:

$$D_k = \sum_{\text{hot streams in interval}} CP_h \cdot (T^*_k - T^*_{k+1}) - \sum_{\text{cold streams in interval}} CP_c \cdot (T^*_k - T^*_{k+1})$$

> [!NOTE]
> Convention: $D_k > 0$ ⇒ net heat *deficit* (cold utility required in that interval); $D_k < 0$ ⇒ net heat *surplus* (hot utility available).

### Phase 3 — Heat Cascade (Backward Sweep)
- Starting from the top (hottest interval), cascade the heat down:

$$Q_{k+1} = Q_k - D_k$$

where $Q_k$ is the cumulative heat entering interval $k$ from above.

- $Q_1 = 0$ (no heat enters from above the hottest interval).
- Any $Q_k < 0$ is a *violation* (impossible — would need a heat pump).

### Phase 4 — Feasibility Correction (Forward Sweep)
- Identify the **largest negative** $Q_k$. Let its magnitude be $Q_{pen}$.
- Add $Q_{pen}$ to **all** $Q_k$ values (i.e., supply that much hot utility at the top).
- This guarantees $Q_k \geq 0$ everywhere.

### Phase 5 — Target Extraction
- After correction:
  - **Minimum hot utility** = $Q_{pen}$ (heat supplied to top interval)
  - **Minimum cold utility** = $Q_{N+1}$ (heat leaving the bottom interval)
  - **Pinch location** = the interval(s) where $Q_k = 0$ after correction.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Purpose / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T^*_h = T_h - \dfrac{\Delta T_{min}}{2}$ | Shifted temperature of a hot stream |
| 2 | $T^*_c = T_c + \dfrac{\Delta T_{min}}{2}$ | Shifted temperature of a cold stream |
| 3 | $\Delta H_i = CP_i \cdot (T_{s,i} - T_{t,i})$ | Enthalpy load of stream $i$ |
| 4 | $D_k = \sum_h CP_h (T^*_k - T^*_{k+1}) - \sum_c CP_c (T^*_k - T^*_{k+1})$ | Net heat duty of interval $k$ |
| 5 | $Q_{k+1} = Q_k - D_k$ | Heat cascade recursion |
| 6 | $Q_{H,min} = \vert \min_k (Q_k) \vert$ | Minimum hot utility (if min is negative) |
| 7 | $Q_{C,min} = Q_{N+1,\text{corrected}}$ | Minimum cold utility |
| 8 | $\text{Pinch interval} = \{ k : Q_{k,\text{corr}} = 0 \}$ | Pinch identification |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The PTA is the **universal energy targeting tool** in:

- **Oil refineries** — preheat train optimization of crude distillation units (CDU).
- **Petrochemical complexes** — olefin plant cracking furnace gas cooling.
- **Bulk chemical plants** — ammonia, methanol, ethylene oxide plants.
- **Food & pharmaceutical** — pasteurization, fermentation broth cooling.
- **Pulp & paper** — digester heat recovery network design.
- **Low-carbon process design** — quantifying $\text{CO}_2$ emission reduction potential before capital investment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Engineering Value: PTA converts a complex multi-stream problem into a *single one-dimensional numerical table*, enabling rapid scenario analysis (varying $\Delta T_{min}$, adding/removing streams, or evaluating utility price changes).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Full PTA Walkthrough

### Given Data

A process has 3 hot streams and 2 cold streams. $\Delta T_{min} = 20\,°C$.

| Stream | Type | $T_s$ (°C) | $T_t$ (°C) | $CP$ (kW/°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | Hot | 250 | 80 | 2.0 |
| H2 | Hot | 200 | 60 | 4.0 |
| H3 | Hot | 150 | 40 | 3.0 |
| C1 | Cold | 30 | 220 | 2.5 |
| C2 | Cold | 90 | 250 | 3.0 |

### Step 1 — Compute Shifted Temperatures

Apply the shift rules with $\Delta T_{min}/2 = 10\,°C$.

| Stream | $T^*_s$ (°C) | $T^*_t$ (°C) |
|---|---|---|
| H1 | $250 - 10 = 240$ | $80 - 10 = 70$ |
| H2 | $200 - 10 = 190$ | $60 - 10 = 50$ |
| H3 | $150 - 10 = 140$ | $40 - 10 = 30$ |
| C1 | $30 + 10 = 40$ | $220 + 10 = 230$ |
| C2 | $90 + 10 = 100$ | $250 + 10 = 260$ |

### Step 2 — Sort All Shifted Temperatures Descending

Unique shifted temperatures in descending order:
$260,\ 240,\ 230,\ 190,\ 140,\ 100,\ 70,\ 50,\ 40,\ 30$

This yields **9 intervals**.

### Step 3 — Identify Stream Presence in Each Interval

Build a presence matrix. A "✓" means the stream is active (its $T^*$ range covers the interval).

| Interval | Range (°C) | H1 | H2 | H3 | C1 | C2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 260 → 240 | — | — | — | — | ✓ |
| 2 | 240 → 230 | ✓ | — | — | ✓ | ✓ |
| 3 | 230 → 190 | ✓ | — | — | ✓ | — |
| 4 | 190 → 140 | ✓ | ✓ | — | — | — |
| 5 | 140 → 100 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | — | — |
| 6 | 100 → 70 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | — | ✓ |
| 7 | 70 → 50 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | — | — |
| 8 | 50 → 40 | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | — |
| 9 | 40 → 30 | — | — | ✓ | ✓ | — |

### Step 4 — Compute $D_k$ for Each Interval

Using $D_k = \sum_h CP_h (T^*_k - T^*_{k+1}) - \sum_c CP_c (T^*_k - T^*_{k+1})$:

**Interval 1 (260 → 240, ΔT\* = 20):**
- Hot: none
- Cold: C2 (CP = 3.0)
- $D_1 = 0 - (3.0)(20) = -60$ kW

**Interval 2 (240 → 230, ΔT\* = 10):**
- Hot: H1 (CP = 2.0)
- Cold: C1 (CP = 2.5), C2 (CP = 3.0) → total cold = 5.5
- $D_2 = (2.0)(10) - (5.5)(10) = 20 - 55 = -35$ kW

**Interval 3 (230 → 190, ΔT\* = 40):**
- Hot: H1 (CP = 2.0)
- Cold: C1 (CP = 2.5)
- $D_3 = (2.0)(40) - (2.5)(40) = 80 - 100 = -20$ kW

**Interval 4 (190 → 140, ΔT\* = 50):**
- Hot: H1 + H2 = 2.0 + 4.0 = 6.0
- Cold: none
- $D_4 = (6.0)(50) - 0 = 300$ kW

**Interval 5 (140 → 100, ΔT\* = 40):**
- Hot: H1 + H2 + H3 = 2.0 + 4.0 + 3.0 = 9.0
- Cold: none
- $D_5 = (9.0)(40) = 360$ kW

**Interval 6 (100 → 70, ΔT\* = 30):**
- Hot: H1 + H2 + H3 = 9.0
- Cold: C2 (CP = 3.0)
- $D_6 = (9.0)(30) - (3.0)(30) = 270 - 90 = 180$ kW

**Interval 7 (70 → 50, ΔT\* = 20):**
- Hot: H1 + H2 + H3 = 9.0
- Cold: none
- $D_7 = (9.0)(20) = 180$ kW

**Interval 8 (50 → 40, ΔT\* = 10):**
- Hot: H2 + H3 = 4.0 + 3.0 = 7.0
- Cold: C1 (CP = 2.5)
- $D_8 = (7.0)(10) - (2.5)(10) = 70 - 25 = 45$ kW

**Interval 9 (40 → 30, ΔT\* = 10):**
- Hot: H3 (CP = 3.0)
- Cold: C1 (CP = 2.5)
- $D_9 = (3.0)(10) - (2.5)(10) = 30 - 25 = 5$ kW

### Step 5 — Heat Cascade (Backward Sweep)

Initialize $Q_1 = 0$. Apply $Q_{k+1} = Q_k - D_k$:

| Interval $k$ | $D_k$ (kW) | $Q_k$ (kW) | $Q_{k+1} = Q_k - D_k$ (kW) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | −60 | 0 | $0 - (-60) = 60$ |
| 2 | −35 | 60 | $60 - (-35) = 95$ |
| 3 | −20 | 95 | $95 - (-20) = 115$ |
| 4 | 300 | 115 | $115 - 300 = -185$ |
| 5 | 360 | −185 | $-185 - 360 = -545$ |
| 6 | 180 | −545 | $-545 - 180 = -725$ |
| 7 | 180 | −725 | $-725 - 180 = -905$ |
| 8 | 45 | −905 | $-905 - 45 = -950$ |
| 9 | 5 | −950 | $-950 - 5 = -955$ |

### Step 6 — Identify Feasibility Violation

The **most negative** $Q_k = -955$ kW, occurring at $Q_{10}$ (the heat exiting the bottom of interval 9).

### Step 7 — Apply Correction

Add $|Q_{10}| = 955$ kW to **all** $Q_k$ values:

| Interval $k$ | $Q_k$ before | $Q_k$ corrected | $Q_{k+1}$ corrected |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | **955** | 1015 |
| 2 | 60 | 1015 | 1050 |
| 3 | 95 | 1050 | 1070 |
| 4 | −185 | **770** | 470 |
| 5 | −545 | 410 | 50 |
| 6 | −725 | 230 | 50 |
| 7 | −905 | **50** | −130 → **0** |
| 8 | −950 | 5 | −40 → **0** |
| 9 | −955 | 0 | −5 → **0** |
| 10 | — | — | 0 |

### Step 8 — Extract Targets

Recompute corrected cascade (subtract each interval's net surplus $D_k^-$ if needed, but the corrected values are $Q_{k,\text{corr}} = Q_k + 955$):

$$\boxed{Q_{H,min} = 955 \text{ kW}}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{C,min} = 0 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the corrected $Q_{10} = 0$ means **no cold utility is needed** — all heat is internally balanced. The pinch is located at the bottom (cold end), specifically at the **shifted temperature of 30 °C**, i.e., actual cold stream inlet at $30 - 10 = 20\,°C$ and hot stream outlet at $40 - 10 = 30\,°C$ (when working in the shifted frame the pinch is at the interval 9 boundary).

### Step 9 — Analytical Insight

- The pinch is identified as the interval(s) where the corrected $Q$ **first touches zero** from the hot end — here it spans intervals 7–9 (shifted 70 °C down to 30 °C).
- Above the pinch: net heat source → surplus 955 kW rejected as hot utility to the **top** of the cascade.
- Below the pinch: net heat sink → but $Q_{C,min} = 0$ because the top surplus exactly feeds the lower deficit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flow — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Stream Data] --> B[Record T_s, T_t, CP for each stream]
    B --> C[Apply Temperature Shift using delta Tmin/2]
    C --> D[Sort Shifted Temperatures in Descending Order]
    D --> E[Construct Intervals and Identify Stream Presence]
    E --> F[Compute Net Heat Duty D_k per Interval]
    F --> G[Initialize Q_1 = 0]
    G --> H[Backward Cascade: Q_k+1 = Q_k - D_k]
    H --> I{Any Q_k Negative?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Find Most Negative Q_pen]
    J --> K[Add abs Q_pen to all Q_k]
    K --> L[Extract Q_Hmin, Q_Cmin, Pinch]
    I -- No --> L
    L --> M[Output: Minimum Utility Targets and Pinch Location]
    M --> N[End]
```

## 4.2 Information-Flow Schematic of PTA

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Stream Inventory] --> P2[Temperature Shift]
    P2 --> P3[Interval Grid]
    P3 --> P4[Stream Presence Matrix]
    P4 --> P5[Duty Vector D_k]
    P5 --> P6[Heat Cascade]
    P6 --> P7[Feasibility Check]
    P7 --> P8[Utility Targets QH and QC]
    P7 --> P9[Pinch Temperatures]
    P8 --> P10[GCC Input]
    P9 --> P10
    P10 --> P11[HEN Design]
```

## 4.3 Pinch Identification Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Corrected Cascade Q_k] --> S2{Does Q_k = 0 for any k?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Mark that interval as Pinch Candidate]
    S3 --> S4[Convert Shifted T* to Actual T via Reverse Shift]
    S4 --> S5[Report Pinch Temperatures for Hot and Cold Side]
    S2 -- No --> S6[Pinch is outside process range or trivial case]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **reverse shift** for reporting actual pinch temperatures is:
> - Hot side pinch : $T_{h,\text{pinch}} = T^*_{\text{pinch}} + \Delta T_{min}/2$
> - Cold side pinch : $T_{c,\text{pinch}} = T^*_{\text{pinch}} - \Delta T_{min}/2$

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Problem Table Algorithm? State its significance in process integration.**

**Model Answer:**
The Problem Table Algorithm (PTA), developed by Linnhoff and Flower (1978), is a numerical targeting procedure that calculates the **minimum hot and cold utility requirements** and locates the **pinch point** of a process by cascading enthalpy balances across a set of temperature intervals. Its significance lies in providing **quantitative energy targets before any HEN design**, enabling engineers to evaluate the thermodynamic potential of a process, screen design alternatives, and quantify the economic trade-off between capital cost (via $\Delta T_{min}$) and operating cost (utility consumption).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is a temperature shift of $\Delta T_{min}/2$ applied in PTA?**

**Model Answer:**
The temperature shift is applied to **transform the actual stream temperatures into a common reference frame** where hot and cold streams can be simultaneously represented on a single temperature axis while preserving a constant minimum temperature driving force of $\Delta T_{min}$ at every point of contact. The hot streams are shifted downward by $\Delta T_{min}/2$ and the cold streams are shifted upward by $\Delta T_{min}/2$. This simplifies the cascade mathematics and ensures that the resulting targets are thermodynamically feasible.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** A process consists of two hot streams and two cold streams as given below. $\Delta T_{min} = 20\,°C$. Construct the Problem Table and determine $Q_{H,min}$, $Q_{C,min}$, and the pinch temperature. **(7 Marks)**

| Stream | Type | $T_s$ (°C) | $T_t$ (°C) | $CP$ (kW/°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | Hot | 180 | 60 | 3.0 |
| H2 | Hot | 150 | 30 | 2.0 |
| C1 | Cold | 40 | 170 | 2.5 |
| C2 | Cold | 100 | 200 | 4.0 |

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Shifted temperatures** (with $\Delta T_{min}/2 = 10$):

| Stream | $T^*_s$ | $T^*_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| H1 | 170 | 50 |
| H2 | 140 | 20 |
| C1 | 50 | 180 |
| C2 | 110 | 210 |

**Step 2 — Sorted unique $T^*$ (descending):** $210,\ 180,\ 170,\ 140,\ 110,\ 50,\ 20$

This yields **6 intervals**.

**Step 3 — Interval table and duties:**

| Int. | Range | ΔT\* | Hot CP | Cold CP | $D_k$ (kW) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 210→180 | 30 | — | C2 (4) | $0 - 120 = -120$ |
| 2 | 180→170 | 10 | H1 (3) | C1 (2.5) | $30 - 25 = 5$ |
| 3 | 170→140 | 30 | H1 (3) | C1 (2.5) | $90 - 75 = 15$ |
| 4 | 140→110 | 30 | H2 (2) | C1 (2.5) | $60 - 75 = -15$ |
| 5 | 110→50 | 60 | H2 (2) | C1 (2.5)+C2 (4) | $120 - 390 = -270$ |
| 6 | 50→20 | 30 | H1 (3)+H2 (2) | C1 (2.5) | $150 - 75 = 75$ |

**Step 4 — Backward cascade ($Q_1 = 0$):**

| Int. | $D_k$ | $Q_k$ | $Q_{k+1}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | −120 | 0 | 120 |
| 2 | 5 | 120 | 115 |
| 3 | 15 | 115 | 100 |
| 4 | −15 | 100 | 115 |
| 5 | −270 | 115 | 385 |
| 6 | 75 | 385 | 310 |
| 7 | — | 310 | — |

**Step 5 — All $Q_k \geq 0$, no correction needed.**

$$\boxed{Q_{H,min} = 0 \text{ kW}, \quad Q_{C,min} = 310 \text{ kW}}$$

**Pinch location:** The minimum $Q_k$ is 0, but inspection of the cascade shows heat flows entirely from hot streams to cold streams. The pinch is at the top boundary $T^* = 210\,°C$ (i.e., actual hot side $T = 220\,°C$, cold side $T = 200\,°C$).

---

**(b)** Explain how the **Pinch Point** identified by PTA governs the **fundamental rules of HEN design**. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

The pinch divides the process into two thermodynamically distinct regions:

1. **Above the pinch (heat source region):** Net heat surplus. Only hot utility is needed; cold utility must NOT be used.
   - Rule: $\,N_H \geq N_C$ (number of hot streams ≥ number of cold streams).
   - No cold stream should be heated using a cold utility.

2. **Below the pinch (heat sink region):** Net heat deficit. Only cold utility is needed; hot utility must NOT be used.
   - Rule: $\,N_C \geq N_H$ (number of cold streams ≥ number of hot streams).
   - No hot stream should be cooled using a hot utility.

3. **Across the pinch (ΔT = 0):** No heat flow.
   - This is the **bottleneck**; if any utility is wrongly placed across the pinch, the minimum targets are violated, and additional utility is required.

These rules are the foundation of the **Pinch Design Method** for synthesizing maximum energy recovery (MER) networks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the limitations of the Problem Table Algorithm. How does the **Grand Composite Curve (GCC)** extend PTA's information? **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Limitations of PTA:**
- Provides only **aggregate targets** ($Q_{H,min}$, $Q_{C,min}$, pinch) — no information on individual stream matches.
- Cannot directly suggest which utility type to use (LP/HP steam, cooling water, refrigeration levels).
- The pinch is identified only as a temperature *interval*, not a single line.
- Does not account for **utility placement** at intermediate temperatures (multiple utility levels).
- Assumes constant $CP$ per stream within its range.

**How GCC extends PTA:**
- The GCC is a **graphical plot** of the corrected heat cascade versus shifted temperature, with $Q$ on the horizontal axis and $T^*$ on the vertical axis.
- It visualizes the **heat availability profile** of the entire process.
- Enables identification of **multiple utility levels** (e.g., LP steam, MP steam, HP steam, refrigeration).
- Reveals **"pinch regions"** and areas of excess enthalpy at various temperature levels.
- Used as the basis for **HEN grid diagrams** and utility system integration.

---

**(b)** A process has the following streams ($\Delta T_{min} = 10\,°C$). Determine $Q_{H,min}$, $Q_{C,min}$, and pinch temperatures using PTA. **(7 Marks)**

| Stream | Type | $T_s$ (°C) | $T_t$ (°C) | $CP$ (kW/°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | Hot | 200 | 100 | 1.5 |
| H2 | Hot | 150 | 50 | 2.5 |
| C1 | Cold | 80 | 180 | 2.0 |
| C2 | Cold | 120 | 220 | 3.0 |

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Shifted temperatures** ($\Delta T_{min}/2 = 5$):

| Stream | $T^*_s$ | $T^*_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| H1 | 195 | 95 |
| H2 | 145 | 45 |
| C1 | 85 | 185 |
| C2 | 125 | 225 |

**Step 2 — Sorted $T^*$ descending:** $225,\ 195,\ 185,\ 145,\ 125,\ 95,\ 85,\ 45$ → **7 intervals**

**Step 3 — Duties:**

| Int. | Range | ΔT\* | Hot CP | Cold CP | $D_k$ (kW) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 225→195 | 30 | — | C2 (3) | $-90$ |
| 2 | 195→185 | 10 | H1 (1.5) | C2 (3) | $15 - 30 = -15$ |
| 3 | 185→145 | 40 | H1 (1.5) | C1 (2) | $60 - 80 = -20$ |
| 4 | 145→125 | 20 | H1 (1.5)+H2 (2.5) | C1 (2) | $80 - 40 = 40$ |
| 5 | 125→95 | 30 | H1 (1.5)+H2 (2.5) | C1 (2)+C2 (3) | $120 - 150 = -30$ |
| 6 | 95→85 | 10 | H2 (2.5) | C1 (2) | $25 - 20 = 5$ |
| 7 | 85→45 | 40 | H2 (2.5) | C1 (2) | $100 - 80 = 20$ |

**Step 4 — Cascade:**

| Int. | $D_k$ | $Q_k$ | $Q_{k+1}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | −90 | 0 | 90 |
| 2 | −15 | 90 | 105 |
| 3 | −20 | 105 | 125 |
| 4 | 40 | 125 | 85 |
| 5 | −30 | 85 | 115 |
| 6 | 5 | 115 | 110 |
| 7 | 20 | 110 | 90 |
| 8 | — | 90 | — |

**Step 5 — No negative $Q_k$, no correction needed.**

$$\boxed{Q_{H,min} = 0 \text{ kW}, \quad Q_{C,min} = 90 \text{ kW}}$$

**Pinch:** At the top, $T^* = 225\,°C$ (i.e., actual $T_h = 230\,°C$, $T_c = 220\,°C$).

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes (Costing Marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting the temperature shift** — always apply $T^* = T \pm \Delta T_{min}/2$ *before* constructing intervals. Examiners will deduct up to **2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Sign convention error in $D_k$** — using "hot minus cold" in the wrong order flips all signs. State the convention explicitly.
> 3. **Not correcting the cascade** — failing to add $|Q_{min}|$ to all entries will give physically impossible negative heat flows.
> 4. **Confusing shifted and actual pinch temperatures** — the pinch *interval* is in shifted coordinates; actual pinch must be reverse-shifted.
> 5. **Arithmetic slips in $D_k$** — a single miscalculated interval can propagate. Always re-check by summing $\sum D_k = \sum \Delta H_{\text{hot}} - \sum \Delta H_{\text{cold}}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **PTA = Linnhoff & Flower (1978) energy targeting algorithm.**
- ✅ **5 phases:** Data → Shift → Intervals → Cascade → Correct → Extract.
- ✅ **Shift rule:** Hot $T^* = T - \Delta T_{min}/2$; Cold $T^* = T + \Delta T_{min}/2$.
- ✅ **Duty per interval:** $D_k = \sum CP_h \Delta T^*_k - \sum CP_c \Delta T^*_k$.
- ✅ **Cascade recursion:** $Q_{k+1} = Q_k - D_k$, with $Q_1 = 0$.
- ✅ **Correction step:** $Q_{H,min} = \vert \min_k (Q_k) \vert$; add to all $Q_k$.
- ✅ **Cold utility:** $Q_{C,min} = Q_{N+1}$ (corrected).
- ✅ **Pinch:** Interval(s) where corrected $Q_k = 0$.
- ✅ **Pinch split:** Above pinch = hot utility region; Below pinch = cold utility region.
- ✅ **PTA output feeds GCC** → which enables multiple-utility targeting and HEN design.
- ✅ **Units:** All $CP$ in kW/°C, $T$ in °C, $Q$ in kW. Be consistent.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grand Composite Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Grand Composite Curve # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) The **Grand Composite Curve (GCC)** is a fundamental graphical tool in **Pinch Analysis** (a core methodology of Process Integration) that represents the **net enthalpy profile** of a process as a function of **shifted temperature** ($T^*$). It is constructed by plotting the cumulative heat availability (or demand) at each shifted temperature level after accounting for all hot and cold streams in the process. $$T^* = T - \frac{\Delta T_{min}}{2} \quad \text{(for hot streams)}$$ $$T^* = T + \frac{\Delta T_{min}}{2} \quad \text{(for cold streams)}$$ The GCC is a derived representation that simplifies the **Composite Curves (CC)** into a single, clean load-versus-temperature profile, making it the standard visual for targeting minimum utility consumption. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The GCC is the primary tool used to identify the **Pinch Point** (the point of zero net heat flow) and to set targets for **minimum hot utility ($Q_{H,min}$)**, **minimum cold utility ($Q_{C,min}$)**, and the **minimum number of heat exchanger units**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a multi-storey building where each floor represents a temperature range. On each floor, some rooms (cold streams) need heating, while others (hot streams) need cooling. Now think of a moving elevator (the "heat cascade") that starts at the top floor (highest temperature) and descends floor by floor, carrying a certain amount of heat energy. - If the elevator arrives at a floor with a **surplus** of heat (more hot streams than cold), it can **drop off** some heat to the floor below. - If the elevator arrives at a floor with a **deficit** (more cold streams than hot), it must **carry in** additional heat from the roof (hot utility). - At the **Pinch Point**, the elevator has exactly **zero** heat to drop off — the system is perfectly balanced locally. - The amount of heat the elevator picked up from the roof is the **minimum hot utility** ($Q_{H,min}$). - The amount of heat the elevator dumps at the basement is the **minimum cold utility** ($Q_{C,min}$). The **GCC** is simply a graph showing how much heat the elevator is carrying at each floor (shifted temperature). ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Conventions) - **Heat Capacity Flow Rate ($CP$):** Measured in **kW/K** (kilowatts per Kelvin). - **Shifted Temperature ($T^*$):** Measured in **°C** or **K**. - **Enthalpy Load ($Q$):** Measured in **kW** (steady-state) or **kJ** (batch). - **Minimum Approach Temperature ($\Delta T_{min}$):** The smallest allowable temperature difference between hot and cold streams at any point in the network; a **design choice** (typically **10 °C** for fluids, **20 °C** for reactors). > [!NOTE] > The GCC is essentially a plot of $T^*$ (y-axis) vs. cumulative enthalpy change $H$ (x-axis). Rightward movement indicates **heat deficit** (need for additional hot utility), leftward movement indicates **heat surplus** (need for cold utility rejection). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why is the GCC Useful? While Composite Curves (CC) provide a visual representation of the overall process heat balance, they suffer from a key limitation: they do not directly show **how much heat flows through each temperature interval**. The GCC solves this by: 1. **Decomposing** the temperature axis into discrete intervals (sub-systems). 2. **Calculating** the net heat available or required in each interval. 3. **Cascading** the heat surplus/deficit from the highest temperature downward. 4. **Identifying** the bottleneck (Pinch) and the utility targets. This makes the GCC the **starting point** for almost all advanced Pinch Analysis applications: HEN synthesis, distillation column placement, reactor integration, and total site heat integration. ## 2.2 Construction Logic — Step-by-Step Theory **Step 1: Stream Data Enumeration** List every process stream with its supply temperature ($T_s$), target temperature ($T_t$), and heat capacity flow rate ($CP = \dot{m} \cdot C_p$). **Step 2: Compute Shifted Temperatures** Apply the shift equations from §1.1 to convert all stream endpoints into the $T^*$ axis. **Step 3: Form Temperature Intervals** Sort all shifted temperatures in descending order. Each unique temperature creates a boundary; consecutive boundaries define an **interval**. **Step 4: Determine Net Heat Capacity in Each Interval** For each interval, identify which streams are **active** (i.e., span that interval). Compute: $$CP_{net} = \sum_{i \in \text{cold}} CP_i - \sum_{j \in \text{hot}} CP_j$$ - If $CP_{net} > 0$: the interval has a **heat deficit** (cold streams dominate). - If $CP_{net} < 0$: the interval has a **heat surplus** (hot streams dominate). **Step 5: Compute Interval Enthalpy Change** The enthalpy change in each interval is: $$\Delta H_k = CP_{net,k} \cdot \Delta T_k$$ where $\Delta T_k$ is the temperature span of the $k^{th}$ interval. **Step 6: Cascade Heat Down the Temperature Axis** Starting from the highest interval with a value of zero (or with the addition of $Q_{H,min}$), accumulate the interval enthalpies: $$H_k = H_{k-1} + \Delta H_k$$ The minimum value reached in the cascade defines $Q_{H,min}$: $$Q_{H,min} = -\min(H_k)$$ **Step 7: Construct the GCC** Plot the cumulative $H$ values on the x-axis and the shifted temperatures on the y-axis, connecting them with line segments. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula | Symbol / Unit | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | Shifted temperature (hot) | $T^*_h = T_h - \Delta T_{min}/2$ | °C | Effective hot-stream temperature | | Shifted temperature (cold) | $T^*_c = T_c + \Delta T_{min}/2$ | °C | Effective cold-stream temperature | | Heat capacity flow rate | $CP = \dot{m} \cdot C_p$ | kW/K | Stream's heat demand per degree | | Net interval CP | $CP_{net} = \Sigma CP_{cold} - \Sigma CP_{hot}$ | kW/K | Heat balance of an interval | | Interval enthalpy change | $\Delta H = CP_{net} \cdot \Delta T$ | kW | Heat released or absorbed in $k$ | | Cascade accumulation | $H_k = H_{k-1} + \Delta H_k$ | kW | Cumulative enthalpy at boundary $k$ | | Minimum hot utility | $Q_{H,min} = -\min(H_k)$ | kW | Heat to be added externally | | Minimum cold utility | $Q_{C,min} = H_{bottom}$ | kW | Heat to be removed externally | | Pinch temperature (hot side) | $T_{h,pinch} = T^*_{pinch} + \Delta T_{min}/2$ | °C | Hot-stream pinch temperature | | Pinch temperature (cold side) | $T_{c,pinch} = T^*_{pinch} - \Delta T_{min}/2$ | °C | Cold-stream pinch temperature | | Stream enthalpy load | $Q = CP \cdot (T_s - T_t)$ | kW | Total heat in a single stream | > [!NOTE] > **Critical sign convention used by KTU examiners:** In the cascade, the convention is **heat deficit = positive** (need to add heat) and **heat surplus = negative** (excess to be removed). The minimum cascade value determines the hot utility target. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The GCC is the cornerstone of **Heat Exchanger Network (HEN) design** in refineries, petrochemical plants, ammonia plants, and pulp/paper mills. By establishing the **merit frontier** of the process, it allows engineers to: - **Minimize external utility consumption**, reducing fuel and cooling water costs. - **Reduce CO₂ emissions** (often by 20–50% in retrofit projects). - **Identify the Pinch**, which guides the placement of reactors, distillation columns, and heat pumps. - **Estimate the minimum number of heat exchangers** required, using Euler's graph theorem: $$U_{min} = N_{streams} + N_{utilities} - N_{independent\ subsystems}$$ # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Construction of a Grand Composite Curve ### 3.1.1 Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Scheme Style) A process has the following stream data. The minimum approach temperature is $\Delta T_{min} = 20 \text{ °C}$. Construct the **Grand Composite Curve** and determine the minimum hot and cold utility targets and the pinch temperatures. | Stream | Type | $T_s$ (°C) | $T_t$ (°C) | $CP$ (kW/K) | |---|---|---|---|---| | H1 | Hot | 200 | 80 | 2.0 | | H2 | Hot | 150 | 40 | 1.0 | | C1 | Cold | 60 | 180 | 1.5 | | C2 | Cold | 100 | 200 | 1.0 | ### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Compute the Shifted Temperatures Using the shift equations with $\Delta T_{min}/2 = 10 \text{ °C}$: For hot streams (shift down by 10 °C): $$T^*_{H1,s} = 200 - 10 = 190 \text{ °C}, \quad T^*_{H1,t} = 80 - 10 = 70 \text{ °C}$$ $$T^*_{H2,s} = 150 - 10 = 140 \text{ °C}, \quad T^*_{H2,t} = 40 - 10 = 30 \text{ °C}$$ For cold streams (shift up by 10 °C): $$T^*_{C1,s} = 60 + 10 = 70 \text{ °C}, \quad T^*_{C1,t} = 180 + 10 = 190 \text{ °C}$$ $$T^*_{C2,s} = 100 + 10 = 110 \text{ °C}, \quad T^*_{C2,t} = 200 + 10 = 210 \text{ °C}$$ ### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Identify Temperature Intervals Collect all shifted temperatures, sort them in **descending order**, and mark the unique boundary points: $$T^* \in \{210,\ 190,\ 140,\ 110,\ 70,\ 30\}^{\circ}C$$ This produces **five intervals**: | Interval | $T^*$ range (°C) | $\Delta T^*$ (K) | |---|---|---| | I | 210 – 190 | 20 | | II | 190 – 140 | 50 | | III | 140 – 110 | 30 | | IV | 110 – 70 | 40 | | V | 70 – 30 | 40 | ### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Determine Active Streams and Net $CP$ in Each Interval | Interval | Active Hot Streams | Active Cold Streams | $\Sigma CP_{hot}$ | $\Sigma CP_{cold}$ | $CP_{net}$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | I (210–190) | H1 | C1 | 2.0 | 1.5 | $-0.5$ | | II (190–140) | H1, H2 | C1, C2 | 3.0 | 2.5 | $-0.5$ | | III (140–110) | H1, H2 | C1, C2 | 3.0 | 2.5 | $-0.5$ | | IV (110–70) | H1, H2 | C1 | 3.0 | 1.5 | $-1.5$ | | V (70–30) | H2 | (none) | 1.0 | 0.0 | $-1.0$ | ### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Compute Interval Enthalpy Change $\Delta H_k$ Applying $\Delta H_k = CP_{net,k} \cdot \Delta T^*_k$: $$\Delta H_I = (-0.5) \times 20 = -10 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_{II} = (-0.5) \times 50 = -25 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_{III} = (-0.5) \times 30 = -15 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_{IV} = (-1.5) \times 40 = -60 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_V = (-1.0) \times 40 = -40 \text{ kW}$$ ### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Cascade Calculation (Problem Table Algorithm) Starting with $H_{top} = 0$ (assuming no external hot utility at the top boundary): | Interval | $T^*$ (°C) | $\Delta H_k$ (kW) | Cumulative $H$ (kW) | |---|---|---|---| | — | 210 | — | 0 | | I | 190 | $-10$ | $-10$ | | II | 140 | $-25$ | $-35$ | | III | 110 | $-15$ | $-50$ | | IV | 70 | $-60$ | $-110$ | | V | 30 | $-40$ | $-150$ | ### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Identify Utility Targets The **minimum** (most negative) cumulative value is at the bottom: $H_{min} = -150 \text{ kW}$. The maximum cumulative value is at the top: $H_{max} = 0 \text{ kW}$. Applying the target equations: $$Q_{H,min} = -\min(H_k) = -(-150) = 150 \text{ kW}$$ $$Q_{C,min} = Q_{H,min} + H_{bottom} = 150 + (-150) = 0 \text{ kW}$$ Wait — we need to recompute the cascade **with** the added hot utility at the top. Adding $Q_{H,min} = 150$ to every cascade value: | $T^*$ (°C) | Adjusted $H$ (kW) | |---|---| | 210 | 150 | | 190 | 140 | | 140 | 115 | | 110 | 100 | | 70 | 40 | | 30 | 0 | **Re-checking the bottom:** $$Q_{C,min} = H_{bottom} = 0 \text{ kW}$$ This indicates that the process has a perfect internal heat balance below the pinch — all remaining heat is internally matched. This is unusual; the more common case has $Q_{C,min} > 0$. ### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Identify the Pinch The pinch occurs at the boundary where the cascade value first becomes zero. Looking at the table, the cascade reaches zero at $T^* = 30 \text{ °C}$. This means: - **Hot-side pinch temperature:** $T_{h,pinch} = 30 + 10 = 40 \text{ °C}$ - **Cold-side pinch temperature:** $T_{c,pinch} = 30 - 10 = 20 \text{ °C}$ ### 3.1.9 Step 8 — Summary of Results | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Minimum hot utility, $Q_{H,min}$ | **150 kW** | | Minimum cold utility, $Q_{C,min}$ | **0 kW** | | Pinch shifted temperature, $T^*_{pinch}$ | **30 °C** | | Hot-side pinch temperature, $T_{h,pinch}$ | **40 °C** | | Cold-side pinch temperature, $T_{c,pinch}$ | **20 °C** | ### 3.1.10 Interpretation The GCC reveals that: - Above the pinch ($T^* > 30 \text{ °C}$), the process is a **net heat sink** — external hot utility is required. - Below the pinch ($T^* < 30 \text{ °C}$), the process is **heat neutral** — no external cooling is needed. - The pinch acts as a **thermodynamic bottleneck**; no heat should be transferred across it in an optimal HEN. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering insight:** A process with $Q_{C,min} = 0$ is unusually well-integrated. In practice, modifying the $\Delta T_{min}$ assumption or adding/removing streams will alter the targets. Always validate the chosen $\Delta T_{min}$ against equipment cost trade-offs. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Construction Pipeline of the GCC The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the systematic procedure for constructing the Grand Composite Curve from raw process stream data. This is a *Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow* mapping the construction interactions rather than the physical shape of the GCC itself. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Stream Data Input
T_s T_t CP for each stream"] --> B["Shift Temperature Axis
Apply delta Tmin over 2 shift"] B --> C["Sort All Shifted Endpoints
in descending order"] C --> D["Form Temperature Intervals
Define boundaries"] D --> E["Identify Active Streams
per interval"] E --> F["Compute Net CP
Sum cold minus Sum hot"] F --> G["Compute Interval Enthalpy
delta H equals CPnet times delta T"] G --> H["Build Heat Cascade Table
Accumulate H from top"] H --> I["Find Minimum Cascade Value
Identify pinch location"] I --> J["Add QHmin to Cascade
Make all values non-negative"] J --> K["Plot Grand Composite Curve
H versus T shifted"] K --> L["Extract Targets
QHmin QCmin Pinch Temperatures"] ``` **Caption:** End-to-end methodology for GCC construction, as expected in KTU ESE 14-mark problems. ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Information Flow from Composite Curves to Grand Composite Curve ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RawData["Raw Process Data"] A1["Hot Stream List
H1 H2 ... Hn"] A2["Cold Stream List
C1 C2 ... Cm"] A3["Delta Tmin Assumption
Design Parameter"] end subgraph Stage1["Stage 1 Composite Curves"] B1["Hot Composite Curve
Total heat release profile"] B2["Cold Composite Curve
Total heat demand profile"] B3["Hot and Cold Overlap
Visualize recovery potential"] end subgraph Stage2["Stage 2 Grand Composite Curve"] C1["Shifted Temperature Axis
T hot minus delta Tmin over 2"] C2["Net Interval Heat Balance
Sum CPcold minus Sum CPhot"] C3["Cascade Construction
Heat surplus or deficit per interval"] C4["Pinch Point Identification
Zero heat flow boundary"] C5["Utility Targets
QHmin and QCmin"] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B2 A3 --> B1 A3 --> B2 B1 --> B3 B2 --> B3 B1 --> C2 B2 --> C2 A3 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C2 --> C3 C3 --> C4 C3 --> C5 ``` **Caption:** The GCC is mathematically derived from the Composite Curves by shifting and differencing. The result is a clean, monotonic target profile that directly yields pinch location and utility requirements. ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Pinch Decomposition Principle ```mermaid flowchart TD P["Grand Composite Curve
Full T-star versus H profile"] --> Q{"Pinch Point
Zero heat flow"} Q --> R["Above Pinch
Heat Sink Region"] Q --> S["Below Pinch
Heat Source Region"] R --> R1["Requires External Hot Utility
QHmin to be supplied"] R --> R2["No Process Cooling Needed
No cold utility above pinch"] S --> S1["Heat Surplus Region
Excess enthalpy to reject"] S --> S2["Requires External Cold Utility
QCmin to be removed"] S --> S3["No Process Heating Below Pinch
Isolated subsystem"] R1 --> T["HEN Design Rule
Do not transfer heat across pinch"] S2 --> T T --> U["Maximum Heat Recovery
Minimum Utility Targets Achieved"] ``` **Caption:** The pinch divides the GCC into two thermodynamically independent subsystems. Crossing heat transfer across the pinch always increases utility consumption. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define the Grand Composite Curve (GCC) and state its significance in process integration.** **Model Answer:** The Grand Composite Curve (GCC) is a plot of the **cumulative net enthalpy change** of a process as a function of the **shifted temperature** ($T^*$). It is constructed by cascading the interval-by-interval heat balance of all hot and cold streams in the process. **Significance in Process Integration:** 1. It is the primary tool for **targeting the minimum hot and cold utility** requirements. 2. It identifies the **Pinch Point**, which is the most constrained temperature in the process. 3. It guides the **placement of process units** such as reactors, distillation columns, and heat pumps. 4. It serves as the basis for the design of the **maximum energy recovery (MER) network**. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is the pinch point? How is it identified from the Grand Composite Curve?** **Model Answer:** The **Pinch Point** is the temperature at which the GCC attains its **minimum** cumulative enthalpy value, i.e., the location of the **narrowest gap** between the hot and cold composite curves where the temperature difference equals $\Delta T_{min}$. **Identification from the GCC:** - In the cascade table, the pinch corresponds to the boundary where the cumulative heat flow first reaches its lowest value (often zero after adding $Q_{H,min}$). - The shifted pinch temperature $T^*_{pinch}$ is then converted to the actual hot-side and cold-side pinch temperatures using: $$T_{h,pinch} = T^*_{pinch} + \frac{\Delta T_{min}}{2}, \quad T_{c,pinch} = T^*_{pinch} - \frac{\Delta T_{min}}{2}$$ The pinch acts as a **thermodynamic bottleneck**: in an optimal network, no heat should be transferred across it. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Forgetting the sign convention** in the cascade is the most common error (loses 2–3 marks). Always remember: cold-stream contributions are **positive** (deficit), hot-stream contributions are **negative** (surplus). > - **Skipping the temperature shift** (Step 2 of the methodology) leads to an incorrect interval structure and an incorrect GCC. > - **Not clearly stating the pinch temperatures in both actual and shifted forms** is penalized. Show both $T^*_{pinch}$ and the converted $T_{h,pinch}, T_{c,pinch}$. > - **Arithmetic mistakes in the cascade accumulation** are easy to make; double-check each row of the problem table. > - Students often confuse $Q_{H,min}$ with $Q_{C,min}$. Re-read carefully: $Q_{H,min}$ comes from the *minimum* (most negative) cascade value, $Q_{C,min}$ comes from the *final* (bottom) cascade value **after** $Q_{H,min}$ is added. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] A process has the following stream data with $\Delta T_{min} = 20 \text{ °C}$: | Stream | Type | $T_s$ (°C) | $T_t$ (°C) | $CP$ (kW/K) | |---|---|---|---|---| | H1 | Hot | 250 | 120 | 3.0 | | H2 | Hot | 200 | 80 | 2.0 | | C1 | Cold | 90 | 230 | 4.0 | | C2 | Cold | 150 | 250 | 1.5 | **(a)** Construct the Problem Table and determine the **minimum hot and cold utility** targets. (7 marks) **(b)** Plot the **Grand Composite Curve** and identify the **Pinch Point** with its hot and cold side temperatures. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — Problem Table Construction** **Step 1: Shifted temperatures (using $\Delta T_{min}/2 = 10$ °C):** $$H1: 250 \to 240; \quad 120 \to 110$$ $$H2: 200 \to 190; \quad 80 \to 70$$ $$C1: 90 \to 100; \quad 230 \to 240$$ $$C2: 150 \to 160; \quad 250 \to 260$$ **Step 2: Sorted unique shifted temperatures (descending):** $$T^* \in \{260,\ 240,\ 190,\ 160,\ 100,\ 70\}^{\circ}C$$ This produces **five intervals**. **Step 3: Active streams and net $CP$ per interval:** | Interval | $T^*$ range (°C) | $\Delta T^*$ (K) | Active Hot | Active Cold | $CP_{net}$ (kW/K) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | I | 260 – 240 | 20 | (none) | C2 | $+1.5$ | | II | 240 – 190 | 50 | H1 | C1, C2 | $4+1.5-3 = +2.5$ | | III | 190 – 160 | 30 | H1, H2 | C1, C2 | $4+1.5-5 = +0.5$ | | IV | 160 – 100 | 60 | H1, H2 | C1 | $4-5 = -1.0$ | | V | 100 – 70 | 30 | H1, H2 | (none) | $-5.0$ | **Step 4: Interval enthalpies $\Delta H_k = CP_{net,k} \cdot \Delta T^*_k$:** $$\Delta H_I = 1.5 \times 20 = 30 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_{II} = 2.5 \times 50 = 125 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_{III} = 0.5 \times 30 = 15 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_{IV} = -1.0 \times 60 = -60 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_V = -5.0 \times 30 = -150 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5: Cascade table (starting from 0 at top):** | $T^*$ (°C) | $\Delta H$ (kW) | Cumulative $H$ (kW) | |---|---|---| | 260 | — | 0 | | 240 | 30 | 30 | | 190 | 125 | 155 | | 160 | 15 | 170 | | 100 | $-60$ | 110 | | 70 | $-150$ | $-40$ | The minimum cascade value is $-40 \text{ kW}$. So: $$Q_{H,min} = 40 \text{ kW}$$ Adding 40 to every entry, the new bottom value (cold utility target) is: $$Q_{C,min} = -40 + 40 = 0 \text{ kW}$$ **Adjusted cascade:** | $T^*$ (°C) | Adjusted $H$ (kW) | |---|---| | 260 | 40 | | 240 | 70 | | 190 | 195 | | 160 | 210 | | 100 | 150 | | 70 | 0 | So the final answers for part (a) are: $Q_{H,min} = 40$ kW, $Q_{C,min} = 0$ kW. --- **Part (b) — GCC Plot and Pinch Identification** Using the adjusted cascade values: | Point | $T^*$ (°C) | $H$ (kW) | |---|---|---| | A | 260 | 40 | | B | 240 | 70 | | C | 190 | 195 | | D | 160 | 210 | | E | 100 | 150 | | F | 70 | 0 | **GCC Shape Description:** The GCC starts at point A (40 kW at $T^* = 260$ °C), rises to the right, peaks at point D (210 kW at $T^* = 160$ °C), then descends, reaching **zero at point F ($T^* = 70$ °C)**. **Pinch Identification:** The pinch is at $T^*_{pinch} = 70 \text{ °C}$, where the cascade reaches zero. Converting to actual temperatures: $$T_{h,pinch} = 70 + 10 = 80 \text{ °C}$$ $$T_{c,pinch} = 70 - 10 = 60 \text{ °C}$$ **Above the pinch** ($T^* > 70$ °C): net heat sink → external hot utility is needed (40 kW from above). **Below the pinch** ($T^* < 70$ °C): no streams present in the example, so the process is heat neutral below. **Summary of Results:** - $Q_{H,min} = 40$ kW - $Q_{C,min} = 0$ kW - Pinch (hot) = 80 °C, Pinch (cold) = 60 °C - The maximum heat recovery is 530 kW (= sum of hot stream loads minus $Q_{H,min}$). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] A refinery process has the following streams with $\Delta T_{min} = 10 \text{ °C}$: | Stream | Type | $T_s$ (°C) | $T_t$ (°C) | $CP$ (kW/K) | |---|---|---|---|---| | H1 | Hot | 180 | 60 | 2.0 | | H2 | Hot | 140 | 40 | 1.0 | | C1 | Cold | 50 | 170 | 1.5 | | C2 | Cold | 100 | 200 | 1.0 | **(a)** Develop the cascade (problem table) for the process and compute the **minimum hot and cold utility** requirements. (7 marks) **(b)** Sketch the **Grand Composite Curve** and locate the **Pinch Point** with both hot-side and cold-side temperatures. Comment on the **process integration** implication of your results. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — Cascade Construction** **Step 1: Shifted temperatures ($\Delta T_{min}/2 = 5$ °C):** $$H1: 180 \to 175; \quad 60 \to 55$$ $$H2: 140 \to 135; \quad 40 \to 35$$ $$C1: 50 \to 55; \quad 170 \to 175$$ $$C2: 100 \to 105; \quad 200 \to 205$$ **Step 2: Sorted unique shifted temperatures (descending):** $$T^* \in \{205,\ 175,\ 135,\ 105,\ 55,\ 35\}^{\circ}C$$ This yields **five intervals**. **Step 3: Active streams and net $CP$ per interval:** | Interval | $T^*$ range (°C) | $\Delta T^*$ (K) | Active Hot | Active Cold | $CP_{net}$ (kW/K) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | I | 205 – 175 | 30 | H1 | C1, C2 | $1.5+1.0-2.0 = +0.5$ | | II | 175 – 135 | 40 | H1, H2 | C1, C2 | $1.5+1.0-3.0 = -0.5$ | | III | 135 – 105 | 30 | H1, H2 | C1, C2 | $2.5-3.0 = -0.5$ | | IV | 105 – 55 | 50 | H1, H2 | C1 | $1.5-3.0 = -1.5$ | | V | 55 – 35 | 20 | H1, H2 | (none) | $-3.0$ | **Step 4: Interval enthalpies:** $$\Delta H_I = 0.5 \times 30 = 15 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_{II} = -0.5 \times 40 = -20 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_{III} = -0.5 \times 30 = -15 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_{IV} = -1.5 \times 50 = -75 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_V = -3.0 \times 20 = -60 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5: Cascade table:** | $T^*$ (°C) | $\Delta H$ (kW) | Cumulative $H$ (kW) | |---|---|---| | 205 | — | 0 | | 175 | 15 | 15 | | 135 | $-20$ | $-5$ | | 105 | $-15$ | $-20$ | | 55 | $-75$ | $-95$ | | 35 | $-60$ | $-155$ | The minimum cascade value is $-155$ kW, so: $$Q_{H,min} = 155 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 6: Adjusted cascade (add 155 to every entry):** | $T^*$ (°C) | Adjusted $H$ (kW) | |---|---| | 205 | 155 | | 175 | 170 | | 135 | 150 | | 105 | 135 | | 55 | 60 | | 35 | 0 | The bottom value of the adjusted cascade is $Q_{C,min} = 0$ kW. --- **Part (b) — GCC Plot, Pinch Identification, and Engineering Interpretation** **GCC Plot Data Points:** | Point | $T^*$ (°C) | $H$ (kW) | |---|---|---| | A | 205 | 155 | | B | 175 | 170 | | C | 135 | 150 | | D | 105 | 135 | | E | 55 | 60 | | F | 35 | 0 | **Pinch Location:** The GCC reaches zero at $T^* = 35$ °C. Converting: $$T_{h,pinch} = 35 + 5 = 40 \text{ °C}$$ $$T_{c,pinch} = 35 - 5 = 30 \text{ °C}$$ **GCC Shape Description:** The curve starts at point A (155 kW at $T^* = 205$ °C), rises slightly to point B (170 kW at $T^* = 175$ °C), then descends steadily to point F (0 kW at $T^* = 35$ °C). It is **monotonically decreasing in the lower region**, indicating a continuous net heat surplus below the highest interval. **Engineering Implications:** 1. **Above the pinch** ($T^* > 35$ °C): the process is a heat sink — external hot utility of 155 kW is mandatory. The system cannot satisfy its own heating demand internally. 2. **Below the pinch** ($T^* < 35$ °C): the process is heat neutral. The hot streams release 60 kW of internal heat that is exactly matched to cold stream demand in this range. 3. **Maximum energy recovery** = sum of hot stream loads − $Q_{H,min}$: $$Q_{hot,total} = 2.0(180-60) + 1.0(140-40) = 240 + 100 = 340 \text{ kW}$$ $$Q_{recovery} = 340 - 155 = 185 \text{ kW}$$ 4. **Process Integration Comment:** Although $Q_{C,min} = 0$ kW (no external cooling required), the hot utility demand is still significant (155 kW). To reduce this: - **Reduce $\Delta T_{min}$** in the heat exchangers (with due consideration of capital cost). - **Introduce a hot utility below the pinch** (e.g., a heat pump or waste-heat recovery from another plant section). - **Revise the cold stream data** to extract more heat from the hot streams. 5. **Placement of Utilities:** In an optimal HEN, all hot utility should be supplied **above the pinch**, and all cold utility (if any) should be supplied **below the pinch**. The pinch point (40 °C hot, 30 °C cold) acts as a strict dividing boundary. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Grand Composite Curve** - ✅ **Definition:** The GCC is the **cumulative net enthalpy** of all process streams plotted against the **shifted temperature** $T^*$. - ✅ **Two Key Shifts:** Hot streams shift **down** by $\Delta T_{min}/2$; cold streams shift **up** by $\Delta T_{min}/2$. - ✅ **Sign Convention:** Cold contributions = **positive** (deficit); hot contributions = **negative** (surplus). The most negative cascade value determines $Q_{H,min}$. - ✅ **Pinch Point:** The boundary where the cascade reaches its **minimum** value. It splits the process into a **heat sink** (above pinch) and a **heat source** (below pinch). - ✅ **Pinch Temperatures:** $T_{h,pinch} = T^*_{pinch} + \Delta T_{min}/2$ and $T_{c,pinch} = T^*_{pinch} - \Delta T_{min}/2$. - ✅ **No Cross-Pinch Rule:** In an optimal HEN, **no heat should be transferred across the pinch** — this is the foundational rule of Pinch Analysis. - ✅ **Utility Targets:** $Q_{H,min}$ is the most negative cascade value (made positive); $Q_{C,min}$ is the bottom cascade value **after** $Q_{H,min}$ is added. - ✅ **Engineering Trade-off:** Larger $\Delta T_{min}$ → smaller HEN, larger utility; smaller $\Delta T_{min}$ → larger HEN, smaller utility. Optimize via **total cost** (TAC) analysis. - ✅ **GCC vs. CC:** Composite Curves show the holistic process heat balance; GCC provides a **cleaner, more analyzable profile** for targeting. - ✅ **Common Pitfalls to Avoid in Exams:** - Forgetting to apply the temperature shift. - Mixing up which streams are hot vs. cold when summing $CP$. - Misidentifying the minimum cascade value (look at every row, not just the bottom). - Reporting only one pinch temperature (always show both hot and cold). - Skipping the "no cross-pinch" engineering interpretation in long-answer questions.

topics.title: Number of Units Targeting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Number of Units Targeting ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Number of Units Targeting** is a **Pinch Analysis** methodology that establishes the **theoretical minimum number of heat transfer units** (process-to-process exchangers, heaters, and coolers) required to achieve a target heat recovery for a given process. It is derived from **Euler's Network Theorem** applied to the topology of a Heat Exchanger Network (HEN) and is mathematically expressed as: > > $$U_{min} = N - S$$ > > where $U_{min}$ is the minimum number of units, $N$ is the total number of streams (hot + cold), and $S$ is the number of **independent subsystems** generated after pinch decomposition. This target sits between the **Energy Target** ($Q_{H,min}$ and $Q_{C,min}$) and the **Cost Target** in the classical **Onion Diagram** of process integration. While the energy target tells us "how much heat must be supplied", the units target tells us "**how many boxes/pipes**" are needed to achieve that recovery. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Delivery Van Network Imagine a logistics company with **4 delivery vans** (2 carrying hot pizzas 🍕 from the central kitchen, and 2 carrying cold ice-creams 🍦 to suburbs). The **pinch point** is the geographic location where the heat and cold demands exactly balance. To minimize the **number of delivery vehicles and transfer stations** (our "units"), the company must: - Allow free transfer between vans at intermediate hubs (process-to-process exchange). - Recognize that the network **splits into two independent zones** — north of the city center (above pinch) and south (below pinch). - Place **one cold-storage van** (hot utility) servicing all routes north, and **one refrigerated van** (cold utility) servicing all routes south. The number of transfer stations (units) is thus constrained by a topological rule, not by distance or volume. This rule is exactly **Euler's theorem**. ### 1.3 Why This Matters in KTU Context In KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, this topic is a **high-yield Module 2 question**, typically appearing as either: - A **3-mark short question** on the formula and its meaning, or - A **14-mark derivation + numerical** combining Euler's theorem with pinch decomposition. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (PECHT745 Module 2):** "Number of units targeting using Euler's network theorem — minimum number of units above and below the pinch, effect of stream splitting on units, and its place in the targeting hierarchy." ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Targeting Hierarchy (Onion Diagram Recap) Number of Units Targeting is the **second layer** in Linnhoff's hierarchical procedure: 1. **Energy Target** → How much heat to recover? 2. **Units Target** → How few heat exchangers to use? 3. **Area Target** → What is the minimum heat transfer area? 4. **Cost Target** → What is the optimum capital-energy trade-off? ### 2.2 Euler's Network Theorem — Step Logic The theorem connects three topological properties of any connected network: | Symbol | Property | HEN Meaning | |--------|----------|-------------| | $V$ | Vertices (nodes) | Stream inlet/outlet junctions = $N + 1$ | | $E$ | Edges (connections) | Heat exchanger units = $U$ | | $L$ | Independent loops | Recycles / cyclic paths | | $F$ | Faces | $L + 1$ for a planar network | Euler's planar graph identity states: $$V - E + F = 2$$ Substituting $F = L + 1$ and $V = N + 1$: $$(N + 1) - U + (L + 1) = 2$$ Solving for the number of units $U$: $$U = N + L$$ ### 2.3 The Pinch Decomposition Trick > [!IMPORTANT] > A **pinch point** divides the problem into **two thermodynamically independent subsystems** — the **Above-Pinch (cold-side problem)** and the **Below-Pinch (hot-side problem)**. No heat may flow across the pinch (it would violate the minimum driving force $\Delta T_{min}$). Each subsystem contributes one **independent "face"** that does not require a unit, so the loop count effectively *absorbs* one less per subsystem: $$U_{min} = N - S$$ where $S$ = number of subsystems (= 2 for a single-pinch problem; = $k+1$ for $k$ pinch points). ### 2.4 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below assume a Maximum Energy Recovery (MER) network with no utility streams crossing the pinch. | Formula | Meaning | Application | |---------|---------|-------------| | $U = N + L$ | General Euler count | Any HEN with $L$ loops | | $U_{min} = N - S$ | MER without utilities | Process-to-process only | | $U_{min,total} = N - S + U_{util}$ | MER with heaters/coolers | Adds hot and cold utility units | | $U_{above} = N_{above} - S_{above}$ | Units above pinch | Each side treated separately | | $U_{below} = N_{below} - S_{below}$ | Units below pinch | Each side treated separately | | $U_{util,above} = 1$ (if $N_{c,above} > 0$) | Hot utility count | Heater for cold streams | | $U_{util,below} = 1$ (if $N_{h,below} > 0$) | Cold utility count | Cooler for hot streams | | Stream-split if $\Sigma CP_{hot} \neq \Sigma CP_{cold}$ | CP-rule violation | May increase $U_{min}$ | ### 2.5 Engineering Significance - **Capital cost driver** — Every heat exchanger costs ₹₹₹. Reducing unit count directly reduces installed capital, plot area, piping, instrumentation, and maintenance cost. - **Plot-space optimisation** — Refineries and petrochemical plants are constrained by plot space; minimum-unit networks enable compact layouts. - **Operability trade-off** — Often, achieving the absolute minimum units requires **stream splitting**, which reduces operability. The KTU answer must always comment on this trade-off. ### 2.6 Stream Splitting and the CP Rule > [!WARNING] > The minimum units formula $U_{min} = N - S$ assumes **no stream splitting**. If the **CP rule** is violated (i.e., $\Sigma CP_{hot} \neq \Sigma CP_{cold}$ on one side of the pinch), one or more streams must be split, **increasing the actual number of units** beyond $U_{min}$. The actual minimum with splits: $$U_{actual} = U_{min} + (\text{number of required splits})$$ This is a frequent KTU trap — examiners award marks only when students explicitly check the CP rule on both sides of the pinch. ## 3.
