The minimum pinch typically occurs at the *highest* lean-stream supply composition *plus* the minimum driving force. Assuming $\Delta y_{min} = 50$ ppmw in equilibrium terms, $y_p = 100$ ppmw, hence $y_p^{*} = 1.5 \times 100 = 150$ ppmw.

**Step 3 — Compute Minimum MSA:**

$$L^{min} = \frac{2.00 \times 10^{-3}}{(150 - 20) \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2.00 \times 10^{-3}}{1.30 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{\,L^{min} \approx 15.38 \; \text{kg/s}\,}$$

## 3.4 Multi-MSA Extension

When $S$ candidate MSAs are available with **different supply compositions** $y_{j,s}$ and **equilibrium curves** $y_{j}^{*} = f_j(y)$, the targeting is performed *in stages*:

1. The MSA with the **largest** $(y_{j}^{*} - y_{j,s})$ per unit $L_j$ is exploited first (top of the lean composite).
2. Each MSA is used only in the composition interval where it is the *most effective* (analogous to utility placement in HEN).
3. The total $L^{min}$ is the sum of contributions in each interval.

The **Stagewise Minimum MSA Equation** is:

$$L_j^{min,stage\,k} = \frac{M_R^{cum}(y_{k+1}) - M_R^{cum}(y_k)}{y_{j}^{*}(y_k) - y_{j,s}}$$

with the total:

$$L^{min} = \sum_j L_j^{min} = \sum_{k} \frac{\Delta M_R^{(k)}}{\Delta y_{j}^{(k)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> This stagewise approach is the direct mass analog of the *Multiple Utility Targeting* procedure in HEN synthesis and frequently appears in KTU Part B numericals.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map: MSA Targeting Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Rich Streams] --> B[Identify Candidate MSAs]
    B --> C[Obtain Equilibrium Data y* vs y]
    C --> D[Plot Rich Composite Curve]
    D --> E[Plot Lean Composite Curve]
    E --> F{Determine Pinch Point}
    F --> G[Apply Vertical Gap Minimization]
    G --> H[Calculate Minimum MSA Flow]
    H --> I[Design MEN Network Above and Below Pinch]
    I --> J[Validate Network Feasibility]
    J --> K[Optimize Capital vs Operating Cost]
```

## 4.2 Mass Exchange Network Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Process["Process Sources (Rich Streams)"]
        R1[Rich Stream R1]
        R2[Rich Stream R2]
        R3[Rich Stream R3]
    end
    subgraph MEN["Mass Exchange Network"]
        MX1[Mass Exchanger Stage 1]
        MX2[Mass Exchanger Stage 2]
        MX3[Mass Exchanger Stage 3]
    end
    subgraph MSAs["Mass Separating Agents (Lean Streams)"]
        L1[Fresh MSA L1]
        L2[Fresh MSA L2]
    end
    subgraph Regen["Regeneration Section"]
        RG[Regenerator]
    end
    R1 --> MX1
    R2 --> MX2
    R3 --> MX3
    L1 --> MX1
    L2 --> MX2
    MX1 --> MX3
    MX2 --> MX3
    MX3 --> RG
    RG --> L1
    RG --> L2
```

## 4.3 Y-M Composite Curve Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Plot["Y-M Composite Curve (Conceptual)"]
        axis1[Y axis - Composition y]
        axis2[M axis - Mass Load]
        CCP[Rich Composite Curve - descending staircase]
        LCP[Lean Composite Curve - ascending staircase]
        PZ[Pinch Point - vertical gap is minimum]
    end
    CCP --- PZ
    LCP --- PZ
```

> [!NOTE]
> The actual Y-M composite curve is a *step diagram* (staircase) plotting cumulative rich mass load (descending, top-down) and cumulative lean mass load (ascending, bottom-up) against composition. The pinch is where these two staircases come closest without intersecting. This representation mirrors the T-H composite curve of HEN.

## 4.4 Multi-MSA Allocation Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Composition Range Top] --> A1[Use MSA-1 high driving force]
    A1 --> A2[Composition Range Middle]
    A2 --> A3[Use MSA-2 medium driving force]
    A3 --> A4[Composition Range Bottom]
    A4 --> A5[Use MSA-3 low driving force]
    A5 --> S2[Composition Range Minimum]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**

**Q1.** Define *Mass Separating Agent (MSA)*. Differentiate between a *rich stream* and a *lean stream* in the context of mass exchange network synthesis.

**Model Answer:**
A **Mass Separating Agent (MSA)** is a lean stream (typically a solvent, adsorbent, or extractant) that selectively accepts target solute(s) from one or more rich streams when contacted in a mass transfer device.
- **Rich stream** is a *process source*: it *gives up* the target solute (e.g., a contaminated gas stream).
- **Lean stream / MSA** is a *process sink*: it *accepts* the target solute (e.g., a fresh solvent).

> **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for rich/lean distinction)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**

**Q2.** State the significance of targeting the *Minimum Mass Separating Agent* in process integration. What graphical tool is used for this purpose?

**Model Answer:**
Targeting the **Minimum MSA** establishes a *theoretical lower bound* on MSA consumption before detailed network design, ensuring that the resulting MEN uses the *least possible solvent/adsorbent*, which directly reduces regeneration energy, fresh MSA make-up, and column/equipment size.
The graphical tool used is the **Y–M Composite Curve** (composition vs. cumulative mass load), with the **pinch point** identified via vertical gap minimization (analogous to $\Delta T_{min}$ in HEN).

> **(3 marks: 2 for significance, 1 for graphical tool)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 3, Sample Q with Internal Choice]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Single MSA Targeting

**(a)** A wastewater treatment plant has two rich streams and one candidate MSA. The data is given below:

| Stream | G or L (kg/s) | $y_{s}$ (ppm) | $y_{t}$ (ppm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | 4.0 | 500 | 50 |
| R2 | 3.0 | 400 | 100 |
| MSA (L1) | ? | 10 | — (equilibrium) |

The equilibrium relation is linear: $y^{*} = 2y$.
**(i)** Compute the total mass load to be removed. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Identify the pinch composition. **(4 marks)**
**(iii)** Calculate the minimum MSA flow rate. **(4 marks)**
**(iv)** Briefly explain what happens to $L^{min}$ if the equilibrium curve is changed to $y^{*} = 3y$. **(3 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(i) Total Mass Load:**

$$M_R^{tot} = 4.0 \times (500 - 50) \times 10^{-6} + 3.0 \times (400 - 100) \times 10^{-6}$$

$$= 4.0 \times 450 \times 10^{-6} + 3.0 \times 300 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$= 1800 \times 10^{-6} + 900 \times 10^{-6} = 2700 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$= 2.70 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

**(ii) Pinch Composition:**

The lowest rich-stream target is $y = 50$ ppm (R1). Since only one MSA is available, the pinch corresponds to this lowest target. With $y_p = 50$ ppm:

$$y_p^{*} = 2 \times 50 = 100 \text{ ppm}$$

**(iii) Minimum MSA Flow Rate:**

$$L^{min} = \frac{M_R^{tot}}{y_p^{*} - y_{s,MSA}} = \frac{2.70 \times 10^{-3}}{(100 - 10) \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= \frac{2.70 \times 10^{-3}}{90 \times 10^{-6}} = 30.0 \; \text{kg/s}$$

**(iv) Effect of Changing Equilibrium Curve:**

If $y^{*} = 3y$, then $y_p^{*} = 3 \times 50 = 150$ ppm. The driving force increases from 90 to 140 ppm, so:

$$L^{min}_{new} = \frac{2.70 \times 10^{-3}}{(150 - 10) \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2.70 \times 10^{-3}}{140 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 19.3 \text{ kg/s}$$

$L^{min}$ **decreases** because a *steeper* equilibrium curve provides a *larger driving force* at the same rich-side composition.

> **(14 marks: distributed as above)**

### Question B (14 Marks) — Multi-MSA Targeting

**(a)** A refinery has two rich streams (R1, R2) and two candidate MSAs (L1, L2). Data is given below:

| Stream | G or L (kg/s) | $y_{s}$ (ppm) | $y_{t}$ (ppm) | Equilibrium |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | 5.0 | 600 | 100 | — |
| R2 | 2.5 | 400 | 50 | — |
| L1 (MSA-1) | ? | 20 | — | $y_1^{*} = 2y$ |
| L2 (MSA-2) | ? | 5 | — | $y_2^{*} = 1.5y$ |

**(i)** Plot the rich composite curve and tabulate the cumulative mass load. **(4 marks)**
**(ii)** Determine which MSA is used in the *upper* composition range and which in the *lower* range. **(3 marks)**
**(iii)** Calculate the minimum flow rate of each MSA. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(i) Rich Composite Curve Data:**

At each $y$ value, the cumulative mass load of rich streams with $y_{i,s} \geq y$ is computed.

| $y$ (ppm) | Streams active | $M_R^{cum}(y)$ (kg/s) |
|---|---|---|
| 600 | R1 | $5.0 \times (600 - 600) \times 10^{-6} = 0$ |
| 400 | R1, R2 | $5.0 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} + 2.5 \times (400 - 400) \times 10^{-6} = 1.00 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 100 | R1, R2 | $5.0 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} + 2.5 \times 300 \times 10^{-6} = 2.50 \times 10^{-3} + 0.75 \times 10^{-3} = 3.25 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 50 | R1, R2 | $5.0 \times 550 \times 10^{-6} + 2.5 \times 350 \times 10^{-6} = 2.75 \times 10^{-3} + 0.875 \times 10^{-3} = 3.625 \times 10^{-3}$ |

**(ii) MSA Allocation:**

Compute the effective driving force at each candidate pinch:

- At $y_p = 100$ ppm: $y_1^{*} = 200$ ppm, gap = 180 ppm; $y_2^{*} = 150$ ppm, gap = 145 ppm.
- At $y_p = 50$ ppm: $y_1^{*} = 100$ ppm, gap = 80 ppm; $y_2^{*} = 75$ ppm, gap = 70 ppm.

MSA-1 (L1) provides *larger* driving force at all compositions, so it is preferentially used. However, by mass-load balance, both MSAs may be used. **MSA-1 is used in the upper range (100 to 400 ppm) and MSA-2 supplements in the lower range (50 to 100 ppm)** if total demand exceeds MSA-1 capacity. For this problem, allocate as:

- **MSA-1: $y$ from 50 to 400 ppm (lower pinch to upper kink)**
- **MSA-2: $y$ from 50 to 100 ppm (additional support at low end)**

**(iii) Minimum Flow Rate Calculation:**

**MSA-1:** Used over the entire range, mass load covered from $M_R^{cum}(50) = 3.625 \times 10^{-3}$ to $M_R^{cum}(400) = 1.00 \times 10^{-3}$:

$$L_1^{min} = \frac{3.625 \times 10^{-3} - 1.00 \times 10^{-3}}{(2 \times 50 - 20) \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= \frac{2.625 \times 10^{-3}}{80 \times 10^{-6}} = 32.8 \; \text{kg/s}$$

**MSA-2:** Used from $y = 50$ to $y = 100$ ppm:

$$L_2^{min} = \frac{3.625 \times 10^{-3} - 3.25 \times 10^{-3}}{(1.5 \times 50 - 5) \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= \frac{0.375 \times 10^{-3}}{70 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.36 \; \text{kg/s}$$

> **(14 marks: distributed as above)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units Mistake (–1 mark):** Students often forget to convert ppm to mass fraction ($10^{-6}$ factor). Always state units clearly: $M$ in kg/s, $y$ dimensionless, $L$ in kg/s.
> 2. **Wrong Pinch Identification (–2 marks):** The pinch is NOT necessarily the highest $y$ — it is where the *vertical gap* between rich and lean composites is minimum but non-negative. Test multiple compositions to confirm.
> 3. **Confusion between $y$ and $y^{*}$ (–2 marks):** $y$ is the rich-phase composition, $y^{*}$ is the *equilibrium* composition in the lean phase. The driving force is $y^{*} - y$, not the other way around.
> 4. **Forgetting the MSA Supply Composition (–1 mark):** $L^{min}$ uses $(y_p^{*} - y_{s,MSA})$, NOT $(y_p^{*} - 0)$. The MSA enters dirty (or partially loaded) at $y_{s,MSA}$.
> 5. **Multi-MSA Stagewise Mistake (–3 marks):** Do not allocate MSAs to the *whole* range blindly. Compute mass-load per interval and divide by the *interval-specific* driving force.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Minimum MSA**

- **Definition:** MSA = lean stream that *receives* solute from a rich stream. Minimum MSA = theoretical lower bound on MSA consumption.
- **Originators:** Nishida, Stephanopoulos, Westerberg (1981) — same era as pinch analysis for HEN by Linnhoff.
- **Analogy with HEN:** Hot/Cold Utility ↔ Rich/Lean Mass Load. $\Delta T_{min}$ ↔ $\Delta y_{min}$ (minimum composition driving force).
- **Key Equation (Single MSA):**
$$L^{min} = \frac{\sum_i G_i (y_{i,s} - y_{i,t})}{y_p^{*} - y_{s,MSA}}$$
- **Key Equation (Multi-MSA, stagewise):**
$$L_j^{min} = \frac{\Delta M_R^{(k)}}{\Delta y_{j}^{(k)}}$$
- **Pinch Definition:** The composition at which the vertical gap between the rich composite and the lean composite is minimum but non-negative.
- **Driving Force:** Always $y^{*} - y$ (lean equilibrium minus rich actual). Cannot be negative anywhere in the network.
- **Composite Curve Construction:**
  - **Rich:** descending staircase — $M_R^{cum}(y) = \sum G_i (y_{i,s} - y)$ for $y \leq y_{i,s}$.
  - **Lean:** ascending staircase — $M_L^{cum}(y^{*}) = \sum L_j (y_{j,t}^{*} - y_{j,s})$ for $y^{*} \geq y_{j,t}^{*}$.
- **Slope Interpretation:** The slope of the rich composite at any $y$ is the sum of $G_i$ of streams active above that $y$. The slope of the lean composite is the sum of $L_j$ of streams active below that $y^{*}$.
- **Units Convention:** Work in solute-free (carrier-free) basis. Mass load has units of kg solute per second. Compositions are mass fractions.
- **Application Hierarchy:** MSA targets come *before* MEN design (sequencing analogy: targets → design → optimization in pinch methodology).
- **Energy Linkage:** Minimum MSA is linked to minimum regeneration energy because $L^{min}$ directly scales the reboiler/duty in the regenerator column.
- **Common Errors:** Using $y_{i,t}$ instead of $y_{j,t}^{*}$; ignoring the supply composition of MSA; using mole fraction when mass fraction is required; confusing additive vs. subtractive driving force.
- **Above-Pinch Rule:** All MSA flow must be placed *above* the pinch (i.e., only MSAs with $y_{s,MSA} \leq y_p$ are feasible). Below-pinch needs are met by fresh streams or different agents.
- **Capital vs. Operating Trade-off:** Choosing a *larger* minimum driving force (artificial pinch) reduces $L^{min}$? No — it *increases* $L^{min}$ (smaller gap = more MSA needed), but reduces the *number of stages* in exchangers. This is the classic MSA vs. Stages trade-off analogous to $\Delta T_{min}$ vs. Area in HEN.
- **Multi-MSA Order:** Always try the MSA with the *largest* $(y_j^{*} - y_j,s)$ at the highest pinch first — it is the most cost-effective for the upper composition range.
- **Pinch Design Rule (post-targeting):** No mass transfer should occur *across* the pinch. Above the pinch, only MSAs are used; below, only rich streams reach their targets without further MSA addition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mass Exchange Networks for Minimum External MSA
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mass Exchange Networks for Minimum External MSA

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Mass Exchange Network (MEN)** is an integrated network of mass transfer operations (such as absorption, stripping, extraction, adsorption, leaching, or ion exchange) that systematically recovers and transfers **key components (pollutants or valuable species)** from process **rich streams** to a set of **lean streams** known as **Mass Separating Agents (MSAs)**, with the objective of meeting specified outlet composition targets at **minimum operating cost** and **minimum external resource consumption**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> - **Rich Stream (Source):** A process stream that contains an unwanted (or valuable) component to be removed. Composition decreases from supply ($y^S$) to target ($y^T$).
> - **Lean Stream / MSA (Sink):** A stream capable of accepting the key component. Composition increases from supply ($x^S$) to target ($x^T$).
> - **External MSA:** A lean stream purchased/imported from outside the process boundary (e.g., fresh water, fresh solvent, fresh adsorbent).
> - **Internal MSA:** A lean stream already existing within the process (e.g., wastewater, condensate, off-gas, spent solvent).
> - **Key Component (KC):** The specific species (pollutant or solute) being transferred through the network.
> - **Pinch Point in MEN:** The composition where the driving force (gap between rich and lean composite curves) is minimum, analogous to the thermal pinch in HEN.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of MEN as the "Mass-Transfer Twin" of Heat Exchange Network (HEN):**
>
> In a **Heat Exchange Network**, hot streams (sources) transfer heat to cold streams (sinks), and we ask: *"What is the minimum external hot/cold utility?"* The **Pinch Method** answers this by analyzing the composite curves of hot and cold streams.
>
> In a **Mass Exchange Network**, the same logic applies, but instead of temperature, we use **composition** as the driving potential:
> - **Rich streams** (analogous to *hot streams*) carry the key component.
> - **Lean streams/MSAs** (analogous to *cold streams*) absorb that component.
> - The **Pinch** now occurs in the **composition axis**, not the temperature axis.
> - The **minimum external MSA** is the mass-transfer equivalent of *minimum hot/cold utility*.

### 1.2.1 Real-World Analogy: A Sponge Network

Imagine a factory emitting soiled water (rich stream). Instead of buying huge amounts of fresh clean water (external MSA) to scrub the pollutants, the plant first uses its **already-available clean-ish process streams** (internal MSAs — like cooling tower blowdown or condensate) to absorb the pollutants. Only the **shortfall** is met by fresh external MSA. **Mass Exchange Network synthesis** finds exactly how much of that fresh water is truly needed — no more, no less.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The composition driving force in MEN is the difference between the rich stream composition and the **equilibrium composition** that the lean stream can achieve under steady contact. The minimum external MSA is highly sensitive to the **slope of the equilibrium line ($m$)**, since steeper equilibrium reduces capacity.
> - **Equilibrium slope for linear systems:** $m = y^*/x$ (dimensionless).
> - **Mass-load transferred per rich stream:** $W_i = G_i (y_i^S - y_i^T)$ measured in **kg of key component / s** (SI).

## 1.4 Classification of MSAs (Per KTU Syllabus)

| Category | Examples | Cost Implication |
|:---------|:---------|:-----------------|
| **External MSAs** | Fresh water, fresh solvent, virgin activated carbon | Purchased — to be **minimized** |
| **Internal MSAs** | Wastewater, condensate, off-gas, spent solvent streams | Already in the process — use **freely** above the pinch |
| **Interception MSAs** | Used to prevent MSAs from being polluted further | Strategic placement |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Steps of MEN Synthesis for Minimum External MSA

The systematic procedure (based on **El-Halwagi & Manousiouthakis, 1989**, foundational to the KTU syllabus) is:

1. **Identify rich process streams** — list supply composition $y^S$, target composition $y^T$, and flow rate $G$ for each.
2. **Identify candidate external MSAs** — list supply composition $x^S$ (usually pure) and maximum allowable target $x^T$ (often limited by regeneration capacity or environmental regulation).
3. **Determine mass transfer equilibrium relations** $y^* = f(x)$ for each rich-MSA pair.
4. **Construct the Composition Interval Diagram (CID)** — divide the composition axis into intervals where the set of active streams is constant.
5. **For each interval**, compute the mass-load transferred by the rich streams (sources) and that absorbed by the lean streams (sinks) including any internal MSAs.
6. **Identify the pinch** — the interval where the cumulative deficit is maximum (driving force is minimum but non-zero, $\varepsilon_{\min} > 0$).
7. **Calculate the minimum external MSA** as the cumulative external MSA needed in the intervals **above the pinch**.

## 2.2 Why the Pinch Defines the Minimum External MSA

> [!NOTE]
> **Above the Pinch (high composition region):**
> Only **internal MSAs** are allowed to flow; no external MSA may be introduced here (else it would cross the pinch and pollute the high-purity region of the lean composite curve).
>
> **Below the Pinch (low composition region):**
> External MSA is introduced to make up the absorption deficit.
>
> The pinch thus fixes the **maximum amount of internal MSA that can be regenerated/recycled** — any attempt to reduce external MSA further would require the pinch to be violated, which is thermodynamically infeasible.

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are exam-critical. Memorize the units and sign conventions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary Condition |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:------------------|:-------------------|
| 1 | Mass-load of a rich stream | $W_i = G_i \,(y_i^S - y_i^T)$ | $G_i$ = kg/s; $y$ = mass fraction; $W_i$ = kg KC/s | $y^S > y^T$ |
| 2 | Mass-load absorbed by an MSA | $W_k = L_k \,(x_k^T - x_k^S)$ | $L_k$ = kg/s of MSA; $x$ = mass fraction in MSA | $x^T > x^S$ |
| 3 | Linear equilibrium | $y^* = m \, x + b$ | $m$ = slope; $b$ = intercept | Linear systems |
| 4 | Driving force in interval $j$ | $\Delta y_j = y^{\text{rich}}_j - y^*_j$ | Dimensionless composition | $\Delta y_j \ge \varepsilon_{\min}$ |
| 5 | Minimum external MSA (above pinch) | $L_{\text{ext,min}} = \dfrac{\sum_{j \in \text{above}} W^{\text{rich}}_j - \sum_{k \in \text{int,above}} W^{\text{MSA}}_k}{x^T - x^S}$ | kg of fresh MSA/s | Strictly positive |
| 6 | Total mass balance | $\sum W^{\text{rich}}_i = \sum W^{\text{ext}}_k + \sum W^{\text{int}}_k$ | kg KC/s | Closed network |
| 7 | Composition Interval load | $\Delta W_j^{\text{net}} = \sum G_{i,j}\,\Delta y_{i,j} - \sum L_{k,j}\,\Delta x_{k,j}$ | kg KC/s per interval | Used to build CID |
| 8 | Pinch criterion | $\Delta W^{\text{net}}_{\text{above pinch}} = 0$ at $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{\min}$ | — | Defines bottleneck |
| 9 | Minimum number of units (empirical) | $N_{\min} = N_R + N_S - 1$ | $N_R$ = rich streams; $N_S$ = MSAs | Euler's network theorem |
| 10 | Operating cost of external MSA | $\text{OC} = C_{\text{MSA}} \cdot L_{\text{ext}}$ | Cost per kg/s of MSA | $C_{\text{MSA}}$ = unit cost |

> **Note on $\vert \cdot \vert$ in tables:** Per the formatting rule, the absolute-value brackets are written as `\vert` or `\mid` in raw LaTeX, e.g. $L_{\text{ext}} = \vert \sum \Delta W_j^{\text{net}} \vert \big/ (x^T - x^S)$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wastewater treatment networks** — minimize fresh water usage (water pinch).
- **VOC (Volatile Organic Compound) abatement** — minimize fresh solvent or adsorbent.
- **Hydrogen network integration** — analogous to mass exchange with hydrogen as a "MSA-like" resource.
- **Solvent recovery networks** in pharmaceutical and petrochemical plants.
- **Carbon capture networks** — minimize fresh amine solvent as the MSA.

The minimum-external-MSA target directly sets the **floor for fresh resource consumption** and the **ceiling for the waste-generation footprint** of a chemical plant.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mass-Load and the Composite Mass Transfer Curve

### 3.1.1 Starting from the Single-Stage Mass Balance

Consider a single counter-current mass transfer stage where a rich stream $G$ (carrying the key component) contacts a lean stream $L$ (the MSA). The overall mass balance on the key component (KC) is:

$$
\text{(KC in via rich)} \;-\; \text{(KC out via rich)} \;=\; \text{(KC in via lean)} \;-\; \text{(KC out via lean)}
$$

In symbols:

$$
G\,y^S - G\,y^T = L\,x^T - L\,x^S
$$

Define the **mass-load of the rich stream** and the **mass-load of the MSA** as:

$$
W_R \;=\; G\,(y^S - y^T) \qquad \text{(KC transferred away from rich)}
$$

$$
W_L \;=\; L\,(x^T - x^S) \qquad \text{(KC absorbed by MSA)}
$$

The stage balance reduces to:

$$
W_R \;=\; W_L
$$

### 3.1.2 Driving Force Constraint

Thermodynamic feasibility demands that at every composition point along the contact, the rich stream composition must exceed the lean stream's **equilibrium composition**:

$$
y \;>\; y^*(x)
$$

At the rich inlet (supply point) of the interval, the available driving force is:

$$
\Delta y^{\text{supply}} = y^S - y^*(x^S)
$$

At the rich outlet (target point) of the interval, the remaining driving force is:

$$
\Delta y^{\text{target}} = y^T - y^*(x^T)
$$

The minimum driving force in the interval, $\varepsilon_{\min}$, must be strictly positive. This is the mass-transfer equivalent of $\Delta T_{\min}$ in HEN.

### 3.1.3 Composite Curves Construction

For a set of $N_R$ rich streams and $N_S$ lean streams (MSAs), the **rich composite** is the sum of mass-loads of all rich streams active in each composition interval, plotted as $W_R$ versus composition $y$ (rich). Similarly, the **lean composite** plots $\sum W_L$ versus the corresponding $x$ (lean) axis. Shifting the lean composite to the right by $\varepsilon_{\min}/m$ (for linear equilibrium $y^* = m x$) gives the **feasible lean composite** that touches the rich composite at the pinch.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Minimum External MSA Targeting

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem statement (KTU-style):**
> A process has **two rich streams** carrying a key pollutant (component A) and **one external MSA** (fresh water) plus **one internal MSA** (process condensate). Determine the **minimum external MSA** flow rate.
>
> **Data:**
> | Stream | Type | $G$ or $L$ (kg/s) | Supply $y^S$ or $x^S$ | Target $y^T$ or $x^T$ |
> |:------|:-----|:-----------------:|:----------------------:|:----------------------:|
> | $R_1$ | Rich | $G_1 = 100$ | $y_1^S = 0.10$ | $y_1^T = 0.02$ |
> | $R_2$ | Rich | $G_2 = 200$ | $y_2^S = 0.05$ | $y_2^T = 0.005$ |
> | $S_{\text{ext}}$ | External MSA | $L_{\text{ext}}$ | $x^S = 0.000$ | $x^T = 0.015$ |
> | $S_{\text{int}}$ | Internal MSA | $L_{\text{int}} = 50$ | $x^S = 0.005$ | $x^T = 0.020$ |
>
> **Equilibrium** (linear): $y^* = 1.5\,x$ (for both rich streams, simplified).
> **Minimum composition driving force:** $\varepsilon_{\min} = 0.005$.

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Compute Mass-Load of Each Rich Stream

$$
W_{R_1} = G_1\,(y_1^S - y_1^T) = 100 \times (0.10 - 0.02) = 8.0 \;\;\text{kg A/s}
$$

$$
W_{R_2} = G_2\,(y_2^S - y_2^T) = 200 \times (0.05 - 0.005) = 9.0 \;\;\text{kg A/s}
$$

$$
W_{R,\text{total}} = 8.0 + 9.0 = 17.0 \;\;\text{kg A/s}
$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Compute Maximum Mass-Load Absorbable by Internal MSA

$$
W_{S_{\text{int}}} = L_{\text{int}}\,(x_{\text{int}}^T - x_{\text{int}}^S) = 50 \times (0.020 - 0.005) = 0.75 \;\;\text{kg A/s}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Compute Mass-Load Required from External MSA (Total)

By the overall key-component mass balance:

$$
W_{R,\text{total}} = W_{S_{\text{ext}}} + W_{S_{\text{int}}}
$$

$$
W_{S_{\text{ext}}} = 17.0 - 0.75 = 16.25 \;\;\text{kg A/s}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Convert Mass-Load to Minimum External MSA Flow Rate

$$
L_{\text{ext,min}} \;=\; \frac{W_{S_{\text{ext}}}}{x_{\text{ext}}^T - x_{\text{ext}}^S} \;=\; \frac{16.25}{0.015 - 0.000} \;=\; 1083.33 \;\;\text{kg/s of fresh water}
$$

### 3.2.5 Step 5 — Pinch Identification (Feasibility Check)

Check the driving force at each endpoint to ensure the interval is feasible:

**At rich stream $R_1$ supply ($y_1^S = 0.10$):**

$$
y^*(x = 0.000) = 1.5 \times 0.000 = 0.000
$$

$$
\Delta y = 0.10 - 0.000 = 0.100 \;\;\geq\;\; \varepsilon_{\min} = 0.005 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**At rich stream $R_2$ target ($y_2^T = 0.005$):**

The MSA at the corresponding equilibrium:

$$
x \;=\; \frac{y}{m} \;=\; \frac{0.005}{1.5} \;=\; 0.00333
$$

$$
\Delta y = 0.005 - y^*(x = 0.00333) = 0.005 - 0.005 = 0.000
$$

The driving force vanishes — this is the **pinch**. A minimum driving force $\varepsilon_{\min} = 0.005$ must be enforced by adjusting either the equilibrium slope, the MSA target composition, or accepting a slightly higher external MSA flow rate.

### 3.2.6 Step 6 — Recompute with Enforced $\varepsilon_{\min}$

Shift the lean composite curve to the right by $\varepsilon_{\min}/m$:

$$
\text{Shift} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_{\min}}{m} \;=\; \frac{0.005}{1.5} \;=\; 0.00333 \;\;\text{(in $x$-units)}
$$

The effective target composition of the external MSA becomes:

$$
x_{\text{ext,eff}}^T \;=\; 0.015 - 0.00333 \;=\; 0.01167
$$

Re-solving for the external MSA:

$$
L_{\text{ext,min}}^{\text{pinch-corrected}} \;=\; \frac{16.25}{0.01167 - 0.000} \;\approx\; 1392.86 \;\;\text{kg/s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** With a realistic minimum driving force of $\varepsilon_{\min} = 0.005$, the minimum external MSA requirement rises from **1083.33 kg/s** (idealized) to **~1392.86 kg/s** — a 28.6 % increase. This is precisely the trade-off the designer must report to management: tighter purity = more fresh MSA.

### 3.2.7 Step 7 — Final Tabulated Answer

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|:---------|:-------|:------|:-----|
| Mass-load, rich 1 | $W_{R_1}$ | 8.00 | kg A/s |
| Mass-load, rich 2 | $W_{R_2}$ | 9.00 | kg A/s |
| Total rich load | $W_{R,\text{total}}$ | 17.00 | kg A/s |
| Internal MSA load | $W_{S_{\text{int}}}$ | 0.75 | kg A/s |
| External MSA load | $W_{S_{\text{ext}}}$ | 16.25 | kg A/s |
| **Min. external MSA (pinch-corrected)** | $L_{\text{ext,min}}$ | **1392.86** | **kg/s** |
| Pinch location (in $y$) | $y_{\text{pinch}}$ | 0.0050 | mass fraction |
| Pinch location (in $x$) | $x_{\text{pinch}}$ | 0.0033 | mass fraction |
| Min. driving force | $\varepsilon_{\min}$ | 0.0050 | mass fraction |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Above vs Below the Pinch

| Region | Streams Active | External MSA Permitted? | Mass-Load Balance |
|:-------|:---------------|:------------------------|:------------------|
| **Above the pinch** (rich composition high) | All rich streams + internal MSA (no external) | **NO** — would pollute lean composite | $\sum W_R^{\text{above}} = W_{S_{\text{int}}}^{\text{above}}$ |
| **At the pinch** | Bottleneck interval | Only as required by $\varepsilon_{\min}$ | $\Delta y = \varepsilon_{\min}$ exactly |
| **Below the pinch** (rich composition low) | Remaining rich streams + external MSA | **YES** — primary supply | $\sum W_R^{\text{below}} = W_{S_{\text{ext}}}^{\text{below}}$ |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MEN Synthesis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define process streams and MSAs] --> B[List rich streams: G, yS, yT]
    A --> C[List external MSAs: L, xS, xT]
    A --> D[List internal MSAs: L, xS, xT]
    B --> E[Determine equilibrium: ystar equals f of x]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Construct Composition Interval Diagram CID]
    F --> G[Compute mass-loads in each interval]
    G --> H[Plot rich composite curve in y axis]
    G --> I[Plot lean composite curve in x axis]
    I --> J[Shift lean composite by epsilon min over m]
    J --> K{Lean touches rich?}
    K -->|No| L[Reduce epsilon and retry]
    L --> J
    K -->|Yes| M[Identify pinch point and pinch interval]
    M --> N[Above pinch: internal MSA only]
    M --> O[Below pinch: external MSA allowed]
    N --> P[Compute minimum external MSA above pinch]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Final answer: L ext min in kg per s]
    Q --> R[End: Report target to design team]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Conceptual MEN Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Rich Stream R1: G1, y1S to y1T] -->|stage 1| M1[Mass Exchanger 1]
    R2[Rich Stream R2: G2, y2S to y2T] -->|stage 2| M2[Mass Exchanger 2]
    S1[Internal MSA: Lint, xS to xT] -->|recycle| M1
    S2[External MSA: Lext, xS to xT] -->|fresh feed| M2
    M1 --> P1[Loaded internal MSA: Wint kg KC per s]
    M2 --> P2[Loaded external MSA: Wext kg KC per s]
    R1 --> O1[Purified R1 to process]
    R2 --> O2[Purified R2 to process]
    P1 --> REG[Regeneration or Discharge]
    P2 --> REG
    REG -.->|regen or treat| S1
```

## 4.3 Composition Interval Diagram (CID) Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Composition axis y in rich, x in lean] --> I1[Interval 1: y from 0.10 to 0.05]
    I1 --> I2[Interval 2: y from 0.05 to 0.02]
    I2 --> I3[Interval 3: y from 0.02 to 0.005]
    I3 --> I4[Interval 4: y below 0.005 pinch region]
    I4 --> P[Pinch at y equals 0.005]
    P --> AP[Above pinch: internal MSA only]
    P --> BP[Below pinch: external MSA supply]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the CID:** Each interval lists the active rich streams and active MSAs. The cumulative mass-load difference between rich and lean streams, integrated across intervals, gives the surplus (above) and deficit (below) — the pinch interval is where this cumulative difference peaks.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q1.** Define the term **Mass Separating Agent (MSA)**. Distinguish between **external** and **internal** MSAs with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

A **Mass Separating Agent (MSA)** is a lean stream, internal or external to the process, that selectively accepts one or more key components (such as pollutants or valuable species) from rich process streams through a mass transfer operation (e.g., absorption, extraction, adsorption).

- **External MSA:** A lean stream imported from outside the process boundary, e.g., **fresh water** used to absorb $SO_2$ from a flue gas.
- **Internal MSA:** A lean stream already available within the process, e.g., **process condensate** that can absorb residual VOCs from an off-gas.

The process-integration objective is to **maximize the use of internal MSAs** and thereby **minimize the external MSA requirement**. **[3 Marks]**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q2.** What is the **pinch point** in a Mass Exchange Network? Why is it the bottleneck for the minimum external MSA target?

**Model Answer:**

The **pinch point** in a MEN is the composition at which the driving force (gap between the rich composite curve and the feasible lean composite curve) is minimum, i.e., equal to the prescribed $\varepsilon_{\min}$. It is the mass-transfer analogue of the HEN thermal pinch.

It is the **bottleneck** because:

- **Above the pinch**, the rich stream composition is high; only the most-pure internal MSAs can absorb further. No external MSA may be introduced above the pinch without polluting the lean composite and driving the design infeasibly to the right.
- **Below the pinch**, the rich composition is low and the external MSA absorbs the remaining load.

Thus the **maximum internal MSA absorption** achievable above the pinch fixes the **minimum external MSA** that must be introduced below the pinch. **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 5.2.1 Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **pinch-analysis procedure** for synthesizing a Mass Exchange Network that meets outlet composition targets with the **minimum external MSA**. List the role of (i) rich streams, (ii) lean streams, (iii) the equilibrium relation, and (iv) the minimum composition driving force $\varepsilon_{\min}$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Pinch-analysis procedure for minimum external MSA (El-Halwagi & Manousiouthakis, 1989):**

1. **Identify rich streams (sources):** For each, record flow rate $G$, supply composition $y^S$, and target composition $y^T$.
2. **Identify lean streams / MSAs (sinks):** For each, record $L$, supply $x^S$, target $x^T$, and whether it is **internal** (already in process) or **external** (to be minimized).
3. **Determine the equilibrium relation** $y^* = f(x)$ between the rich and lean streams in the contactor (often linear: $y^* = m x + b$).
4. **Construct the Composition Interval Diagram (CID):** Sort all $y^S$, $y^T$, and equivalent $x$ values; divide the composition axis into intervals where the set of active streams is constant.
5. **For each interval**, calculate the net mass-load $\Delta W_j = \sum G_{i,j}\,\Delta y_{i,j} - \sum L_{k,j}\,\Delta x_{k,j}$.
6. **Plot rich composite curve** ($W$ vs $y$) and **lean composite curve** ($W$ vs $x$).
7. **Shift the lean composite** to the right by $\varepsilon_{\min}/m$ (linear case) to enforce the minimum driving force.
8. **Identify the pinch** — the interval where the feasible lean curve just touches the rich curve.
9. **Compute minimum external MSA** as the net load below the pinch divided by the lean-stream composition change:
$$
L_{\text{ext,min}} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{j \le \text{pinch}} \Delta W_j^{\text{below}}}{x_{\text{ext}}^T - x_{\text{ext}}^S}
$$

Roles: (i) rich streams supply the key component; (ii) MSAs absorb it; (iii) equilibrium dictates the feasible region (driving force); (iv) $\varepsilon_{\min}$ ensures thermodynamic feasibility. **[7 Marks]**

---

**(b)** A process has **two rich gas streams** and **one external liquid MSA** (water). The data are:

| Stream | $G$ or $L$ (kg/s) | Supply | Target | Equilibrium with water |
|:------|:-----------------:|:------:|:------:|:------------------------|
| $R_1$ (gas) | $G_1 = 120$ | $y_1^S = 0.080$ | $y_1^T = 0.010$ | $y^* = 1.20\,x$ |
| $R_2$ (gas) | $G_2 = 80$ | $y_2^S = 0.050$ | $y_2^T = 0.005$ | $y^* = 1.20\,x$ |
| External MSA (water) | $L_{\text{ext}}$ | $x^S = 0.000$ | $x^T = 0.020$ | — |

Take $\varepsilon_{\min} = 0.002$ (in $y$-units). Calculate the **minimum external water (MSA) flow rate** and the **pinch composition**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass-load of each rich stream:**

$$
W_{R_1} = 120 \times (0.080 - 0.010) = 120 \times 0.070 = 8.40 \;\;\text{kg KC/s}
$$

$$
W_{R_2} = 80 \times (0.050 - 0.005) = 80 \times 0.045 = 3.60 \;\;\text{kg KC/s}
$$

$$
W_{R,\text{total}} = 8.40 + 3.60 = 12.00 \;\;\text{kg KC/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Check pinch location (lowest end of $R_2$):**

At $R_2$ target $y_2^T = 0.005$, the equilibrium composition of water is:

$$
x_{\text{eq}} = \frac{y_2^T}{m} = \frac{0.005}{1.20} = 0.00417
$$

The driving force without $\varepsilon_{\min}$ enforcement is:

$$
\Delta y = y_2^T - y^*(x = 0.00417) = 0.005 - 0.005 = 0.000
$$

Thus the **pinch** is at the **$R_2$ target end**, with $y_{\text{pinch}} = 0.005$.

**Step 3 — Apply $\varepsilon_{\min}$ shift on the lean (water) composite:**

$$
\text{Shift} = \frac{\varepsilon_{\min}}{m} = \frac{0.002}{1.20} = 0.001667
$$

The effective target composition for the water becomes:

$$
x_{\text{eff}}^T = 0.020 - 0.001667 = 0.01833
$$

**Step 4 — Compute minimum external water flow rate:**

$$
L_{\text{ext,min}} = \frac{W_{R,\text{total}}}{x_{\text{eff}}^T - x^S} = \frac{12.00}{0.01833 - 0.000} = 654.55 \;\;\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Final results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|:---------|:------|
| Total rich mass-load | **12.00 kg KC/s** |
| Pinch composition (rich) | **$y_{\text{pinch}} = 0.005$** |
| Pinch composition (lean) | **$x_{\text{pinch}} = 0.00417$** |
| Effective water target | **$x_{\text{eff}}^T = 0.01833$** |
| **Minimum external water (MSA)** | **$\boxed{L_{\text{ext,min}} = 654.55 \;\text{kg/s}}$** |

**[7 Marks]**

---

### 5.2.2 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks — Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **Composition Interval Diagram (CID)** method for targeting the **minimum external MSA**. How does the CID differ from the heat-cascade used in HEN pinch analysis? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Composition Interval Diagram (CID)** is a tabular/numerical representation of the composite mass transfer problem in MEN synthesis. The procedure is:

1. Sort all supply and target compositions (both $y$ and equivalent $x$) in descending order.
2. Define intervals between successive composition values.
3. For each interval $j$, sum the mass-loads of all **rich streams** active in that interval:
$$
\Delta W_R^{\text{net}}(j) = \sum_{i \in \text{rich, active in } j} G_i\,\Delta y_{i,j}
$$
4. Sum the mass-loads of all **MSAs** active in the interval:
$$
\Delta W_L^{\text{net}}(j) = \sum_{k \in \text{MSA, active in } j} L_k\,\Delta x_{k,j}
$$
5. The **net mass-load** in each interval is:
$$
\Delta W_{\text{net}}(j) = \Delta W_R^{\text{net}}(j) - \Delta W_L^{\text{net}}(j)
$$
6. Integrate from the highest composition downwards; the **interval where the cumulative load peaks** is the **pinch**.
7. The **minimum external MSA** is the cumulative net load **below the pinch** divided by the lean-stream composition change.

**Difference from HEN heat-cascade:**

| Aspect | HEN Heat-Cascade | MEN Composition Interval Diagram |
|:-------|:-----------------|:--------------------------------|
| Axis | Temperature $T$ (K) | Composition $y$, $x$ (mass fraction) |
| Source potential | $T^S$ (supply) | $y^S$ (rich supply) |
| Sink potential | $T^T$ (target) | $x^T$ (MSA target) |
| Driving force | $\Delta T_{\min}$ | $\varepsilon_{\min}$ (composition gap) |
| Cascade quantity | Heat $Q$ (kW) | Mass-load $W$ (kg KC/s) |
| Feasibility constraint | $\Delta T \ge \Delta T_{\min}$ | $y - y^*(x) \ge \varepsilon_{\min}$ |

The CID is the **mass-transfer analogue of the HEN problem table algorithm** and is treated using the same cascade-from-top (or bottom) approach, with the pinch pinpointed at the interval of zero (or maximum) cumulative net load. **[7 Marks]**

---

**(b)** A refinery has **three rich process streams** and an **external solvent MSA**. Compute the **minimum external solvent flow rate** and the **pinch composition**. **[7 Marks]**

**Data:**

| Stream | $G$ or $L$ (kg/s) | Supply | Target | Equilibrium with solvent |
|:------|:-----------------:|:------:|:------:|:------------------------|
| $R_1$ | $G_1 = 150$ | $y_1^S = 0.12$ | $y_1^T = 0.02$ | $y^* = 0.80\,x$ |
| $R_2$ | $G_2 = 100$ | $y_2^S = 0.06$ | $y_2^T = 0.01$ | $y^* = 0.80\,x$ |
| $R_3$ | $G_3 = 200$ | $y_3^S = 0.04$ | $y_3^T = 0.005$ | $y^* = 0.80\,x$ |
| External MSA | $L_{\text{ext}}$ | $x^S = 0$ | $x^T = 0.025$ | — |

Take $\varepsilon_{\min} = 0.003$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass-loads:**

$$
W_{R_1} = 150 \times (0.12 - 0.02) = 15.00 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

$$
W_{R_2} = 100 \times (0.06 - 0.01) = 5.00 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

$$
W_{R_3} = 200 \times (0.04 - 0.005) = 7.00 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

$$
W_{R,\text{total}} = 15.00 + 5.00 + 7.00 = 27.00 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify pinch candidate (lowest target = tightest constraint):**

The smallest target is $R_3$ with $y_3^T = 0.005$. Equilibrium composition of solvent:

$$
x_{\text{eq}} = \frac{y_3^T}{m} = \frac{0.005}{0.80} = 0.00625
$$

Driving force:

$$
\Delta y = y_3^T - y^*(x=0.00625) = 0.005 - 0.005 = 0.000
$$

So the **pinch** is at $y_{\text{pinch}} = 0.005$ (on the rich axis), $x_{\text{pinch}} = 0.00625$ (on the lean axis).

**Step 3 — Apply $\varepsilon_{\min}$ shift:**

$$
\text{Shift} = \frac{\varepsilon_{\min}}{m} = \frac{0.003}{0.80} = 0.00375
$$

$$
x_{\text{eff}}^T = 0.025 - 0.00375 = 0.02125
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum external solvent:**

$$
L_{\text{ext,min}} = \frac{27.00}{0.02125 - 0} = 1270.59 \;\text{kg/s of solvent}
$$

**Final answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|:---------|:------|
| Total rich load | 27.00 kg KC/s |
| Pinch ($y$-axis) | 0.0050 |
| Pinch ($x$-axis) | 0.00625 |
| Effective MSA target | 0.02125 |
| **Minimum external solvent** | **$\boxed{L_{\text{ext,min}} \approx 1270.59 \;\text{kg/s}}$** |

**[7 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in MEN Minimum External MSA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\varepsilon_{\min}$ shift.** A surprising number of students compute $L_{\text{ext,min}}$ using the raw $x^T$ instead of $x_{\text{eff}}^T = x^T - \varepsilon_{\min}/m$. The correct, **thermodynamically feasible** value is **larger** than the naive value. The examiner deducts up to **2 marks** for this omission.
>
> 2. **Sign error in mass balance.** Remember: rich streams **lose** the key component ($y^S > y^T$), MSAs **gain** it ($x^T > x^S$). Mixing the sign gives a negative or impossibly small $L_{\text{ext,min}}$.
>
> 3. **Confusing the equilibrium slope direction.** For gas absorption into a liquid, $y^* = m x$ where $m$ can be $>1$ or $<1$. Forgetting to invert $m$ when converting between the rich ($y$) and lean ($x$) axes loses up to **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the pinch completely.** Some students use the *total* mass balance without identifying the pinch. The minimum external MSA in a true MEN is **not** a single mass balance — it is determined by the **pinch interval**. The examiner awards at most 3 out of 7 if the pinch is missing.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** Always report $L_{\text{ext,min}}$ in **kg/s** or **kg/h** consistently with the flow rates given.
>
> 6. **Mixing up "external" vs "internal" MSAs.** Internal MSAs can be used freely above the pinch; external MSAs cannot. Confusing them leads to over-designed networks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Mass Exchange Networks for Minimum External MSA**
>
> - **MEN** = integrated network of mass transfer operations (absorption, stripping, extraction, adsorption, leaching, ion exchange) that transfers a key component from rich streams to lean streams (MSAs).
> - **Rich stream (source):** $y^S \to y^T$, with $y^S > y^T$.
> - **Lean stream / MSA (sink):** $x^S \to x^T$, with $x^T > x^S$.
> - **External MSA:** imported, must be **minimized**.
> - **Internal MSA:** already in process, used **freely above the pinch**.
> - **Equilibrium relation:** $y^* = m x + b$ (linear) or $y^* = f(x)$ (non-linear).
> - **Driving force:** $\Delta y = y - y^*(x) \ge \varepsilon_{\min}$.
> - **Pinch** = the composition where $\Delta y$ is minimum (typically at one of the rich stream target compositions).
> - **CID (Composition Interval Diagram):** divides the composition axis into intervals with constant active streams; cumulative net load is computed from top.
> - **Pinch-shift correction:** $x_{\text{eff}}^T = x^T - \varepsilon_{\min}/m$ for linear equilibrium.
> - **Minimum external MSA formula:**
$$
L_{\text{ext,min}} = \frac{\sum_{j \in \text{below pinch}} \Delta W_j^{\text{net}}}{x_{\text{ext}}^T - x^S}
$$
> - **Above-pinch rule:** Only internal MSAs are used; **no** external MSA is permitted above the pinch (would cross the pinch and violate feasibility).
> - **Below-pinch rule:** External MSA supplies the residual absorption load.
> - **Network minimum units (empirical):** $N_{\min} = N_R + N_S - 1$ (Euler's theorem).
> - **Analogy with HEN:** Temperature $\leftrightarrow$ Composition; $\Delta T_{\min} \leftrightarrow \varepsilon_{\min}$; hot utility $\leftrightarrow$ external MSA; composite curves $\leftrightarrow$ rich/lean composite curves; problem table $\leftrightarrow$ CID.
> - **Key takeaway:** The pinch is the **bottleneck**; relaxing $\varepsilon_{\min}$ reduces $L_{\text{ext,min}}$ at the cost of larger mass-transfer equipment; tightening $\varepsilon_{\min}$ increases $L_{\text{ext,min}}$ but reduces exchanger size — a classic **operating-vs-capital trade-off**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water System Design: Water Use
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Water System Design: Water Use # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the framework of **Process Integration** (specifically **Water Pinch Analysis** pioneered by Wang & Smith, 1994), **Water System Design: Water Use** refers to the systematic methodology of characterizing industrial water-consuming operations to establish the *minimum theoretical freshwater demand* and *minimum wastewater generation* of a process plant. It is founded on the principle that water is a **mass-transfer agent**, not a consumer — its role is to *pick up contaminants* from process streams up to a maximum allowable limit. A **water-using operation** (WUO) is any unit operation where water enters, contacts a process stream, and leaves carrying an increased contaminant load. For each WUO, three parameters are mandatory: 1. **Maximum inlet concentration** $C_{in}^{max}$ — the highest contaminant concentration the operation can tolerate at the water inlet (driven by equilibrium, solubility, or product purity constraints). 2. **Maximum outlet concentration** $C_{out}^{max}$ — the highest contaminant concentration permitted in the discharged water (driven by downstream reuse, corrosion, fouling, or environmental discharge limits). 3. **Mass load transferred** $\Delta m$ (kg/h) — the fixed quantity of contaminant the operation must remove from the process stream per unit time. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT745 Module 3):** > The student must be able to identify water sources, sinks, and determine **limiting water flowrates** ($L_{min}$) for each water-using operation. This is the *first* step in any water network pinch problem. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of Water as a "Dirty Laundry Truck" 🚛** > - A laundry truck (water stream) arrives **clean** (low concentration $C_{in}$) and leaves **dirty** (high concentration $C_{out}$). > - The truck has a **size limit** — you cannot make it infinitely big; so to carry more dirt (mass load $\Delta m$), the dirt must be **concentrated** more efficiently. > - The factory rule ($C_{out}^{max}$) says: *the dirt in the truck cannot exceed a certain concentration*, otherwise it spills out and contaminates the environment. > - The product rule ($C_{in}^{max}$) says: *if the truck arrives too dirty, it cannot accept more dirt without violating product purity*. > - Therefore, to carry a **fixed amount of dirt** ($\Delta m$) at the **highest allowable dirtiness level** ($C_{out}^{max}$) starting from the **cleanest allowable starting state** ($C_{in}^{max}$), you need a **minimum truck size** (limiting water flowrate $L_{min}$). In short: **water flowrate is dictated by the "carrying capacity" between two concentration limits for a given mass load.** ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - Standard concentration unit for water pinch: **ppm (parts per million)** or **mg/kg** — equivalent for dilute aqueous systems. - Standard flowrate unit: **kg/s** or **t/h** (mass-based preferred over volumetric for temperature independence). - Standard mass-load unit: **kg/h** or **kg/s** of contaminant. - Default reference for pure water inlet: **0 ppm** (uncontaminated freshwater). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Water-Using Operations (WUOs) Water-using operations in chemical plants fall into two broad categories: ### A. Mass-Transfer-Based Operations Water physically contacts a process stream and absorbs/desorbs contaminants. | Operation Type | Mechanism | Typical Contaminants | |---|---|---| | **Gas Absorption / Scrubbing** | Gas → Liquid transfer | H₂S, CO₂, SO₂, VOCs | | **Liquid–Liquid Extraction** | Solute partition between solvents | Organic acids, phenols | | **Stripping / Desorption** | Liquid → Gas transfer | NH₃, H₂S | | **Leaching / Washing** | Solid → Liquid dissolution | Salts, sugars, ores | | **Reaction with water (quench, hydrolysis)** | Water participates chemically | Acids, alkalis | ### B. Non-Mass-Transfer Operations Water does **not** pick up contaminants from a process stream but may still have quality constraints. | Operation Type | Constraint | |---|---| | Cooling tower make-up | TDS, hardness, silica limits | | Boiler feedwater | Hardness, dissolved O₂, silica | | General utility / cleaning | Mostly $C_{in}^{max}$ constraint only | ## 2.2 The Three Building Blocks of Any WUO For *every* water-using operation, the following data triad is fixed by the process: 1. **Mass Load** $\Delta m$ (kg/h): Determined by process chemistry — *the operation MUST remove this much contaminant per unit time*. 2. **Maximum Inlet Concentration** $C_{in}^{max}$ (ppm): Determined by *equilibrium*, *solubility*, *product purity*, or *equipment integrity*. 3. **Maximum Outlet Concentration** $C_{out}^{max}$ (ppm): Determined by *downstream reuse compatibility*, *corrosion limit*, or *environmental discharge*. > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Concept:** $C_{in}^{max}$ and $C_{out}^{max}$ are *opposing limits*. $C_{out}^{max}$ sets how much dirt the water can carry out; $C_{in}^{max}$ sets how much dirt the water can already have coming in. The *driving force* for mass transfer is $(C_{out} - C_{in})$ — but for **limiting flowrate** we always use the *maximum allowable driving force*: $(C_{out}^{max} - C_{in}^{max})$. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta m = L \cdot (C_{out} - C_{in})$ | Mass balance on contaminant across WUO | kg/h | | 2 | $L_{min} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{C_{out}^{max} - C_{in}^{max}}$ | Limiting (minimum) water flowrate for a WUO | kg/h | | 3 | $C_{out}^{max} = C_{in}^{max} + \dfrac{\Delta m}{L_{min}}$ | Rearranged form to back-calculate outlet | ppm | | 4 | $\Delta m_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta m_i$ | Total contaminant load of the plant | kg/h | | 5 | $L_{freshwater,min} = \dfrac{\Delta m_{total}}{C_{out,global}^{max} - 0}$ | Minimum freshwater if no reuse | kg/h | | 6 | $F_{waste,max} = L_{waste} \cdot C_{out,waste}^{max}$ | Maximum waste contaminant discharge | kg/h | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vertical pipe rule for tables:** When writing absolute values or magnitudes in exam answers, use $\lvert x \rvert$ or "mod x" notation, **never** the bare pipe `|` symbol, to avoid markdown rendering errors. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters - **Water Scarcity**: In process hubs like Kerala's industrial belts (Cochin, Palakkad), freshwater cost is rising. KTU projects routinely involve targeting 30–60% reduction in freshwater intake. - **Effluent Treatment Cost (ETC)**: Wastewater treatment scales with **both** flowrate and concentration. Minimizing $L_{min}$ directly reduces capex. - **Regulatory Compliance**: Kerala State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB) norms mandate discharge concentrations below threshold — $C_{out}^{max}$ is often set by regulation. - **Energy Pinch Linkage**: Reducing hot/cold utility water also reduces the heating/cooling duty in the heat exchanger network (HEN). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Limiting Water Flowrate $L_{min}$ from Mass Balance ### Starting Premise For a single water-using operation (WUO) operating at steady state, the contaminant mass balance is: $$ \text{Accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} $$ At steady state, Accumulation = 0, and no chemical reaction occurs in the water phase (the contaminant is merely *picked up*, not generated). So: $$ 0 = L \cdot C_{in} + 0 - L \cdot C_{out} + \Delta m_{pickup} $$ > **Step 1 — Write the mass balance over the WUO:** > > $$L \cdot C_{out} = L \cdot C_{in} + \Delta m$$ > > where $L$ = water mass flowrate (kg/h), $C_{in}$ / $C_{out}$ = inlet/outlet contaminant concentrations (ppm = mg/kg), and $\Delta m$ = contaminant mass load picked up by water per unit time (kg/h). > **Step 2 — Factor out $L$:** > > $$L \cdot (C_{out} - C_{in}) = \Delta m$$ > **Step 3 — Solve for $L$:** > > $$L = \dfrac{\Delta m}{C_{out} - C_{in}}$$ > > This is the *general* water flowrate for *any* chosen inlet/outlet concentrations. > **Step 4 — Apply the maximum allowable driving force to find $L_{min}$:** > The operation will work for *any* $C_{out} \leq C_{out}^{max}$ and $C_{in} \geq C_{in}^{max}$ (note reverse inequalities — operation cannot start too dirty and cannot exit too concentrated). To **minimize** $L$, the *driving force* $(C_{out} - C_{in})$ must be **maximized**. Therefore, plug in the *maximum allowable* concentrations: > > $$\boxed{\,L_{min} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{C_{out}^{max} - C_{in}^{max}}\,}$$ > **Step 5 — Sanity check on limiting case:** If $C_{in}^{max} = 0$ (freshwater, no reuse allowed) and $C_{out}^{max}$ is at discharge limit, $L_{min}$ is largest (worst case). If $C_{in}^{max}$ rises (reuse permitted), $L_{min}$ shrinks — confirming that **reuse reduces freshwater demand**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Limiting Water Profile (Composite Curve Concept) When multiple WUOs exist, each has its own $(C_{in}^{max}, C_{out}^{max}, \Delta m, L_{min})$. Plotting all of them on a **Concentration vs Mass Load (C-M)** diagram produces the **Limiting Composite Curve**. ### Procedure (Board Exam Step-by-Step) **Step 1.** List all WUOs with their $\Delta m$, $C_{in}^{max}$, $C_{out}^{max}$. **Step 2.** Order WUOs by *increasing* $C_{out}^{max}$ (i.e., the *strictest exit* first). **Step 3.** Construct a table with cumulative mass load: $$ M_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \Delta m_i $$ **Step 4.** For each WUO, calculate $L_{min,i}$. **Step 5.** Plot $C$ (y-axis) vs $M$ (x-axis). Each WUO becomes a *horizontal segment* at $C_{out}^{max}$ spanning a horizontal width $\Delta m_i$. **Step 6.** Connect the right-end of each segment vertically down (mass-balance continuity) to the $C_{in}^{max}$ of the *next* WUO. These vertical connectors represent **mixing points** in a reuse network. **Step 7.** The resulting composite curve is the **Limiting Composite Curve**. Its slope at any vertical line = the cumulative $L$ in that region. ## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Board Style) ### Problem A process has **three** water-using operations with the following limiting data: | WUO | $\Delta m$ (kg/h) | $C_{in}^{max}$ (ppm) | $C_{out}^{max}$ (ppm) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 4.0 | 0 | 200 | | 2 | 3.0 | 50 | 100 | | 3 | 5.0 | 100 | 400 | **Task:** Calculate $L_{min,i}$ for each WUO, the cumulative mass load, and the limiting composite curve vertices. ### Step-by-Step Solution > **Step 1 — Calculate $L_{min}$ for each WUO** (applying $L_{min} = \Delta m / (C_{out}^{max} - C_{in}^{max})$): > > $$\begin{aligned} > L_{min,1} &= \dfrac{4.0}{200 - 0} = \dfrac{4.0}{200} = 0.0200 \ \text{kg water/kg load} \quad \Rightarrow \text{for } 4 \text{ kg/h: } 80{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \\ > L_{min,2} &= \dfrac{3.0}{100 - 50} = \dfrac{3.0}{50} = 0.0600 \quad \Rightarrow 50{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \\ > L_{min,3} &= \dfrac{5.0}{400 - 100} = \dfrac{5.0}{300} = 0.01667 \quad \Rightarrow 125{,}000 \text{ kg/h} > \end{aligned}$$ > **Step 2 — Order WUOs by increasing $C_{out}^{max}$:** > > WUO 2 ($C_{out}=100$) → WUO 1 ($C_{out}=200$) → WUO 3 ($C_{out}=400$) > **Step 3 — Build the cumulative mass-load table:** > > $$\begin{aligned} > M_0 &= 0 \ \text{kg/h} \quad (\text{freshwater inlet}) \\ > M_1 &= 0 + 3.0 = 3.0 \ \text{kg/h} \quad (\text{after WUO 2}) \\ > M_2 &= 3.0 + 4.0 = 7.0 \ \text{kg/h} \quad (\text{after WUO 1}) \\ > M_3 &= 7.0 + 5.0 = 12.0 \ \text{kg/h} \quad (\text{after WUO 3}) > \end{aligned}$$ > **Step 4 — Build the C-M diagram vertices (concentration at each mass-load point):** > > $$\begin{aligned} > \text{Point A: } &(M=0, \ C=0) && \text{Freshwater enters} \\ > \text{Point B: } &(M=0, \ C=C_{in,2}^{max}=50) && \text{Rise to WUO 2 inlet} \\ > \text{Point C: } &(M=3.0, \ C=C_{out,2}^{max}=100) && \text{WUO 2 exit} \\ > \text{Point D: } &(M=3.0, \ C=C_{in,1}^{max}=0) && \text{Drop to WUO 1 inlet} \\ > \text{Point E: } &(M=7.0, \ C=C_{out,1}^{max}=200) && \text{WUO 1 exit} \\ > \text{Point F: } &(M=7.0, \ C=C_{in,3}^{max}=100) && \text{Drop to WUO 3 inlet} \\ > \text{Point G: } &(M=12.0, \ C=C_{out,3}^{max}=400) && \text{Final wastewater} > \end{aligned}$$ > **Step 5 — Total plant freshwater without reuse:** > > $$L_{fresh} = \sum L_{min,i} = 80{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 125{,}000 = 255{,}000 \ \text{kg/h}$$ > **Step 6 — Verification of total load:** > > $$\Delta m_{total} = 4.0 + 3.0 + 5.0 = 12.0 \ \text{kg/h} \quad \checkmark$$ The **limiting composite curve** is plotted from the vertices A→B→C→D→E→F→G. The *minimum freshwater target* is read off as the slope of the *lowest translated* supply line touching the composite curve at the **water pinch**. ## 3.4 Inlet vs Outlet Concentration — The "Reverse Inequality" Pitfall > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Exam Trap:** Students confuse $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ as both increasing. They are **inlet** and **outlet** of the *water* stream. For an operation that **picks up** contaminant: > > - $C_{in} \leq C_{in}^{max}$ (water entering — *cannot* be dirtier than the limit, hence the upper bound). > - $C_{out} \leq C_{out}^{max}$ (water leaving — *cannot* be dirtier than the limit, hence the upper bound). > > Numerically, $C_{out} > C_{in}$ *always* (water picks up dirt). But both are *bounded above*. Writing "$C_{in}^{max}$ = 200" when you mean $C_{out}^{max}$ is the single most common error in KTU valuation. # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: Define the term "limiting water flowrate" for a water-using operation. List the three parameters required to compute it.** **Model Answer:** The *limiting water flowrate* ($L_{min}$) is the **minimum theoretical water flowrate** required by a water-using operation when operated at the **maximum allowable inlet and outlet contaminant concentrations**. It represents the absolute minimum water demand if the operation is pushed to its thermodynamic/process limits. The three required parameters are: 1. **Maximum inlet concentration** $C_{in}^{max}$ (ppm) 2. **Maximum outlet concentration** $C_{out}^{max}$ (ppm) 3. **Mass load transferred** $\Delta m$ (kg/h) The formula is: $$ L_{min} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{C_{out}^{max} - C_{in}^{max}} $$ (3 marks — definition 1 mark, list 1 mark, formula 1 mark) ### Question A2 — Application `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: A scrubber removes 2 kg/h of H₂S from a natural gas stream. The water inlet must contain at most 20 ppm H₂S and the outlet at most 250 ppm. Calculate the limiting water flowrate.** **Model Answer:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{min} &= \dfrac{\Delta m}{C_{out}^{max} - C_{in}^{max}} \\ &= \dfrac{2 \ \text{kg/h}}{(250 - 20) \ \text{ppm}} \\ &= \dfrac{2}{230} \\ &= 0.008696 \ \text{kg water / kg contaminant} \end{aligned} $$ For a contaminant load of 2 kg/h: $$ L_{min} = \dfrac{2}{250 - 20} \times 10^{6} \ \text{ppm}^{-1} \cdot (\text{kg/h}) $$ Wait — the units require care: $C$ is in **mg/kg = ppm**, $\Delta m$ is in **kg/h**, so $L$ is in **kg water/h**: $$ L_{min} = \dfrac{2 \ \text{kg/h}}{(250 - 20) \times 10^{-6} \ \text{kg contaminant / kg water}} = \dfrac{2}{230 \times 10^{-6}} = 8{,}695.65 \ \text{kg/h} \approx 8.7 \ \text{t/h} $$ (3 marks — 1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution with unit conversion, 1 mark final answer with units) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Regulation 2024:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. Full internal choice means you may attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **A process plant has four water-using operations with the following limiting data:** | WUO | $\Delta m$ (kg/h) | $C_{in}^{max}$ (ppm) | $C_{out}^{max}$ (ppm) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 6.0 | 0 | 250 | | 2 | 4.5 | 50 | 150 | | 3 | 8.0 | 100 | 350 | | 4 | 3.5 | 200 | 450 | #### Part (a) — [7 Marks] **(i)** Calculate the limiting water flowrate $L_{min}$ for **each** operation. **(ii)** Arrange the operations in increasing order of $C_{out}^{max}$ and compute the cumulative mass load $M_k$ at each stage. #### Part (b) — [7 Marks] **(i)** Determine the **minimum freshwater requirement** for the plant assuming no reuse (i.e., each operation receives freshwater directly). **(ii)** If perfect reuse is permitted (subject only to $C_{in}^{max}$ constraints), what is the **theoretical minimum freshwater flowrate** using the limiting composite curve? **(iii)** Comment on the percentage reduction in freshwater demand achievable. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **A solvent extraction plant uses water to wash an organic stream. Two water-using operations exist:** | WUO | $\Delta m$ (kg/h) | $C_{in}^{max}$ (ppm) | $C_{out}^{max}$ (ppm) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 5.0 | 0 | 100 | | 2 | 7.5 | 50 | 300 | #### Part (a) — [7 Marks] **(i)** Write the general contaminant mass balance over a single WUO and derive the limiting water flowrate formula. **(ii)** Calculate $L_{min}$ for both WUOs. #### Part (b) — [7 Marks] **(i)** Construct the **limiting composite curve** by computing all C-M diagram vertices. **(ii)** Identify the **water pinch concentration** and justify whether direct freshwater reuse is feasible. --- ### 📘 Complete Model Solution — Question A #### Solution to A(a): > **Step A1 — Compute $L_{min,i}$ for each WUO using** $L_{min} = \Delta m / (C_{out}^{max} - C_{in}^{max})$: > > $$\begin{aligned} > L_{min,1} &= \dfrac{6.0}{250 - 0} \times 10^{6} = \dfrac{6.0}{250 \times 10^{-6}} = 24{,}000 \ \text{kg/h} \\ > L_{min,2} &= \dfrac{4.5}{150 - 50} \times 10^{6} = \dfrac{4.5}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 45{,}000 \ \text{kg/h} \\ > L_{min,3} &= \dfrac{8.0}{350 - 100} \times 10^{6} = \dfrac{8.0}{250 \times 10^{-6}} = 32{,}000 \ \text{kg/h} \\ > L_{min,4} &= \dfrac{3.5}{450 - 200} \times 10^{6} = \dfrac{3.5}{250 \times 10^{-6}} = 14{,}000 \ \text{kg/h} > \end{aligned}$$ > **Step A2 — Order operations by increasing $C_{out}^{max}$:** > > WUO 1 (250) ← WUO 2 (150) ← WUO 4 (450) ← WUO 3 (350) > > Correct ascending order: > > WUO 2 (150) → WUO 1 (250) → WUO 3 (350) → WUO 4 (450) > **Step A3 — Cumulative mass load table:** > > $$\begin{aligned} > M_0 &= 0 \\ > M_1 &= 0 + 4.5 = 4.5 \ \text{kg/h (after WUO 2)} \\ > M_2 &= 4.5 + 6.0 = 10.5 \ \text{kg/h (after WUO 1)} \\ > M_3 &= 10.5 + 8.0 = 18.5 \ \text{kg/h (after WUO 3)} \\ > M_4 &= 18.5 + 3.5 = 22.0 \ \text{kg/h (after WUO 4)} > \end{aligned}$$ (7 marks complete) #### Solution to A(b): > **Step B1 — Total freshwater without reuse:** > > $$L_{fresh,no\,reuse} = \sum L_{min,i} = 24{,}000 + 45{,}000 + 32{,}000 + 14{,}000 = 115{,}000 \ \text{kg/h}$$ > **Step B2 — Total contaminant load:** > > $$\Delta m_{total} = 6.0 + 4.5 + 8.0 + 3.5 = 22.0 \ \text{kg/h}$$ > **Step B3 — Minimum freshwater with reuse (limiting composite curve approach):** > The limiting composite curve has its **highest concentration at the final waste stream** ($C_{out,4}^{max} = 450$ ppm) and **freshwater inlet at 0 ppm**. The pinch corresponds to the minimum slope supply line that just touches the composite curve without crossing it. From the C-M vertices calculated in the example above, the minimum freshwater target is the slope of the supply line from $(M=0, C=0)$ to the lowest point on the composite curve where it can sit tangent. > > At the pinch, the freshwater slope is approximately: > > $$L_{fresh,min} \approx \dfrac{\Delta m_{total}}{C_{out,final}^{max}} = \dfrac{22.0}{450 \times 10^{-6}} = 48{,}889 \ \text{kg/h}$$ > **Step B4 — Percentage reduction:** > > $$\% \text{reduction} = \dfrac{115{,}000 - 48{,}889}{115{,}000} \times 100 \approx 57.5\%$$ (7 marks complete) ### 📘 Complete Model Solution — Question B (Alternative) #### Solution to B(a): > **Step 1 — Mass balance derivation** (as shown in Section 3.1 above) — **3 marks** > > **Step 2 — Calculate $L_{min}$ for both WUOs:** > > $$\begin{aligned} > L_{min,1} &= \dfrac{5.0}{100 - 0} \times 10^{6} = \dfrac{5.0}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 50{,}000 \ \text{kg/h} \\ > L_{min,2} &= \dfrac{7.5}{300 - 50} \times 10^{6} = \dfrac{7.5}{250 \times 10^{-6}} = 30{,}000 \ \text{kg/h} > \end{aligned}$$ (7 marks — derivation 3, calculation 4) #### Solution to B(b): > **Step 1 — Order by increasing $C_{out}^{max}$:** > WUO 1 (100 ppm) → WUO 2 (300 ppm) > **Step 2 — Build C-M vertices:** > > $$\begin{aligned} > \text{Point A: } &(0, 0) \\ > \text{Point B: } &(0, C_{in,1}^{max} = 0) \\ > \text{Point C: } &(5.0, C_{out,1}^{max} = 100) \\ > \text{Point D: } &(5.0, C_{in,2}^{max} = 50) \\ > \text{Point E: } &(12.5, C_{out,2}^{max} = 300) > \end{aligned}$$ > **Step 3 — Pinch identification:** The pinch lies between points D and C (concentrations overlap: 50 ≤ 100), so the exit of WUO 1 (100 ppm) can be reused as inlet to WUO 2 (max 50 ppm) only with **freshwater dilution**. The minimum freshwater target is: > > $$L_{fresh,min} = \dfrac{12.5}{300 \times 10^{-6}} = 41{,}667 \ \text{kg/h}$$ > **Step 4 — Direct reuse feasibility:** Not fully feasible because $C_{out,1}^{max}$ (100 ppm) > $C_{in,2}^{max}$ (50 ppm); partial reuse with bypass is required. (7 marks — vertices 3, pinch 2, comment 2) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Conversion on ppm:** 1 ppm = 1 mg/kg = $10^{-6}$ kg/kg. Forgetting this gives answers off by a factor of $10^6$. Always show the unit conversion step. > 2. **Mixing vs Reuse confusion:** Mixing two streams reduces concentration — this is the **whole point** of reuse. State the mixing equation $L_1 C_1 + L_2 C_2 = (L_1 + L_2) C_{mix}$ explicitly. > 3. **Ordering by $C_{out}^{max}$ vs $C_{in}^{max}$:** Must sort by **outlet** concentration for the composite curve. > 4. **Negative driving force:** If $C_{out}^{max} \leq C_{in}^{max}$, the WUO is infeasible — flag this in your answer (rare but a board favorite). > 5. **Missing the vertical drop:** In the C-M diagram, the connection from one WUO outlet to the next inlet is a **vertical line at constant M** — students often draw a diagonal and lose 1–2 marks. ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember ⚡ > **Quick-Fire Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam)** - **Water pinch goal:** Minimize freshwater $L_f$ AND wastewater $L_w$ simultaneously. - **Limiting flowrate formula:** $L_{min} = \Delta m / (C_{out}^{max} - C_{in}^{max})$. Memorize. - **Three mandatory WUO data points:** $\Delta m$, $C_{in}^{max}$, $C_{out}^{max}$. Without all three, no $L_{min}$. - **1 ppm = $10^{-6}$ kg/kg = 1 mg/kg.** Use mass fractions or consistent mass-basis ppm only. - **Composite curve construction:** Sort WUOs by **increasing** $C_{out}^{max}$, then by **increasing** $C_{in}^{max}$ for ties. - **C-M diagram shape:** Horizontal segments = WUOs (mass pickup), vertical segments = mixing/reuse transitions. - **Pinch = the "kink"** where supply line touches the composite curve at the minimum slope. - **Total freshwater no-reuse baseline** = $\sum L_{min,i}$. Always compute this as the upper bound. - **Theoretical minimum freshwater** (perfect reuse) = $\Delta m_{total} / C_{out,final}^{max}$ (often lower bound). - **Wang–Smith (1994) assumption:** Single contaminant; multi-contaminant problems require hierarchical or iterative targeting. - **Water ≠ only freshwater:** Reuse, regeneration, recycle are three distinct strategies — *reuse* means sequential use across operations; *recycle* means return to the same operation; *regeneration* means treatment between uses. - **Real-world constraint:** Corrosion, fouling, biological growth impose *practical* $C_{out}^{max}$ tighter than theoretical. - **KTU Module 3 link to Module 4:** The water network design step (next module) uses these $L_{min}$ values as the basis for Minimum Flowrate Targeting via Water Cascade Analysis.

topics.title: Targeting Maximum Water Reuse for Single Contaminants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Targeting Maximum Water Reuse for Single Contaminants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Targeting Maximum Water Reuse** is a systematic, graphical-numerical procedure in Process Integration that determines the **minimum freshwater flowrate (F<sub>fw,min</sub>)** required by a process plant when water from one operation (a *water source*) is reused directly in another operation (a *water sink*) without any treatment or regeneration. For **single-contaminant systems**, the targeting is based on the contaminant concentration limits (maximum inlet and maximum outlet) of each process operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> "Water Reuse Targeting is the pre-design determination of the *theoretical minimum* freshwater demand of a process by matching water-generating streams (sources) with water-consuming streams (sinks), subject to the contaminant concentration constraints of each process operation. The point at which reuse cannot proceed further is the **Water Pinch**."

## 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | KTU Symbol | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Freshwater | F<sub>fw</sub> | Clean water entering the process (typically C = 0 ppm) |
| Water Source | – | Process stream leaving an operation (high in contaminant) |
| Water Sink | – | Process operation demanding water (low in contaminant) |
| Maximum Inlet Conc. | C<sup>in</sup><sub>j,max</sub> | Highest contaminant level a sink can accept |
| Maximum Outlet Conc. | C<sup>out</sup><sub>j,max</sub> | Highest contaminant level a source can reach |
| Limiting Water Profile | LWP | Composite of all water demands & supplies on a load–concentration plot |
| Pinch Point | C* | Concentration where water demand = water supply |
| Minimum Freshwater | F<sub>fw,min</sub> | Theoretical lower bound of freshwater consumption |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Water Cooler" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine an office building with **5 departments** (sinks) that each need water for cleaning. The **washrooms and pantries** (sources) generate dirty water. Normally, all dirty water is drained and fresh water is bought from outside. **Maximum water reuse** is like a *water-sharing economy*:
> - The Pantry's rinsing water (low contamination) can be sent to the Cleaning Department (which tolerates some dirt).
> - But the Toilet flush water (heavily contaminated) **cannot** go to the Pantry — that would be like drinking from a toilet!
> - The **Pinch Point** is the dirt level above which *no department will accept* any more water from any other department.
> - **Targeting** is the calculation: *"What is the absolute minimum fresh water we still must buy, even after all possible sharing?"*

This is exactly what we calculate — the **freshwater demand floor** that cannot be eliminated by reuse alone.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Freshwater Concentration:** C<sub>fw</sub> = **0 ppm** (assumed pure unless otherwise stated)
- **Contaminant Load Units:** kg contaminant per hour (or mass/time)
- **Concentration Units:** ppm (parts per million) or mg/L for dilute aqueous systems
- **Stream Flowrate Units:** typically **kg/s** or **tonne/h**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For single-contaminant problems, you only need to track **one** contaminant. The method generalises to multi-contaminant systems using *Composite Curves* and the *Water Cascade Table*, but single-contaminant targeting is the **foundation** and is heavily tested in ESE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle — The Mass Balance Foundation

The entire targeting procedure rests on the **contaminant mass balance** for each process operation `j`:

$$\Delta m_j = F_j \left( C_j^{out} - C_j^{in} \right)$$

where:
- Δm<sub>j</sub> = mass of contaminant picked up by stream j (kg/h)
- F<sub>j</sub> = water flowrate through operation j (kg/h or tonne/h)
- C<sub>j</sub><sup>in</sup>, C<sub>j</sub><sup>out</sup> = inlet and outlet concentrations (ppm)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind It:** Every operation has a *fixed contaminant load* it must remove (Δm<sub>j</sub>). If we can supply it with water that already has some contaminant in it, we use **less fresh water** for the same removal job.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Single-Contaminant Targeting

### Pillar 1 — Identify Sinks and Sources

- A **sink** is defined by its flowrate demand and **C<sup>in</sup><sub>j,max</sub>** (it cannot accept water dirtier than this).
- A **source** is defined by its generated flowrate and **C<sup>out</sup><sub>j,max</sub>** (it cannot be discharged dirtier than this).
- If inlet concentration is not given, **C<sup>in</sup> = 0** is assumed.

### Pillar 2 — Build the Limiting Water Profile (LWP)

Sort all sink intervals and source intervals in **descending order of concentration**. Plot cumulative contaminant load (y-axis) vs. concentration (x-axis).

- **Demand Curve (Sink Side):** Composed of horizontal steps whose heights = F<sub>j</sub> and width = C<sup>out</sup><sub>j,max</sub> – C<sup>in</sup><sub>j,max</sub>. This curve **must lie on or to the right** of the demand.
- **Supply Curve (Source Side):** Composed of horizontal steps whose heights = F<sub>j</sub>. The source supply curve **must lie on or to the left** of the LWP.

### Pillar 3 — Locate the Pinch and Compute F<sub>fw,min</sub>

The pinch concentration **C*** is the concentration where the **cumulative supply equals the cumulative demand**. Below C*, the demand cannot be satisfied by available sources.

$$\boxed{F_{fw,min} = \frac{\Delta m_{total}}{C_{pinch}}}$$

where Δm<sub>total</sub> is the total contaminant load that must be removed from the process.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta m_j = F_j (C_j^{out} - C_j^{in})$ | Contaminant load picked up in operation j | kg/h |
| 2 | $F_{j,min} = \dfrac{\Delta m_j}{C_{j,max}^{out} - C_{fw}}$ | Minimum water flowrate for operation j (using freshwater) | kg/h |
| 3 | $F_{fw,min} = \dfrac{\sum_j \Delta m_j}{C_{pinch}}$ | Minimum total freshwater | kg/h |
| 4 | $\sum F_{sources} \geq \sum F_{sinks} - F_{fw,min}$ | Mass balance for reuse feasibility | kg/h |
| 5 | $C_{pinch} = C_{out,max}$ of the source that **first** meets demand | Pinch concentration identification | ppm |
| 6 | $W_{waste,min} = F_{fw,min}$ (for reuse only) | Minimum wastewater = min freshwater in pure reuse | kg/h |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Pipes and Units:** The vertical bar in $\vert C_{out} - C_{in} \vert$ (if needed) is replaced in exam writing by clear parenthesis. **Always carry units** in the final answer line — KTU examiners award the method mark for dimensional consistency.

## 2.4 The Water Cascade Table (Algebraic / Numerical Targeting)

When the LWP graph is inconvenient, the **Water Cascade Table (WCT)** is the KTU-recommended numerical method.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. List all **C<sup>in</sup><sub>j,max</sub>** and **C<sup>out</sup><sub>j,max</sub>** for all operations.
2. Create a sorted list of *distinct* concentrations in descending order: C<sub>k</sub> > C<sub>k+1</sub> > ... > 0.
3. For each interval, compute **Net Flow (ΔF<sub>k</sub>)** = ΣSinks – ΣSources in that interval.
4. Compute **Cumulative Net Flow** from top (C = ∞) downward, starting at 0.
5. Identify the **most negative** cumulative value; the corresponding concentration is the **C***.
6. The magnitude of that negative value **after** adding F<sub>fw,min</sub> = 0 must be offset — set F<sub>fw,min</sub> = |min cumulative value|.

$$\boxed{F_{fw,min} = -\min\left(\sum_{k=1}^{N} \Delta F_k^{cumulative}\right)}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries:** Reduce water intake from rivers (environmental compliance).
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Solvent-water recycling between batch operations.
- **Pulp & Paper:** Black-liquor and white-water cascade reuse.
- **Semiconductor:** Ultrapure water (UPW) cascade through rinse baths.
