## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Decision Flow for Column Placement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Distillation Column Data Q_R, Q_C, T_R, T_C] --> B{Compare T_R with T_pinch}
    B -- T_R greater than T_pinch --> C{Compare T_C with T_pinch}
    B -- T_R less than T_pinch --> D{Compare T_C with T_pinch}
    C -- T_C greater than T_pinch --> E[Column ABOVE Pinch]
    C -- T_C less than T_pinch --> F[Column CROSSES Pinch]
    D -- T_C less than T_pinch --> G[Column BELOW Pinch]
    D -- T_C greater than T_pinch --> F
    E --> H{Q_C greater or equal Q_R ?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Acceptable Heat-Engine Mode]
    H -- No --> J[External Hot Utility Required Above Pinch]
    G --> K{Q_C less or equal Q_R ?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Acceptable Heat-Pump Mode]
    K -- No --> M[External Cold Utility Required Below Pinch]
    F --> N[Reconfigure Pressure or Accept Penalty]
    N --> O[Penalty DeltaQh,min DeltaQc,min]
    O --> P[End: Calculate Annualised Cost]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — GCC Pocket Identification (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AbovePinch[Above Pinch Region]
        R1[Reboiler Heat Input]
        C1[Condenser Heat Rejection]
        R1 -- Provides Heat --> Net1[Net Heat Source Pocket]
        C1 -- Consumes Heat --> Net1
    end
    subgraph BelowPinch[Below Pinch Region]
        R2[Reboiler Heat Input]
        C2[Condenser Heat Rejection]
        R2 -- Adds Heat --> Net2[Net Heat Sink Pocket]
        C2 -- Removes Heat --> Net2
    end
    PinchNode((Pinch Point T_pinch)) -.separates.-> AbovePinch
    PinchNode -.separates.-> BelowPinch
    AbovePinch -- No Cross-Pinch Transfer Allowed --> BelowPinch
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Reboiler/Condenser Integration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Process[Process Background]
        HS[Hot Streams]
        CS[Cold Streams]
    end
    subgraph Column[Distillation Column]
        Feed[Feed Mixture] --> Reboil[Reboiler Q_R]
        Reboil --> Tray[Trays / Packing]
        Tray --> Cond[Condenser Q_C]
        Cond --> Dist[Distillate]
        Tray --> Bott[Bottom Product]
    end
    subgraph UtilityLayer[Utility and Integration Layer]
        HU[Hot Utility Steam]
        CU[Cold Utility CW / Refrig]
        HEN[Heat Exchanger Network]
    end
    Reboil -.supplied by.-> HEN
    HEN -.if deficient.-> HU
    Cond -.supplied by.-> HEN
    HEN -.if deficient.-> CU
    HS -.integrated with.-> HEN
    CS -.integrated with.-> HEN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for the Exam:**
> - **Above**: Identify which GCC pocket the column fits into.
> - **Below**: Show how reboiler/condenser hook into the HEN.
> - **Right-Hand Side**: Understand that the penalty arises when arrows must cross the *Pinch Node*.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1 | 3 Marks]**
> **Q.** Define the term *Pinch* as used in process integration. Why is crossing the pinch with a distillation column undesirable?
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The **Pinch** is the temperature level in a process at which the net enthalpy flow across all streams is zero, representing the most constrained point of the heat-recovery network. It divides the process into a *heat-source region* (above the pinch) and a *heat-sink region* (below the pinch).
> Crossing the pinch with a distillation column is undesirable because the column's reboiler and condenser then transfer heat across the pinch, forcing an **equal increase in both hot and cold utility consumption**. This violates the principle of minimum external utility and inflates the **Total Annualised Cost (TAC)**.
> *(3 marks — 1 mark for pinch definition, 1 mark for source/sink concept, 1 mark for penalty implication.)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2 | 3 Marks]**
> **Q.** Distinguish between a distillation column acting as a *heat engine* and as a *heat pump* in the context of pinch placement.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> - **Heat-Engine Mode**: Column is placed **above the pinch**. It receives hot utility at the reboiler and rejects useful heat at the condenser that can be recovered by cold streams of the above-pinch region. Net heat source behaviour.
> - **Heat-Pump Mode**: Column is placed **below the pinch**. It receives cold utility at the condenser and rejects useful heat at the reboiler that can be absorbed by hot streams of the below-pinch region. Net heat sink behaviour.
> *(3 marks — 1.5 marks for each mode with correct placement and duty direction.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]**
>
> **Question A:**
>
> **(a)** A crude distillation column in a refinery has a reboiler duty $Q_R = 12$ MW at $T_R = 145$ °C and a condenser duty $Q_C = 14$ MW at $T_C = 100$ °C. The process pinch has been identified at $T_{pinch} = 120$ °C.
>   - **(i)** Identify whether the column should be placed above or below the pinch.
>   - **(ii)** If the column crosses the pinch, calculate the resulting penalty in minimum hot and cold utilities. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the role of the **Grand Composite Curve (GCC)** in deciding the placement of distillation columns. Illustrate with a sketch description. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Question A (a):**

- **(i)** Comparing temperatures:
  $T_R = 145$ °C $> T_{pinch} = 120$ °C
  $T_C = 100$ °C $< T_{pinch} = 120$ °C$
  Hence the column **crosses the pinch**. **Bad placement.**

- **(ii)** Applying the simplified penalty relation assuming even duty distribution:

$$
Q_R^{above} \approx \frac{145 - 120}{145 - 100} \times 12 = \frac{25}{45} \times 12 = 6.67 \text{ MW}
$$

$$
Q_R^{below} = 12 - 6.67 = 5.33 \text{ MW}
$$

$$
Q_C^{below} \approx 12 - 4.67 = ... \text{(students should show similar calculation)}
$$

For board-style simplicity, use the **uniform distribution approximation** $\alpha = 0.5$:

$$
Q_R^{above} = 6 \text{ MW}, \quad Q_C^{above} = 7 \text{ MW}
$$

$$
\Delta Q_{h,\min} = Q_C^{above} - Q_R^{above} = 7 - 6 = 1 \text{ MW}
$$

$$
\Delta Q_{c,\min} = 1 \text{ MW}
$$

**Recommended Action:** Adjust operating pressure of the column to shift both $T_R$ and $T_C$ either above 120 °C (preferred if GCC pocket exists) or below 120 °C.

*(Full marks split: 2 for identification, 3 for penalty calculation, 2 for recommendation.)*

**Model Solution for Question A (b):**

The **Grand Composite Curve (GCC)** is a shifted-temperature vs net-enthalpy plot that combines the hot and cold composite curves into a single profile. Its relevance to distillation column placement:

- The GCC shows **pockets** (open regions between the curve and the enthalpy axis) where process heat can be supplied or rejected without external utilities.
- A column should be **fitted into a GCC pocket** on either side of the pinch. The side with the larger pocket is preferred for economic reasons.
- If the column duties exceed the available pocket, supplementary external utility is unavoidable.
- The GCC also helps **decide between the above-pinch and below-pinch placement** when both are physically feasible.

*(Marks: 2 for GCC definition, 3 for pocket interpretation, 2 for placement rule using GCC.)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice | 14 Marks]**
>
> **Question B:**
>
> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **three possible placements** of a distillation column relative to the pinch. State the pinch-placement rule in each case. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A process has $Q_{h,\min} = 25$ MW and $Q_{c,\min} = 18$ MW at a pinch temperature of 100 °C. A proposed distillation column has $Q_R = Q_C = 6$ MW with $T_R = 130$ °C and $T_C = 75$ °C. Determine the new utility targets if the column is placed as proposed. Suggest an alternative placement strategy. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Question B (a):**

1. **Column Above Pinch**: Reboiler and condenser both above pinch temperature. Functions as heat engine. Recommended if $Q_C \geq Q_R$.
2. **Column Below Pinch**: Both below pinch temperature. Functions as heat pump. Recommended if $Q_C \leq Q_R$.
3. **Column Across Pinch**: Reboiler on one side, condenser on the other. Forces cross-pinch heat transfer → utility penalty $\Delta Q_{h,\min} = \Delta Q_{c,\min}$.

(Use the Mermaid-style diagrams shown in Section 4 as the basis for the sketches.)

**Model Solution for Question B (b):**

The column crosses the pinch (130 > 100 > 75).

Assuming $\alpha = \beta = 0.5$:

$$
Q_R^{above} = 3 \text{ MW}, \quad Q_C^{above} = 3 \text{ MW}
$$

$$
\Delta Q_{h,\min} = \Delta Q_{c,\min} = \vert 3 - 3 \vert = 0 \text{ MW} \quad \text{(symmetric case)}
$$

So in this idealised case the penalty is **zero**, but in reality the *non-uniform temperature distribution* inside the column will create a small positive penalty. KTU examiners typically expect students to write:

> "Penalty is zero in the ideal symmetric case but non-zero in realistic asymmetric columns."

**Alternative Placement Strategy:**
- **Option 1:** Operate the column at a lower pressure so that $T_C \geq 100$ °C → place entirely above pinch.
- **Option 2:** Operate at higher pressure so that $T_R \leq 100$ °C → place entirely below pinch.
- **Option 3:** Re-pocket the GCC by changing the integration scheme.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU Board Style):**
> 1. **Confusing $\Delta Q_h$ and $\Delta Q_c$**: Many students interchange hot and cold utilities. Remember — *extra heat crossing from hot side to cold side increases BOTH* hot and cold utility demands equally.
> 2. **Forgetting the "pocket" check**: Just placing a column above the pinch is NOT enough; you must verify that the GCC pocket can accommodate the reboiler–condenser pair.
> 3. **Not stating the assumption of duty distribution**: Always write "Assuming uniform duty distribution, $\alpha = 0.5$" or similar in the answer. Otherwise, examiners deduct marks for vagueness.
> 4. **Ignoring pressure as a design lever**: A common 2-mark question asks "How can you relocate the column?" — answer must mention **operating pressure adjustment**.
> 5. **Mis-stating the heat-engine/heat-pump terminology**: KTU board values precise usage. "Heat engine above pinch" must be tied to $T_R > T_{pinch} > T_C$ contextually.
> 6. **Skipping the graphical sketch**: A 14-mark question that asks for "neat sketch" without a Mermaid-style or hand-drawn block diagram loses 2 marks easily.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Pinch Definition**: Zero net heat-flow temperature level in a process. Splits process into *source* (above) and *sink* (below) regions.
- **Column = Reboiler + Condenser pair**: Treat as integrated sub-system in pinch analysis.
- **Heat-Engine Mode**: Above pinch; $Q_C \geq Q_R$.
- **Heat-Pump Mode**: Below pinch; $Q_C \leq Q_R$.
- **Crossing the Pinch**: Both utilities increase by the excess cross-pinch duty; penalty equation $\Delta Q_{h,\min} = \Delta Q_{c,\min} = \max(\text{excess}, 0)$.
- **GCC Pocket Check**: Always verify that the GCC can absorb the column duties before accepting any placement.
- **Pressure as Design Variable**: Re-locate the column by adjusting operating pressure (raise pressure → raise temperatures → shift column upward on T-axis).
- **Minimum Utility Increase**: Crossing pinch doubles the penalty in terms of total utility cost.
- **Standard ΔTmin Values**: 10 °C (process–process), 20 °C (process–utility) — quote these when relevant.
- **Vapour Boil-up**: $V = (R+1)D$; affects reboiler duty sizing.
- **Exam Strategy**: Always begin 14-mark answers with a *clear statement of the pinch rule*, then proceed to numerical demonstration.
- **Final Check**: For any column placement, compute the new $Q_{h,\min}^{new}$ and $Q_{c,\min}^{new}$, then compare with original targets to confirm the penalty.
